- Bagasse composition: 50% cellulose, 25% hemicellulose, balance = lignin + ash (not fermentable).
- Cellulose hydrolysis: 1 mol cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅, $M = 162\,g/mol$, monomer unit) → 1 mol glucose (180 g/mol). For simplicity, treat yield as 1 g glucose per 1 g cellulose (since 180/162 = 1.111, we apply this factor).
- Hemicellulose (xylan, $M_{monomer} = 132\,g/mol$) → xylose → ethanol. Use the same theoretical yield factor (0.511 g ethanol/g sugar) for both C6 and C5 sugars, as the Gay-Lussac stoichiometry applies to any hexose; for xylose (C5H₁₀O₅), the ethanol yield is slightly higher per gram of sugar (≈0.60 g/g) due to different stoichiometry, but the KTU convention treats both as 0.511 g/g for simplicity.

**Step 1 — Glucose from cellulose.**

$$m_{glucose} = 1000\,kg \times 0.50 \times \frac{180}{162} = 500 \times 1.1111 = 555.6\,kg$$

**Step 2 — Xylose from hemicellulose.**

$$m_{xylose} = 1000\,kg \times 0.25 \times \frac{150}{132} = 250 \times 1.1364 = 284.1\,kg$$

(Note: xylose molecular weight = 150 g/mol; xylan monomer = 132 g/mol)

**Step 3 — Total fermentable sugars (C6 + C5).**

$$m_{sugar}^{total} = 555.6 + 284.1 = 839.7\,kg$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical ethanol mass.**

$$m_{ethanol} = 839.7 \times 0.511 = 429.1\,kg$$

**Step 5 — Convert to litres at ethanol density $\rho = 0.789\,kg/L$.**

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{429.1}{0.789} = 543.9\,L \approx 544\,L$$

**Answer:** Approximately **544 L of anhydrous bioethanol** can theoretically be obtained from 1 tonne of dry bagasse (≈ 50% conversion efficiency in practice gives ~270 L).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors**: Converting mass to litres using 0.789 kg/L for ethanol (or 0.789 g/mL). Many students incorrectly use 1 kg/L.
> 2. **Stoichiometric oversight**: Forgetting the **180/162 hydrolysis correction factor** for cellulose, or applying only a 1:1 mass ratio.
> 3. **Lignin inclusion**: Counting lignin (which is non-fermentable) as part of fermentable substrate.
> 4. **Azeotrope confusion**: Claiming distillation alone gives 100% ethanol — it does not; the **95.6% azeotrope** requires molecular-sieve or extractive dehydration.
> 5. **Net ATP confusion**: Writing "ethanol fermentation produces 38 ATP" (this is the aerobic value; fermentation yields only **2 ATP/glucose**).
> 6. **Efficiency definition**: Forgetting that fermentation efficiency is a **ratio with 0.511 in the denominator**, not a generic percentage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (high priority):** Ethanol fermentation = anaerobic C6 sugar → 2 ethanol + 2 CO₂ by yeast.
- **Gay-Lussac equation:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$
- **Theoretical yield:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (51.1% w/w) — derived from $2 \times 46 / 180$.
- **Theoretical CO₂ yield:** **0.489 g CO₂ / g glucose** — derived from $2 \times 44 / 180$.
- **Net ATP:** **2 ATP per glucose** (anaerobic) vs. 30–32 ATP (aerobic) — a 6.25% energy recovery.
- **Two key enzymes:** PDC (TPP, Mg²⁺) and ADH (Zn²⁺); ADH regenerates NAD⁺ from NADH.
- **NAD⁺/NADH cycling** is the **central redox logic** of fermentation — without it, glycolysis halts at GAPDH.
- **PFK-1** is the master rate-controlling enzyme of the upstream pathway.
- **Optimum conditions:** *S. cerevisiae* — 30 °C, pH 4.0–5.0, anaerobic.
- **Fermentation efficiency:** $\eta = Y_{P/S}^{obs} / 0.511$; industrial range 80–95%.
- **Ethanol tolerance of yeast:** ~12–15% v/v; above this the product inhibits growth.
- **Industrial feedstocks:** Sugarcane, molasses, corn starch (1st gen); lignocellulose (2nd gen).
- **Process sequence:** Pretreatment → Enzymatic hydrolysis → Fermentation → Distillation → Molecular-sieve dehydration → Anhydrous bioethanol.
- **Biorefinery closure:** CO₂ captured for industrial use; stillage valorized as DDGS / biogas — embodies environmental-biotechnology principles.
- **Heat release:** ~92 kJ/mol glucose drives bioreactor cooling-duty calculations.
- **Lignocellulose composition:** Cellulose (C6 polymer) + Hemicellulose (C5/C6 polymer) + Lignin (non-fermentable, phenolic).
- **Hydration correction factor** for cellulose → glucose: **180/162 = 1.111**.
- **Ethanol density:** $\rho = 0.789\,kg/L$ at 20 °C (used in all volume conversions).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biochemical Oxygen Demand - Sources of BOD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biochemical Oxygen Demand - Sources of BOD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is defined as the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the biodegradable organic matter present in a water sample under standard conditions of temperature (typically **20°C**) and time (conventionally **5 days**, denoted as **BOD₅**).

Mathematically, the standard parameter is expressed as:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \text{DO}_{\text{initial}} - \text{DO}_{\text{final, after 5 days at 20°C}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** BOD is an empirical, bioassay-type test that indirectly measures the concentration of biodegradable organic matter in water. It is the most widely used parameter to characterize the **strength of wastewater** and the **receiving water body pollution load**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded swimming pool filled with hungry children (these represent **aerobic bacteria**). Now, the pool owners throw in a huge pile of pizza (this represents the **organic waste** in the water). The children will frantically eat the pizza, and to digest it, they need to gulp down huge amounts of air (this represents **dissolved oxygen**).

- The **more pizza** you throw into the pool → the **more air** the children gulp.
- If the children gulp all the available air and start gasping, the pool becomes **oxygen-depleted**, and the pool "dies" (this is **anoxic/aerobic collapse** of the water body).

> BOD is essentially a measure of **"how much oxygen the microorganisms will consume"** if you feed them the organic matter in the water sample for 5 days.

### 1.3 Why BOD Matters — The Pollution Indicator

| Parameter | Value (typical, mg/L) | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure river water** | < 2 | Pristine / acceptable |
| **Treated domestic sewage** | ≤ 20 | CPCB discharge limit |
| **Raw domestic sewage** | 200 – 400 | High strength |
| **Strong industrial effluent** | > 1000 | Toxic / requires pretreatment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units:** BOD is universally reported in **mg/L** (equivalent to **ppm** for dilute aqueous systems), since 1 L of water ≈ 1 kg.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The BOD Reaction Sequence

The BOD test does **not** measure all the oxygen demand at once. Instead, the deoxygenation follows a **biochemical sequence** that environmental engineers must understand:

1. **Carbonaceous BOD (CBOD)** — Stage 1 (0 to ~5–7 days at 20 °C)
   - Heterotrophic bacteria oxidize **organic carbon compounds** (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) to **CO₂ + H₂O**.
   - This is the dominant oxygen-consuming phase in the standard 5-day BOD test.

2. **Nitrogenous BOD (NBOD)** — Stage 2 (after ~5–8 days)
   - Nitrifying bacteria (*Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter*) oxidize **ammonia (NH₃)** to **nitrite (NO₂⁻)** and then to **nitrate (NO₃⁻)**.
   - This phase is normally suppressed in the standard BOD₅ test using a **nitrification inhibitor** (allyl thiourea, ATU).

3. **Endogenous / Stabilization Phase** — Stage 3
   - Microbes oxidize their **own cellular protoplasm** (endogenous respiration) once external substrate is exhausted.

### 2.2 First-Order Kinetic Model (Streeter-Phelps Foundation)

The rate of oxidation of organic matter by bacteria follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the remaining (unoxidized) organic load:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

Integrating with initial condition $L_t = L_0$ at $t = 0$:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

The **oxygen consumed up to time *t*** is the difference between the initial ultimate BOD ($L_0$) and the remaining load ($L_t$):

$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$$

where:
- $y_t$ = BOD exerted up to time *t* (mg/L)
- $L_0$ = Ultimate (first-stage) BOD (mg/L)
- $k$ = Deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.1 – 0.35 day⁻¹ at 20 °C**
- $t$ = Time in days

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard BOD₅ calculation | $\text{BOD}_5 = \text{DO}_i - \text{DO}_f$ | mg/L | DO at day 0 minus DO at day 5 |
| 2 | First-order BOD exertion | $y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$ | mg/L | The fundamental BOD model |
| 3 | BOD₅ in terms of L₀ | $\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$ | mg/L | Most-used form in KTU problems |
| 4 | Ultimate BOD from BOD₅ | $L_0 = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | mg/L | Rearranged form |
| 5 | Deoxygenation constant (typical) | $k_{20}$ | 0.23 day⁻¹ (domestic sewage) | Used when not specified |
| 6 | Temperature correction (k) | $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | day⁻¹ | $\theta \approx 1.047$ |
| 7 | Dilution factor | $f = \dfrac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{sample}} + V_{\text{dilution water}}}$ | dimensionless | Critical in BOD bottle math |
| 8 | Corrected BOD | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{D_1 - D_2 - (B_1 - B_2)(1-f)}{f}$ | mg/L | With seed correction |

> [!NOTE]
> **DOs vs DO₅ boundary conditions** used in KTU valuation:
> - $D_1$ = DO of diluted sample on day 0
> - $D_2$ = DO of diluted sample on day 5
> - $B_1, B_2$ = DO of seed blank on day 0 and day 5
> - $f$ = dilution factor (volume of wastewater ÷ total bottle volume)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sewage Treatment Plant (STP) Design:** BOD loading directly determines **aeration tank volume**, **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids)** requirements, **F/M ratio**, and **sludge age**.
- **Self-purification capacity** assessment of rivers (Streeter-Phelps DO sag curve).
- **Discharge consent / regulatory compliance** under the **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** and **CPCB norms**.
- **Industrial effluent classification** — high-BOD effluents (distillery, dairy, tanneries) must have a **CETP** (Common Effluent Treatment Plant).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — First-Order BOD Equation

**Starting premise:** The rate of biochemical oxidation of organic matter is proportional to the amount of unoxidized organic matter remaining at time *t*.

**Step 1:** Write the rate equation. The rate of disappearance of organic matter is:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} \propto -L_t$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the proportionality constant *k* (deoxygenation constant):

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables to prepare for integration:

$$\frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $L_0$ (at $t=0$) to $L_t$ (at time *t*):

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln L_t - \ln L_0 = -k \cdot t$$

**Step 6:** Apply logarithm rules to simplify:

$$\ln \left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k t$$

**Step 7:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{L_t}{L_0} = e^{-kt}$$

**Step 8:** Solve for the remaining unoxidized load:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

**Step 9:** The BOD exerted is the difference between initial ultimate BOD and remaining load:

$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 - L_0 e^{-kt}$$

**Step 10:** Factor out $L_0$ to obtain the **canonical BOD equation**:

$$\boxed{\,y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)\,}$$

> This is the **single most important equation** for KTU 2024 Environmental Biotechnology derivations.

### 3.2 Derivation II — BOD₅ Form and Ultimate BOD Recovery

Starting from the canonical form $y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$, set $t = 5$ days:

**Step 1:** Substitute $t = 5$:

$$y_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $y_5$ is the standard BOD₅:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $L_0$ by dividing both sides by $(1 - e^{-5k})$:

$$\boxed{\,L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}\,}$$

**Step 4 (numerical shortcut):** For $k = 0.23 \,\text{day}^{-1}$ (standard domestic sewage at 20 °C):

$$1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23} = 1 - e^{-1.15} = 1 - 0.3166 = 0.6834$$

So the ratio $L_0 / \text{BOD}_5 = 1/0.6834 \approx 1.46$. This is a **classic KTU value**: BOD₅ ≈ **68 % of ultimate BOD**.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Worked Numerical (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** A wastewater sample is diluted such that 5 mL is made up to 300 mL with dilution water. The initial DO of the diluted sample is 8.6 mg/L, and after 5 days at 20 °C it drops to 3.2 mg/L. The seed blank consumed 0.4 mg/L in 5 days. Calculate BOD₅ of the wastewater.

**Step 1 — Compute the dilution factor:**

$$f = \frac{V_{\text{sample}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{5}{300} = 0.01667$$

**Step 2 — Write the full equation with seed correction:**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - f)}{f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values** (note: $(B_1 - B_2) = 0.4$ mg/L, $D_1 = 8.6$, $D_2 = 3.2$):

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(8.6 - 3.2) - (0.4)(1 - 0.01667)}{0.01667}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 5.4 - (0.4)(0.98333) = 5.4 - 0.3933 = 5.0067 \,\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by the dilution factor:**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{5.0067}{0.01667} \approx 300.4 \,\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 6 — State the answer with units:**

$$\boxed{\,\text{BOD}_5 \approx 300 \,\text{mg/L}\,}$$

> This is a **moderately strong domestic sewage**, well above the CPCB discharge limit of 20 mg/L, indicating that the wastewater **must be treated** before disposal.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Stage | Duration (20 °C) | Reaction | Microbes | Oxygen consumed? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lag phase | 0 – 1 day | Microbial acclimatization | Heterotrophs | Minimal |
| Log growth | 1 – 3 days | Exponential substrate oxidation | Heterotrophs | Heavy (CBOD peak) |
| Stationary | 3 – 5 days | Substrate depletion | Heterotrophs | Tapering off |
| Nitrification | 5 – 8+ days | NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ | *Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter* | Yes (NBOD, suppressed in BOD₅) |
| Endogenous | > 8 days | Self-oxidation of biomass | All | Slow, sustained |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sources of BOD — Hierarchical Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["BOD SOURCES IN ENVIRONMENT"] --> B["POINT SOURCES"]
    A --> C["NON-POINT SOURCES"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — BOD Test Procedure

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["STEP 1: Sample collection and preservation at 4 degree C"] --> S2["STEP 2: Prepare dilution water aerated to near saturation"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Add nutrient buffer and seed if required"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Dilute sample in BOD bottle"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Measure initial DO using Winkler or membrane electrode"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Seal and incubate at 20 degree C for 5 days"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Measure final DO on day 5"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8: Compute BOD5 = DOi minus DOf divided by dilution factor"]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — BOD Sources vs. Treatment Implications

| Source Category | Typical BOD₅ Range (mg/L) | Dominant Organic Fraction | Suggested Primary Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic sewage (raw) | 200 – 400 | Proteins, carbs, lipids | Primary settling + activated sludge |
| Dairy effluent | 1 000 – 3 000 | Lactose, milk fats | Equalization + anaerobic UASB |
| Distillery spent wash | 30 000 – 60 000 | Sugars, melanoidins | Anaerobic digestion + RO |
| Pulp & paper | 200 – 800 | Lignin, cellulose | Primary + biological aerated lagoon |
| Tannery effluent | 1 500 – 4 000 | Proteins, hair, fats | Sulfide removal + activated sludge |
| Slaughterhouse | 800 – 2 500 | Blood, fats, paunch | DAF + anaerobic lagoon |
| Landfill leachate | 5 000 – 50 000 | VFAs, humic acids | MBR + NF / RO |
| Stormwater runoff | 5 – 50 | Leaves, pet waste, oil | Oil–water separator + bioswale |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiners' Hint:** When asked about **"sources of BOD"** in a 14-mark question, students should always classify into **point vs. non-point vs. natural**, give **3 – 4 examples in each category with typical BOD₅ values**, and **link the source to a suitable biological treatment process** (this is a guaranteed 2-mark bonus).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**  
> **Q: Define Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD). State the standard conditions used in the BOD test.**
>
> **Model Answer:**  
> BOD is the amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microorganisms while oxidizing the biodegradable organic matter in a water sample under controlled conditions.  
> **Standard conditions:** (i) Temperature = **20 °C**, (ii) Time = **5 days** (yielding BOD₅), (iii) Darkness, (iv) Aerobic environment, (v) Presence of nutrients and buffer; nitrification suppressed using **allyl thiourea (ATU)**. BOD is expressed in **mg/L**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**  
> **Q: Differentiate between BOD and COD. (3 marks)**
>
> **Model Answer:**  
> | Aspect | BOD | COD |
> |---|---|---|
> | Full form | Biochemical Oxygen Demand | Chemical Oxygen Demand |
> | Principle | Biological oxidation (5 days) | Chemical oxidation (2 – 3 h, K₂Cr₂O₇) |
> | Test duration | 5 days | 2 – 3 hours |
> | Oxidizes | Biodegradable organics | Biodegradable + non-biodegradable organics |
> | Typical value (sewage) | 200 – 400 mg/L | 400 – 1000 mg/L |
> | BOD/COD ratio | — | Indicates biodegradability (> 0.5 = easily treatable) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model 14-Mark Q]**  
>
> ### **Question A (Choice 1):**
>
> **(a)** Define BOD. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **5-day BOD test procedure**. Discuss the various **sources of BOD** in natural water bodies, classifying them into point and non-point sources. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Derive the **first-order BOD equation** $y_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ and explain the significance of the deoxygenation constant *k*. A wastewater has BOD₅ = 200 mg/L and *k* = 0.23 day⁻¹ at 20 °C. Calculate the **ultimate BOD** $L_0$ and the **BOD remaining after 10 days**. *(7 marks)*
>
> ### **Model Solution – (a):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** BOD is the dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic bacteria during biological oxidation of organic matter in a water sample over a standard period of 5 days at 20 °C. Unit: mg/L.
>
> **5-Day BOD Test Procedure (3 marks):**
> 1. Collect sample, preserve at 4 °C.
> 2. Prepare aerated dilution water with phosphate buffer, MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃.
> 3. Add nitrification inhibitor (ATU) to suppress NBOD.
> 4. Dilute the wastewater appropriately in a 300 mL BOD bottle.
> 5. Measure **initial DO** ($D_1$) using Winkler titration or DO meter.
> 6. Seal bottle, incubate at 20 °C in darkness for 5 days.
> 7. Measure **final DO** ($D_2$) and compute: $\text{BOD}_5 = (D_1 - D_2)/\text{dilution factor}$.
>
> **Sources of BOD (3 marks):**
> - **Point Sources** (discrete discharge points): municipal sewage outfalls, industrial effluents (dairy, distillery, tannery, paper), hospital waste.
> - **Non-Point Sources** (diffuse): agricultural runoff with manure and crop residue, livestock feedlots, urban stormwater carrying pet waste and oil, atmospheric deposition.
>
> ### **Model Solution – (b):**
>
> **Derivation (3 marks):** See the **Derivation I in Section 3** above for the full 10-step derivation of $y_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$.
>
> **Significance of *k* (1 mark):** *k* is the deoxygenation rate constant, day⁻¹. Higher *k* = faster oxidation = more biodegradable substrate; typical range 0.1 – 0.35 day⁻¹ for municipal sewage.
>
> **Step 1 — Compute ultimate BOD $L_0$** (1.5 marks):
>
> $$L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}} = \frac{200}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23}}$$
>
> $$L_0 = \frac{200}{1 - e^{-1.15}} = \frac{200}{1 - 0.3166} = \frac{200}{0.6834} \approx 292.6 \,\text{mg/L}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Compute BOD remaining after 10 days** (1.5 marks):
>
> First, oxygen consumed in 10 days:
>
> $$y_{10} = L_0 (1 - e^{-10k}) = 292.6 \left(1 - e^{-2.30}\right)$$
>
> $$y_{10} = 292.6 \left(1 - 0.1003\right) = 292.6 \times 0.8997 \approx 263.3 \,\text{mg/L}$$
>
> **BOD remaining = ultimate BOD − oxygen consumed:**
>
> $$L_{10} = L_0 - y_{10} = 292.6 - 263.3 \approx 29.3 \,\text{mg/L}$$
>
> **Final Answers:** $L_0 \approx 292.6 \,\text{mg/L}$ and BOD remaining after 10 days $\approx 29.3 \,\text{mg/L}$.

> ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative 14-Mark Question):**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **different sources of BOD** with typical values. Explain the role of **nitrification** in the BOD test and how it is **suppressed** in BOD₅ measurement. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A 10 mL wastewater sample is diluted to 250 mL. Initial DO is 8.2 mg/L, after 5 days DO is 2.4 mg/L. Seed blank drops from 8.4 to 7.9 mg/L. Calculate BOD₅ of the wastewater. If $k = 0.20 \,\text{day}^{-1}$, find the **ultimate BOD** and the **oxygen consumed in 7 days**. *(7 marks)*
>
> ### **Model Solution – (a):**
>
> **Sources with typical BOD₅ (4 marks):**
> 1. Domestic sewage: 200 – 400 mg/L
> 2. Dairy effluent: 1 000 – 3 000 mg/L
> 3. Distillery spent wash: 30 000 – 60 000 mg/L
> 4. Sugar mill effluent: 800 – 2 000 mg/L
> 5. Tannery effluent: 1 500 – 4 000 mg/L
> 6. Pulp & paper: 200 – 800 mg/L
> 7. Agricultural runoff: 10 – 50 mg/L
>
> **Nitrification and its suppression (3 marks):**
> - Nitrification is the biological oxidation of ammonia to nitrate, consuming 4.57 mg O₂ per mg NH₃-N.
> - It normally begins after **5–8 days** at 20 °C, so it would inflate the BOD₅ reading if not suppressed.
> - **Suppression method:** Add **allyl thiourea (ATU)** at 0.5 mg/L to the dilution water — ATU inhibits the enzymes (ammonia monooxygenase) of *Nitrosomonas*.

> ### **Model Solution – (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Dilution factor** (0.5 mark):
>
> $$f = \frac{10}{250} = 0.04$$
>
> **Step 2 — Seed blank depletion** (0.5 mark):
>
> $$B_1 - B_2 = 8.4 - 7.9 = 0.5 \,\text{mg/L}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Apply corrected BOD equation** (2 marks):
>
> $$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - f)}{f}$$
>
> $$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(8.2 - 2.4) - (0.5)(1 - 0.04)}{0.04}$$
>
> $$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{5.8 - 0.48}{0.04} = \frac{5.32}{0.04} = 133 \,\text{mg/L}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Compute ultimate BOD** (2 marks):
>
> $$L_0 = \frac{133}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.20}} = \frac{133}{1 - e^{-1.0}} = \frac{133}{1 - 0.3679} = \frac{133}{0.6321} \approx 210.4 \,\text{mg/L}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Oxygen consumed in 7 days** (2 marks):
>
> $$y_7 = L_0 (1 - e^{-7 \times 0.20}) = 210.4 (1 - e^{-1.4})$$
>
> $$y_7 = 210.4 (1 - 0.2466) = 210.4 \times 0.7534 \approx 158.5 \,\text{mg/L}$$
>
> **Final Answers:** $\text{BOD}_5 = 133 \,\text{mg/L}$, $L_0 \approx 210.4 \,\text{mg/L}$, BOD in 7 days $\approx 158.5 \,\text{mg/L}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dilution factor** when computing BOD from raw DO drop — common 1-mark error.
> 2. **Confusing $L_0$ with BOD₅** — $L_0$ is *ultimate* (full exertion), BOD₅ is only ~68 % of $L_0$.
> 3. **Wrong units of *k*** — must be day⁻¹, not hr⁻¹.
> 4. **Ignoring the seed correction** in the BOD equation when industrial samples are tested (lose 1 mark).
> 5. **Writing "BOD = 5" instead of "BOD₅"** — the subscript 5 is mandatory; mark deduction in 2-mark subparts.
> 6. **Not specifying the temperature** (20 °C) in the BOD definition — minimum 0.5 mark cut.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** BOD = dissolved oxygen consumed by aerobic microbes to biodegrade organics in 5 days at **20 °C**; unit **mg/L**.
- **Standard BOD₅ equation:** $\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 (1 - e^{-5k})$ where $L_0$ = ultimate BOD, $k$ = deoxygenation constant (≈ 0.23 day⁻¹).
- **Ultimate BOD recovery:** $L_0 = \text{BOD}_5 / (1 - e^{-5k})$.
- **Sources of BOD — three classes:**
  - **Point** (municipal sewage, industrial effluents — dairy, distillery, tannery, paper)
  - **Non-point** (agricultural runoff, livestock feedlots, urban stormwater)
  - **Natural** (decaying vegetation, leaf litter, soil organic matter leaching)
- **CBOD vs NBOD:** BOD₅ captures only carbonaceous BOD; nitrification suppressed using **ATU**.
- **Test procedure:** Aerated dilution water → buffer + ATU → diluted in BOD bottle → measure D₁ (Winkler or probe) → incubate 5 days at 20 °C → measure D₂ → apply correction.
- **BOD vs COD:** BOD is biological, slow, biodegradable only; COD is chemical, fast, includes non-biodegradable. BOD/COD > 0.5 = easily biodegradable.
- **Typical BOD₅ values:** pure water < 2; treated sewage ≤ 20 (CPCB); raw sewage 200 – 400; distillery 30 000 – 60 000 mg/L.
- **Key constants to remember:** $k_{20} \approx 0.23 \,\text{day}^{-1}$, $\theta \approx 1.047$, BOD₅ ≈ 0.68 $L_0$ for standard sewage.
- **Engineering link:** BOD loading → aeration tank design, F/M ratio, MLVSS, sludge age in activated sludge process.
- **Seed correction equation:** $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)(1 - f)}{f}$.
- **Dilution factor:** $f = V_{\text{sample}} / V_{\text{total}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CBOD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CBOD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carbonaceous Biochemical Oxygen Demand (CBOD)** is the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the **carbonaceous organic matter** present in a water or wastewater sample to stable end products (primarily **CO₂ and H₂O**) under specified conditions of temperature and incubation period, **excluding the oxygen demand exerted by the oxidation of nitrogenous compounds** (ammonia and organic nitrogen).

$$CBOD = \text{Oxygen demand for } (C\text{-organic matter}) \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + \text{Biomass}$$

The standard KTU reference condition for CBOD measurement is:
- **Temperature:** 20°C
- **Incubation time:** 5 days (CBOD₅) or extrapolation to ultimate (CBODₗ or CBODᵤ)
- **Inhibitor:** Nitrification inhibitor (e.g., **allyl thiourea, ATU**) added to suppress nitrifying bacterial activity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CBOD vs BOD vs NBOD — The Triad**
> - **BOD (Total Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** = CBOD + NBOD
> - **CBOD** = Carbonaceous fraction only (organic carbon → CO₂)
> - **NBOD (Nitrogenous BOD)** = Nitrogenous fraction (NH₃ → NO₂ → NO₃)
> KTU questions frequently test this distinction. Memorizing the timing (CBOD: days 1–5; NBOD: starts day 5–8) is **high-yield**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Dishwasher" Model

Imagine a restaurant kitchen with **two dishwashers working in sequence** on the same pile of dirty plates:

- **Dishwasher 1 (CBOD):** Washes away the food scraps (carbon-based organics — sugars, fats, proteins) very quickly. Plates come out sparkling within a few hours. This is the *carbonaceous demand*.
- **Dishwasher 2 (NBOD):** Only activates later (after a delay) and handles the grease trap, protein residues, and nitrogen-based films. It is slower and needs a "wake-up" phase. This is the *nitrification demand*.

If you time the cleaning only for the first 5 days (with a chemical that puts Dishwasher 2 to sleep — *nitrification inhibitor*), you measure **CBOD₅**. If you let both work, you measure **total BOD₅**, which mixes both signals and confuses interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why suppress nitrification?**
> Standard CBOD tests use an inhibitor so that only carbon oxidation is measured. This is critical for plant design — engineers must size aeration tanks based on **carbon load alone**, not on nitrogen transformation that may or may not be desired at that treatment stage.

## 1.3 The Biochemical Reaction

The simplified stoichiometry for complete carbonaceous oxidation of a generic organic substrate is:

$$C_xH_yO_z + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{aerobic microbes}} CO_2 + H_2O + \text{new cells}$$

A representative glucose reaction (ThOD — Theoretical Oxygen Demand):

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O$$

**Theoretical ratio:** 6 mol O₂ consumed per 1 mol glucose = **1.07 g O₂ per g glucose oxidized.**

In real wastewater, only **~40–60%** of organic carbon is fully oxidized; the remainder is **assimilated into new microbial biomass** (cell synthesis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric (KTU Syllabus):** CBOD is reported in **mg/L** (≡ ppm), representing the mass of O₂ consumed per liter of sample under standard conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The First-Order CBOD Reaction Kinetics

CBOD exertion follows a **pseudo-first-order decay model** (Phelps' Law, 1909). The rate of oxygen consumption is directly proportional to the **remaining unmetabolized organic matter** (L):

$$-r = \frac{dL}{dt} = k \cdot L(t)$$

Where:
- **L(t)** = remaining CBOD at time *t* (mg/L)
- **k** = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹, base *e*; sometimes *k₁* in Streeter-Phelps notation)
- **t** = time (days)

Integrating this differential equation yields the **CBOD exertion equation**:

$$L(t) = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

The **oxygen already exerted** up to time *t* (which is what the BOD bottle measures) is:

$$y(t) = L_0 - L(t) = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)$$

Where:
- **L₀** = Ultimate CBOD (CBOD∞ or CBODᵤ) — the total oxygen demand if reaction were allowed to go to completion (mg/L)
- **y(t)** = CBOD exerted by time *t* (mg/L) — **this is what you measure in the lab**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | KTU Importance |
|:--------|:------------|:------|:---------------|
| $y(t) = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$ | CBOD exerted at time *t* (1st order) | mg/L | ★★★★★ |
| $L(t) = L_0 e^{-kt}$ | Remaining CBOD at time *t* | mg/L | ★★★★★ |
| $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | Temperature correction of rate constant (θ ≈ 1.047) | day⁻¹ | ★★★★ |
| $L_0 = \frac{y_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | Ultimate CBOD from 5-day BOD (since CBOD₅ = y(5)) | mg/L | ★★★★★ |
| $k = \frac{1}{t} \ln \left( \frac{L_0}{L_0 - y_t} \right)$ | Rate constant from lab data | day⁻¹ | ★★★★ |
| $CBOD_5 = L_0 (1 - e^{-5k})$ | Standard 5-day CBOD (most common lab measurement) | mg/L | ★★★★★ |
| $CBOD_u \approx 1.42 \cdot BOD_5$ | Empirical ratio (only if k = 0.23/day) | mg/L | ★★★ |
| $C_t = D_0 - D_t$ | Oxygen deficit at time *t* | mg/L | ★★★ |
| $\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 D$ | Streeter-Phelps DO sag equation | mg/L·day | ★★★ |
| $ThOD = \frac{8(4x+y-2z)}{12x + y + 16z}$ | Theoretical O₂ demand for CₓHᵧOᵤ per gram (with z=0, reduces to 8(y/x)/12 form) | g O₂/g | ★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units of k:** Always day⁻¹. If k is given in base 10 (rare in KTU), convert using k(ln) = 2.303 × k(log).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU pitfall:** Many students confuse *L₀* (the *ultimate* demand) with *CBOD₅* (the *5-day exerted* value). They are related by the exponential, **not** by a simple ratio unless k is known.

## 2.3 The CBOD Exertion Curve (Phelps Curve)

The complete CBOD exertion has these characteristic zones:

1. **Lag phase (0–1 day):** Microbial acclimation; minimal O₂ demand.
2. **Log/exponential phase (1–3 days):** Maximum rate; active oxidation.
3. **Declining phase (3–5 days):** Substrate becoming limiting.
4. **Plateau (5–10 days):** CBOD essentially complete (~99% exerted at 20 days for k=0.23/d).
5. **Nitrification onset (~8–10 days):** NBOD begins to dominate if no inhibitor used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The CBOD curve directly informs:
> - Aeration tank sizing in activated sludge plants
> - Required detention time in stabilization ponds
> - Receiving water DO sag modeling (Streeter-Phelps)
> - Effluent discharge permit compliance

## 2.4 The 5-Day Standard — Why 5 days?

CBOD₅ is the international standard because:
- **Convenience:** 5 working days matches a work week.
- **Historically:** British Royal Commission (1908) standardized it.
- **Empirically:** ~68% of ultimate CBOD is exerted at day 5 when k = 0.23/day.

> Mathematical proof:
> $$y(5)/L_0 = 1 - e^{-5(0.23)} = 1 - e^{-1.15} = 1 - 0.317 = 0.683 \rightarrow 68.3\%$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The CBOD Exertion Equation from First-Order Kinetics

### Step 1 — State the rate law
The rate at which dissolved oxygen is consumed is proportional to the concentration of remaining biodegradable organic matter:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k \cdot L$$

The negative sign indicates that L **decreases** with time (organic substrate is being consumed).

### Step 2 — Separate variables
Rearrange so all L-terms are on one side and t-terms on the other:

$$\frac{dL}{L} = -k \cdot dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate within the limits
At time *t = 0*, remaining CBOD = L₀ (initial ultimate CBOD). At time *t*, remaining CBOD = L(t).

$$\int_{L_0}^{L(t)} \frac{dL}{L} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral
The integral of 1/L is ln(L):

$$\left[ \ln L \right]_{L_0}^{L(t)} = -k \cdot t$$

$$\ln L(t) - \ln L_0 = -k \cdot t$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{L(t)}{L_0} \right) = -k \cdot t$$

### Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides
Using the identity $e^{\ln(x)} = x$:

$$\frac{L(t)}{L_0} = e^{-k t}$$

$$L(t) = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t} \quad \text{(Remaining CBOD)}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to exerted CBOD (measured quantity)
The CBOD **exerted** up to time *t* equals the initial minus the remaining:

$$y(t) = L_0 - L(t) = L_0 - L_0 e^{-k t}$$

$$\boxed{y(t) = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k t}\right)} \quad \text{(CBOD Exerted at time t)}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU equation** for CBOD.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Computing L₀ from a Single CBOD₅ Measurement

### Given
- A lab reports CBOD₅ = 200 mg/L
- The deoxygenation constant is k = 0.23 day⁻¹ (typical municipal wastewater at 20°C)

### Step 1 — Apply the CBOD equation at t = 5 days
$$CBOD_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot 5}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the known values
$$200 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-0.23 \times 5}\right)$$

$$200 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-1.15}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential term
$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

### Step 4 — Subtract from 1
$$1 - 0.3166 = 0.6834$$

### Step 5 — Solve for L₀
$$L_0 = \frac{200}{0.6834} = 292.7 \text{ mg/L}$$

### Interpretation
The **ultimate CBOD** is 292.7 mg/L, of which 200 mg/L (≈68.3%) is exerted in the first 5 days, and the remaining 92.7 mg/L would be exerted very slowly over the next 15–20 days.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Correction of k

The deoxygenation rate constant is **temperature dependent**, roughly doubling for every 10°C rise. The empirical van't Hoff-Arrhenius relation used by KTU:

$$k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}$$

Where:
- **k_T** = rate constant at temperature T (°C)
- **k₂₀** = rate constant at 20°C (reference)
- **θ** = temperature coefficient (typically **1.047** for CBOD)
- **T** = incubation temperature (°C)

### Worked Numerical Example
Given: k₂₀ = 0.23 day⁻¹. Find k at 30°C.

$$k_{30} = 0.23 \times (1.047)^{(30 - 20)}$$

$$k_{30} = 0.23 \times (1.047)^{10}$$

$$(1.047)^{10} \approx 1.585$$

$$k_{30} = 0.23 \times 1.585 = 0.365 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

> **Engineering insight:** At 30°C, organic matter decomposes ~59% faster than at 20°C. Tropical Kerala conditions can thus produce substantially faster CBOD exertion than temperate-climate design values.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Numericals

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Municipal WW) | KTU Exam Use |
|:----------|:-------|:----------------------------|:-------------|
| Ultimate CBOD | L₀ | 200–400 mg/L | Often "unknown" to be found |
| 5-day CBOD | CBOD₅ | ~68% of L₀ | Standard measured value |
| Deoxygenation constant | k | 0.23 /day (20°C) | Given or assumed |
| Temperature coefficient | θ | 1.047 | Universal default |
| Typical domestic sewage CBOD₅ | — | 200–250 mg/L | Used in design problems |
| Sewage DO | — | 0–2 mg/L | Useful for D₀ |
| Saturation DO at 20°C | DOs | 9.17 mg/L | Used in sag equation |
| Reaeration constant | k₂ | 0.4–0.8 /day | Given in sag problems |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Linking CBOD to the Streeter-Phelps DO Sag (Brief)

The classical Streeter-Phelps (1925) oxygen sag model couples **CBOD deoxygenation** (via k₁ = k) with **reaeration** (via k₂):

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 D$$

Where **D** = oxygen deficit = (DOs − DOactual). Integration gives:

$$D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left( e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t} \right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_2 t}$$

This equation is foundational for **river water-quality modeling** in KTU Module 3. The CBOD equation you just derived is the **first term** of this larger model.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define CBOD. Why is a nitrification inhibitor added during the standard 5-day CBOD test?**

**Model Answer:**

**CBOD (Carbonaceous Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** is the quantity of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to oxidize only the **carbonaceous organic matter** (proteins, carbohydrates, fats) in a water sample to CO₂ and H₂O at a standard temperature of 20°C, **excluding the oxygen demand from nitrogenous compounds**.

**Role of nitrification inhibitor:**
A nitrification inhibitor (typically **allyl thiourea, ATU**) is added to:
1. **Suppress the activity of nitrifying bacteria** (*Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter*).
2. Prevent the oxygen demand exerted during ammonia → nitrite → nitrate conversion (NBOD).
3. Ensure the measured BOD value reflects **only the carbonaceous fraction**, allowing accurate determination of organic load for treatment plant design.

Without the inhibitor, the standard 5-day BOD would include a partial NBOD component (nitrification typically begins around day 5–8), making the result ambiguous and time-dependent.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between CBOD and NBOD with respect to (i) timing of exertion, (ii) type of substrate, and (iii) microbial organisms involved.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CBOD | NBOD |
|:----------|:-----|:-----|
| **Substrate** | Carbonaceous organics (carbohydrates, fats, proteins) | Nitrogenous compounds (ammonia, organic N) |
| **Reaction** | Organic C → CO₂ + H₂O | NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ |
| **Timing** | Begins immediately; 68% exerted by day 5 | Begins ~day 5–8 (after lag); exerted by day 20 |
| **Microbes** | Heterotrophic bacteria (e.g., *Bacillus*, *Pseudomonas*) | Autotrophic nitrifying bacteria (*Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter*) |
| **Magnitude** | 200–400 mg/L (typical domestic sewage) | 40–60 mg/L (typical domestic sewage) |
| **Inhibitor** | ATU suppresses NBOD; not the other way | Inhibited by ATU, thiourea, chloroform |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A wastewater sample has an ultimate CBOD of 350 mg/L and a deoxygenation rate constant k = 0.20/day at 20°C. Determine the CBOD exerted after **(i) 3 days** and **(ii) 8 days**. Comment on the significance of these values for biological treatment design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same wastewater is discharged into a river at 30°C, calculate the new rate constant using θ = 1.047. Recompute the CBOD exerted at 5 days and explain the practical implications for treatment plant design in tropical climates.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Given:** L₀ = 350 mg/L, k₂₀ = 0.20 /day, t₁ = 3 d, t₂ = 8 d

**Part (i): CBOD at t = 3 days**

$$y(3) = L_0 (1 - e^{-kt}) = 350 \times (1 - e^{-0.20 \times 3})$$

$$y(3) = 350 \times (1 - e^{-0.60}) = 350 \times (1 - 0.5488)$$

$$y(3) = 350 \times 0.4512 = \mathbf{157.9 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Part (ii): CBOD at t = 8 days**

$$y(8) = 350 \times (1 - e^{-0.20 \times 8}) = 350 \times (1 - e^{-1.60})$$

$$y(8) = 350 \times (1 - 0.2019) = 350 \times 0.7981 = \mathbf{279.3 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Design Significance:**
- At 3 days: only ~45% of carbonaceous pollution has been oxidized → insufficient detention time for stabilization ponds.
- At 8 days: ~80% exerted → safer for discharge; detention time of 8–10 days is typical for stabilization pond design.
- Confirms the empirical rule that CBOD is ~68% complete in 5 days, so aeration basins should be sized for at least 5–8 day equivalent CBOD removal.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1 — Apply temperature correction:**

$$k_{30} = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)} = 0.20 \times (1.047)^{(30-20)}$$

$$k_{30} = 0.20 \times (1.047)^{10}$$

Computing (1.047)¹⁰:
- (1.047)² = 1.0962
- (1.047)⁴ = (1.0962)² = 1.2016
- (1.047)⁸ = (1.2016)² = 1.4439
- (1.047)¹⁰ = (1.047)⁸ × (1.047)² = 1.4439 × 1.0962 = **1.5828**

$$k_{30} = 0.20 \times 1.5828 = \mathbf{0.3166 \text{ /day}}$$

**Step 2 — Recompute CBOD₅ at 30°C:**

$$CBOD_5^{30} = 350 \times (1 - e^{-0.3166 \times 5})$$

$$= 350 \times (1 - e^{-1.583})$$

$$= 350 \times (1 - 0.2054)$$

$$= 350 \times 0.7946 = \mathbf{278.1 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Practical Implications for Tropical Kerala:**
- At 30°C, **79.5%** of CBOD is exerted in 5 days, vs **63%** at 20°C.
- Tropical activated-sludge systems can operate with **shorter hydraulic retention times (HRT)**, reducing tank volume by ~20%.
- However, **higher oxygen demand rate** requires greater aeration capacity (O₂ transfer efficiency must be higher).
- Nitrification may also be **faster**, requiring careful design to ensure CBOD is measured independently of NBOD.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the first-order CBOD exertion equation $y(t) = L_0 (1 - e^{-kt})$ starting from the fundamental rate law. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 5 mL wastewater sample is diluted to 300 mL in a BOD bottle. The initial DO is 8.5 mg/L and the final DO after 5 days is 3.0 mg/L. If k = 0.23/day, calculate the ultimate CBOD of the **original undiluted sample**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Assumptions:**
1. The substrate (organic matter) concentration is much greater than microbial concentration, so microbial growth is substrate-limited.
2. Microbial population remains approximately constant (steady-state, no significant growth/death during the test window).
3. All carbonaceous matter is biodegradable.
4. No limiting nutrients (N, P) deficiency.
5. Reaction is irreversible and proceeds in a closed system.
6. Temperature is constant at 20°C.
7. No nitrification (inhibitor added).
8. Oxygen is not rate-limiting (BOD bottle remains aerobic).

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Rate of disappearance of organic substrate is proportional to its remaining concentration:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k L$$

**Step 2:** Separating variables and integrating from t = 0 to t, L₀ to L(t):

$$\int_{L_0}^{L(t)} \frac{dL}{L} = -k \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 3:** Performing integration:

$$\ln L(t) - \ln L_0 = -kt$$

**Step 4:** Simplifying:

$$\ln \left( \frac{L(t)}{L_0} \right) = -kt$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiating:

$$L(t) = L_0 e^{-kt} \quad \text{(remaining CBOD)}$$

**Step 6:** CBOD exerted = initial − remaining:

$$y(t) = L_0 - L(t) = L_0 (1 - e^{-kt})$$

Hence derived. ∎

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Given:**
- Sample volume = 5 mL; Diluted to 300 mL
- Initial DO = 8.5 mg/L
- Final DO (after 5 d) = 3.0 mg/L
- k = 0.23 /day

**Step 1 — Compute the CBOD₅ of the diluted sample:**

$$y_5^{diluted} = D_0 - D_5 = 8.5 - 3.0 = 5.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dilution factor:**

$$DF = \frac{\text{Total Bottle Volume}}{\text{Sample Volume}} = \frac{300}{5} = 60$$

**Step 3 — Compute the CBOD₅ of the original (undiluted) sample:**

$$CBOD_5^{original} = y_5^{diluted} \times DF = 5.5 \times 60 = \mathbf{330 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ultimate CBOD L₀ from the first-order equation:**

$$L_0 = \frac{CBOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}} = \frac{330}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23}} = \frac{330}{1 - e^{-1.15}}$$

$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

$$L_0 = \frac{330}{1 - 0.3166} = \frac{330}{0.6834} = \mathbf{482.9 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Final Answer:** The ultimate CBOD of the undiluted wastewater is approximately **483 mg/L**, indicating a strong (high-strength) industrial or concentrated municipal wastewater.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dilution factor:** Many students report 5.5 mg/L or 330 mg/L incorrectly as the "final answer." Always check whether the question asks for the *diluted* or *original* sample value.
> 2. **Confusing L₀ with CBOD₅:** CBOD₅ ≠ L₀. The relation is exponential, not linear.
> 3. **Using wrong θ value:** For CBOD use θ ≈ 1.047; for NBOD use θ ≈ 1.10. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Sign errors in the rate law:** dL/dt = −kL (negative). Forgetting the negative sign will produce a growing exponential — wrong direction.
> 5. **Unit mismatch:** k is always in day⁻¹; t must be in days. If t is in hours, convert first.
> 6. **Not mentioning ATU:** Any "explain why inhibitor is added" question that omits ATU (allyl thiourea) is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — CBOD Module 3**

- **CBOD** = oxygen demand for **carbonaceous** organics only (proteins, carbs, fats); **excludes nitrogenous demand**.
- **Total BOD = CBOD + NBOD**; this relationship is a KTU favourite.
- **CBOD₅** (5-day, 20°C) is the **standard lab measurement**; CBODᵤ or L₀ is the **ultimate value**.
- The fundamental equation is: $y(t) = L_0 (1 - e^{-kt})$ — **memorize this**.
- **k (deoxygenation constant)** ≈ 0.20–0.25 /day at 20°C for municipal wastewater.
- Temperature correction: $k_T = k_{20} \cdot (1.047)^{(T-20)}$.
- L₀ extraction from CBOD₅: $L_0 = \dfrac{CBOD_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$.
- **Nitrification inhibitor** (ATU) added in CBOD test to suppress *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter*.
- CBOD exertion timing: **Lag (0–1 d) → Log (1–3 d) → Declining (3–5 d) → Plateau (5–7 d)**.
- NBOD begins **after day 5**; becomes significant by day 8–10.
- **Dilution factor** = bottle volume / sample volume — multiply measured BOD by this factor.
- **68% rule:** With k = 0.23/d, CBOD₅ ≈ 68% of L₀.
- **Theoretical O₂ for glucose:** 1.07 g O₂ per g glucose (1:6 molar ratio).
- **Streeter-Phelps DO Sag** uses the CBOD equation as its first deoxygenation term: $k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$.
- **Design implication:** Aeration tank HRT must be at least 5–8 day equivalents for full CBOD stabilization.
- **Tropical effect:** Higher T → higher k → faster CBOD exertion → smaller reactor volume but higher O₂ demand rate.
- **Biomass synthesis:** Only 40–60% of organic carbon is fully oxidized; rest becomes new cells (sludge yield).
- **Units check:** CBOD = mg/L ≡ ppm; k = day⁻¹; t = days.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NBOD (BOD Curve)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** NBOD (BOD Curve)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) Framework

The **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is defined as the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO), expressed in mg/L or ppm, required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the organic matter in a water sample over a specified incubation period at a standard temperature (**20 °C**) in the dark.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard 5-Day BOD, BOD₅)**
> BOD₅ is the oxygen consumed over **5 days** at **20 °C** in a sealed, dark bottle where initial DO is near saturation (~9 mg/L) and a nitrification inhibitor (allyl thiourea) is present. This represents the **carbonaceous BOD (CBOD)** only.

## 1.2 The Three Phases of Oxygen Demand in a BOD Bottle

A complete BOD exertion profile consists of **three distinct biological phases**:

| Phase | Duration (typical) | Biological Process | Oxygen Demand Name |
|:------|:-------------------|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **Phase 1** | 0 – 2 days | Lag + Carbonaceous oxidation (heterotrophic bacteria consume organic carbon: C → CO₂) | **Carbonaceous BOD (CBOD)** |
| **Phase 2** | 5 – 7 days | Plateau / Nitrification lag (ammonia-oxidizing bacteria adapt) | **Inter-phase plateau** |
| **Phase 3** | 7 – 20+ days | Nitrogenous oxidation (ammonia → nitrite → nitrate) | **Nitrogenous BOD (NBOD)** |

## 1.3 Defining NBOD (Nitrogenous BOD)

**Nitrogenous BOD (NBOD)** is the second-stage oxygen demand exerted during the biological oxidation of ammonia (released from nitrogenous organics) all the way to nitrate. The governing biological process is **nitrification**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Nitrification: } 2\text{NH}_4^+ + 3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} 2\text{NO}_2^- + 4\text{H}^+ + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}}
$$

$$
\boxed{2\text{NO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter}} 2\text{NO}_3^-}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand of Nitrification**
> 1 mole of NH₄⁺ → NO₃⁻ consumes **2 moles (4.57 g) of O₂ per gram of NH₃-N oxidized**. This ratio (4.57 mg O₂/mg NH₃-N) is a **board-favorite number** in KTU examinations.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Course Dinner" of Bacteria

Imagine a banquet hall where two different sets of guests arrive at different times:

- **First seating (0–5 days):** The **carbonaceous bacteria** (heterotrophs) — like hungry teenagers — rush in first and devour the easily digestible "sugar" (carbohydrates, sugars, fatty acids). They burn through the carbon snacks using oxygen rapidly. This is your **CBOD** — a quick, dramatic dip in dissolved oxygen.

- **Second seating (5–20+ days):** The **nitrifying bacteria** (chemoautotrophs like *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter*) are slower, pickier diners. They only want ammonia (NH₃/NH₄⁺) and convert it methodically into nitrite and then nitrate. Each gram of NH₃-N they process forces them to consume **4.57 mg of O₂** from the water. This is your **NBOD** — a second, gentler, but persistent dip in DO.

- **In the same bottle, you observe a two-stepped BOD curve**: a sharp first drop, a plateau where DO recovery occurs, and a second drop — exactly the two-hump curve classic to wastewater engineering.

> [!TIP]
> **The Nitrate "Oxygen Wallet"**
> When wastewater containing NO₃⁻ is released into low-oxygen (anoxic) water bodies, denitrifying bacteria can *return* that oxygen. So nitrates act as a "stored oxygen wallet" that can be spent later via denitrification.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Order Kinetics of BOD Exertion (The Theoretical Foundation)

The rate of BOD exertion follows **first-order kinetics** — meaning the rate is proportional to the amount of remaining (unoxidized) organic matter still present at time *t*.

Let:
- $L$ = Ultimate (total) first-stage BOD remaining at time *t* (mg/L)
- $L_0$ = Ultimate first-stage BOD at time *t* = 0 (mg/L)
- $k_1$ = First-order deoxygenation rate constant (base *e*, day⁻¹), typically **0.1 – 0.5 /day** for municipal wastewater at 20 °C
- $t$ = Time in days
- $y$ = BOD exerted up to time *t* (mg/L)

The governing differential equation is:

$$
\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 \cdot L
$$

**Why first-order?** Because bacteria multiply and consume substrate simultaneously — but in classic Streeter-Phelps modeling, the rate-limiting factor is the *concentration of remaining organic matter*, not the number of bacteria (which rapidly reaches steady state).

## 2.2 The Integrated BOD Equation

Integrating the above with the boundary condition that at $t = 0$, $L = L_0$:

$$
L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}
$$

Since **BOD exerted** + **BOD remaining** = **Ultimate BOD**:

$$
y = L_0 - L_t = L_0 \left( 1 - e^{-k_1 t} \right)
$$

This is the **Thériault / Streeter-Phelps BOD equation** — the most-tested formula in KTU environmental biotechnology modules.

## 2.3 The Complete (Two-Phase) BOD Curve Equation

When **both carbonaceous and nitrogenous** demands are considered, the total BOD exerted at time $t$ is:

$$
y_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{L_0 \left( 1 - e^{-k_1 t} \right)}_{\text{Carbonaceous (CBOD)}} \; + \; \underbrace{L_N \left( 1 - e^{-k_2 (t - t_N)} \right)}_{\text{Nitrogenous (NBOD), starts after lag } t_N}
$$

Where:
- $L_0$ = Ultimate carbonaceous BOD (mg/L)
- $L_N$ = Ultimate nitrogenous BOD (mg/L), related to ammonia-N via $L_N = 4.57 \times [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}]$
- $k_1$ = Carbonaceous rate constant (0.1–0.5 /day)
- $k_2$ = Nitrogenous rate constant (**typically 0.05–0.15 /day**, slower than $k_1$)
- $t_N$ = Nitrification lag time (typically 5–8 days in unseeded samples)

## 2.4 Rate Constant Base Conversion

A common KTU exam trap: rate constants in different log bases are **not interchangeable**.

$$
\boxed{k_{(\text{base } e)} = \frac{k_{(\text{base } 10)}}{2.303} \quad \text{or} \quad k = 2.303 \cdot k'}
$$

A common rule of thumb for municipal wastewater at 20 °C is $k_1 \approx 0.23/\text{day}$ (base $e$).

## 2.5 Temperature Correction of Rate Constants

Real wastewater is rarely at exactly 20 °C. KTU tests frequently use the **van't Hoff-Arrhenius** temperature correction:

$$
\boxed{k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}}
$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = Temperature coefficient (≈ **1.047** for CBOD, **1.10** for NBOD)
- $T$ = Actual temperature (°C)
- $k_{20}$ = Rate constant at 20 °C

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All High-Yield Formulas for NBOD / BOD Curve**

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|:--------|:--------|:------------------|:------|
| BOD exerted (CBOD) | $y = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t})$ | $L_0$: mg/L; $k_1$: day⁻¹; $t$: days | First-order Streeter-Phelps |
| BOD remaining | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t}$ | mg/L | Decays exponentially |
| NBOD from ammonia | $L_N = 4.57 \times [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}]$ | 4.57 = mg O₂ per mg NH₃-N | Stoichiometric ratio |
| Total BOD | $y_t = L_0(1-e^{-k_1 t}) + L_N(1-e^{-k_2(t-t_N)})$ | mg/L | Two-phase BOD curve |
| Base conversion | $k = 2.303 \, k'$ | day⁻¹ | $k'$ is base 10 |
| Temperature correction | $k_T = k_{20}\,\theta^{(T-20)}$ | $\theta \approx 1.047$ (CBOD), 1.10 (NBOD) | van't Hoff rule |
| BOD₅ / BODₗ ratio | $f = 1 - e^{-k_1 \cdot 5}$ | dimensionless | Used to find ultimate BOD from BOD₅ |
| Ultimate from BOD₅ | $L_0 = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}$ | mg/L | Inverse of ratio |
| BOD curve inflection | $t = \dfrac{1}{k_1}$ | days | Point of maximum curvature |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sewage Treatment Plant Design:** The BOD curve determines aeration tank volume, retention time, and oxygen supply rate (kg O₂/day) for activated sludge processes.
- **Receiving Water (River) Standards:** NBOD after chlorination can re-grow ammonia; standard **BOD₅ test uses allyl thiourea (ATU)** to suppress nitrification and isolate only CBOD.
- **Self-Purification of Rivers:** The Streeter-Phelps DO sag equation uses these rate constants to predict the *minimum dissolved oxygen* downstream of a discharge point.
- **Industrial Effluent Monitoring:** Industries with high ammonia (e.g., fertilizers, tanneries) are regulated on **Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN)** to predict NBOD loading.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the First-Order BOD Equation

### Step 1: State the empirical observation (Rate Law)
The rate at which organic matter is being oxidized (and hence oxygen consumed) at any instant is directly proportional to the amount of organic matter still remaining at that instant.

$$
\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 \cdot L \quad \text{(Equation 1)}
$$

Where the negative sign indicates that $L$ *decreases* with time.

### Step 2: Separate variables
Rearrange the differential equation by bringing all $L$ terms to one side and $t$ terms to the other:

$$
\frac{dL}{L} = -k_1 \, dt \quad \text{(Equation 2)}
$$

### Step 3: Integrate both sides
Apply integration from initial conditions ($t = 0$, $L = L_0$) to general time $t$ (where remaining is $L$):

$$
\int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = -k_1 \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integrals
The integral of $1/L$ is $\ln(L)$. So:

$$
\bigl[\ln L\bigr]_{L_0}^{L} = -k_1 \, t
$$

$$
\ln L - \ln L_0 = -k_1 \, t
$$

### Step 5: Apply logarithm rules
Use the property $\ln A - \ln B = \ln(A/B)$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = -k_1 \, t
$$

### Step 6: Exponentiate to remove the logarithm

$$
\frac{L}{L_0} = e^{-k_1 t}
$$

### Step 7: Solve for remaining BOD ($L$)

$$
L = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 t} \quad \text{(Equation A — BOD Remaining)}
$$

### Step 8: Convert to "BOD Exerted" (y)
By conservation: $y = L_0 - L$

$$
y = L_0 - L_0 e^{-k_1 t}
$$

$$
\boxed{y = L_0 \left( 1 - e^{-k_1 t} \right) \quad \text{(Equation B — BOD Exerted)}}
$$

### Step 9: Verify boundary conditions
- At $t = 0$: $y = L_0(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓ (no oxygen consumed yet)
- At $t \to \infty$: $y = L_0(1 - 0) = L_0$ ✓ (all ultimate BOD exerted)

This is the most important result; **memorize Equation B**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Converting BOD₅ to Ultimate BOD

A classic KTU 14-mark problem: "Given BOD₅ and $k_1$, find $L_0$."

From Equation B, at $t = 5$ days:

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 \left( 1 - e^{-5 k_1} \right)
$$

Solving for $L_0$:

$$
L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}
$$

If $k_1 = 0.23/\text{day}$ (typical), then $e^{-5 \times 0.23} = e^{-1.15} \approx 0.3166$

$$
1 - 0.3166 = 0.6834
$$

$$
\boxed{L_0 \approx \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{0.6834} \approx 1.46 \times \text{BOD}_5}
$$

This is why the "BOD₅ ≈ 68% of ultimate" rule of thumb is widely used.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style)

### Problem:
A wastewater sample has a 5-day BOD of **200 mg/L** at 20 °C and a deoxygenation constant $k_1 = 0.23/\text{day}$. Find:
1. The ultimate first-stage BOD ($L_0$).
2. The 7-day BOD.
3. The BOD remaining after 5 days.
4. The rate of oxygen consumption at $t = 5$ days.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Ultimate BOD $L_0$:**

$$
L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}} = \frac{200}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23}} = \frac{200}{1 - e^{-1.15}}
$$

$$
e^{-1.15} = 0.3166
$$

$$
L_0 = \frac{200}{1 - 0.3166} = \frac{200}{0.6834} \approx 292.6 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**Part (b) — 7-day BOD:**

$$
\text{BOD}_7 = L_0 (1 - e^{-7 k_1}) = 292.6 \times (1 - e^{-7 \times 0.23})
$$

$$
7 \times 0.23 = 1.61
$$

$$
e^{-1.61} = 0.1999
$$

$$
\text{BOD}_7 = 292.6 \times (1 - 0.1999) = 292.6 \times 0.8001 \approx 234.1 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**Part (c) — BOD remaining after 5 days:**

$$
L_5 = L_0 \cdot e^{-5 k_1} = 292.6 \times 0.3166 \approx 92.6 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**Cross-check:** $L_0 - L_5 = 292.6 - 92.6 = 200$ mg/L ✓ (matches BOD₅)

**Part (d) — Rate of oxygen consumption at $t = 5$ days:**

The rate of BOD exertion equals $-\dfrac{dL}{dt} = k_1 \cdot L$

$$
\text{Rate} = 0.23 \times 92.6 \approx 21.3 \, \text{mg/L/day}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — NBOD Component

### Problem:
A wastewater sample contains **30 mg/L of NH₃-N**. After complete nitrification:
1. Calculate the ultimate NBOD ($L_N$).
2. If the nitrification rate constant is $k_2 = 0.10/\text{day}$ and the nitrification lag is 6 days, find the NBOD exerted at $t = 12$ days.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Ultimate NBOD:**

$$
L_N = 4.57 \times [\text{NH}_3\text{-N}] = 4.57 \times 30 = 137.1 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**Part (b) — NBOD exerted at $t = 12$ days:**

The effective time for NBOD is $t - t_N = 12 - 6 = 6$ days.

$$
\text{NBOD}_{12} = L_N \left( 1 - e^{-k_2(t - t_N)} \right) = 137.1 \times \left( 1 - e^{-0.10 \times 6} \right)
$$

$$
0.10 \times 6 = 0.60
$$

$$
e^{-0.60} = 0.5488
$$

$$
\text{NBOD}_{12} = 137.1 \times (1 - 0.5488) = 137.1 \times 0.4512 \approx 61.9 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

---

## 3.5 Temperature Correction Worked Example

### Problem:
A river discharge has $k_{20} = 0.25/\text{day}$ for CBOD. The river temperature is **28 °C**. Find the equivalent $k$ at 28 °C using $\theta = 1.047$.

### Solution:

$$
k_{28} = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(28 - 20)} = 0.25 \times 1.047^{8}
$$

$$
1.047^{8} \approx 1.446
$$

$$
k_{28} \approx 0.25 \times 1.446 = 0.3615 \, \text{/day}
$$

> **Interpretation:** BOD exerts ~45% faster in summer than in winter — a critical design parameter for tropical Kerala rivers (avg 26–30 °C) versus winter Hill-region streams (16–20 °C).

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Classic Two-Phase BOD Curve (Schematic)

The following Mermaid block renders the conceptual shape of the **BOD exertion curve** including both the carbonaceous (CBOD) and nitrogenous (NBOD) phases.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start t equals 0] --> B[CBOD phase: rapid rise]
    B --> C[CBOD plateau near L0]
    C --> D[Nitrification lag: 5 to 7 days]
    D --> E[NBOD phase: second rise]
    E --> F[Total BOD approaches L0 plus LN asymptotically]
    F --> G[Ultimate Total BOD reached at t greater than 20 days]
    style A fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#ddf,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — BOD Exertion Sub-Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inlet[Influent Wastewater in BOD Bottle] --> C1{Heterotrophs present}
    C1 -->|Yes| C2[Oxidize organic carbon to CO2 and H2O]
    C2 --> C3[Use O2 from dissolved phase]
    C3 --> C4[Result: CBOD exerted curve]
    inlet --> N1{Ammonia Oxidizing Bacteria present}
    N1 -->|After 5 to 7 day lag| N2[Nitrosomonas oxidizes NH4 to NO2]
    N2 --> N3[Nitrobacter oxidizes NO2 to NO3]
    N3 --> N4[Each mg NH3-N consumes 4.57 mg O2]
    N4 --> N5[Result: NBOD exerted curve]
    C4 --> sum[Total BOD equals CBOD plus NBOD]
    N5 --> sum
    style inlet fill:#eef
    style C4 fill:#fdd
    style N5 fill:#dfd
    style sum fill:#ffd
```

```mermaid

        A1[Time: 0 to 2 days] --> A2[Heterotrophic oxidation]
        A2 --> A3[CBOD exerted approaches L0]
    end

        B1[Time: 2 to 7 days] --> B2[Nitrifier adaptation]

    style Phase2 fill:#fffacd
    style Phase3 fill:#e0f7e0
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Carbonaceous BOD (CBOD) and Nitrogenous BOD (NBOD).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CBOD | NBOD |
|:----------|:-----|:-----|
| Definition | Oxygen required to oxidize organic carbon | Oxygen required to oxidize ammonia to nitrate |
| Organisms | Heterotrophic bacteria (*E. coli*, etc.) | Chemoautotrophs (*Nitrosomonas*, *Nitrobacter*) |
| Duration | 0–5 days | 5–20+ days |
| Rate constant $k$ | 0.1 – 0.5 /day (faster) | 0.05 – 0.15 /day (slower) |
| Stoichiometry | 1 mol O₂ per mol CH₂O carbon | 4.57 mg O₂ per mg NH₃-N |
| Inhibitor used in test | Allyl thiourea (ATU) is added to suppress this | Suppressed by ATU; occurs naturally after day 5 |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is the standard 5-day BOD test incubated at 20 °C in the dark?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **20 °C** is a standard reference temperature allowing comparison between laboratories globally and avoiding microbial activity extremes (too slow at low T, too fast at high T).
2. **Darkness** prevents photosynthesis from generating DO inside the bottle, which would mask the true oxygen consumption and lead to underestimation of BOD.
3. The bottle is sealed to prevent atmospheric oxygen re-dissolution during incubation, ensuring that the only DO available is what was initially dissolved in the sample.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Module in the End Semester Exam carries 14 marks with internal choice. Two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Derive the first-order BOD equation $y = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t})$ and use it to solve the following problem.**

A wastewater sample gives the following data at 20 °C:

| Time (days) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|:------------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:--:|
| BOD (mg/L) | 110 | 180 | 230 | 260 | 275 |

Determine:
**(a)** The ultimate BOD $L_0$ and the deoxygenation constant $k_1$. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The BOD₅ value and the rate of oxygen consumption at $t = 5$ days. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

We use the **Thomas slope method** (linearized form of the first-order equation) to determine $L_0$ and $k_1$:

$$
\left(\frac{t}{y}\right)^{1/3} = \frac{1}{k_1^{1/3} \cdot L_0^{2/3}} + \frac{k_1^{2/3}}{6 L_0^{1/3}} \cdot t
$$

Calculate $(t/y)^{1/3}$ for each data point:

| $t$ | $y$ | $t/y$ | $(t/y)^{1/3}$ |
|:----|:----|:------|:---------------|
| 2 | 110 | 0.01818 | 0.2626 |
| 4 | 180 | 0.02222 | 0.2812 |
| 6 | 230 | 0.02609 | 0.2964 |
| 8 | 260 | 0.03077 | 0.3135 |
| 10 | 275 | 0.03636 | 0.3303 |

Plotting $(t/y)^{1/3}$ vs $t$ gives a straight line. From the slope and intercept (linear regression), the best-fit values are:

- **Slope** ≈ 0.00848
- **Intercept** ≈ 0.2455

From the linear equation:

$$
k_1 = \frac{2.61 \times \text{slope}^{3/2}}{\text{intercept}^{1/2} \cdot 6^{3/2}} \quad \text{(standard Thomas formula)}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
k_1 \approx 0.245 \, \text{/day}
$$

And:

$$
L_0 = \frac{1}{k_1 \cdot \text{intercept}^{3}} = \frac{1}{0.245 \times (0.2455)^{3}} \approx 280 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**(Alternative method using least-squares regression on $y$ vs $t$):** Direct nonlinear fitting using Excel Solver or graphical estimation yields similar results: $L_0 \approx 280\text{–}290$ mg/L and $k_1 \approx 0.24\text{–}0.25$ /day.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Using $L_0 = 280$ mg/L and $k_1 = 0.245$ /day:

**BOD₅:**

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = 280 \times (1 - e^{-0.245 \times 5}) = 280 \times (1 - e^{-1.225})
$$

$$
e^{-1.225} \approx 0.2938
$$

$$
\text{BOD}_5 = 280 \times (1 - 0.2938) = 280 \times 0.7062 \approx 197.7 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**Rate of oxygen consumption at $t = 5$ days:**

BOD remaining:

$$
L_5 = L_0 \cdot e^{-k_1 \cdot 5} = 280 \times 0.2938 = 82.3 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

Rate:

$$
\frac{dy}{dt} = k_1 \cdot L_t = 0.245 \times 82.3 \approx 20.2 \, \text{mg/L/day}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **complete BOD curve** for a domestic wastewater sample, clearly marking the carbonaceous, nitrification lag, and nitrogenous phases. List the stoichiometric reactions involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal wastewater has a TKN of 40 mg/L (assumed all as NH₃-N), and the nitrification rate constant $k_2 = 0.12/\text{day}$ with a lag time of 6 days. If the carbonaceous BOD₅ = 220 mg/L and $k_1 = 0.23/\text{day}$, calculate: **(7 marks)**
1. The ultimate NBOD.
2. The NBOD exerted by $t = 10$ days.
3. The **total BOD at $t = 10$ days** (CBOD + NBOD).

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A neatly labeled BOD curve has:
- **X-axis:** Time in days (0 to 30)
- **Y-axis:** BOD exerted in mg/L (0 to ~400)
- **Three regions:**
  1. **CBOD region (0–5 days):** Rapid rise following $y = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t})$; typically completes ~70% of $L_0$ in 5 days.
  2. **Nitrification lag (5–7 days):** A near-plateau where carbonaceous oxidation is largely complete and nitrifiers are multiplying.
  3. **NBOD region (7–20+ days):** A second, gentler rise as $L_N$ is approached. Mathematically: $y_N = L_N(1 - e^{-k_2(t-t_N)})$.

**Stoichiometric Reactions:**

$$
\text{CBOD: } \text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{heterotrophs}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{NBOD (Step 1): } 2\text{NH}_4^+ + 3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrosomonas}} 2\text{NO}_2^- + 4\text{H}^+ + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{NBOD (Step 2): } 2\text{NO}_2^- + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Nitrobacter}} 2\text{NO}_3^- \quad (\text{net: } 4.57 \text{ g O}_2/\text{g NH}_3\text{-N})
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Ultimate NBOD:**

$$
L_N = 4.57 \times 40 = 182.8 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**2. NBOD exerted at $t = 10$ days:**

Effective time for NBOD: $t - t_N = 10 - 6 = 4$ days.

$$
\text{NBOD}_{10} = 182.8 \times (1 - e^{-0.12 \times 4}) = 182.8 \times (1 - e^{-0.48})
$$

$$
e^{-0.48} \approx 0.6188
$$

$$
\text{NBOD}_{10} = 182.8 \times (1 - 0.6188) = 182.8 \times 0.3812 \approx 69.7 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

**3. Total BOD at $t = 10$ days:**

First, find ultimate CBOD:

$$
L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23}} = \frac{220}{1 - e^{-1.15}} = \frac{220}{0.6834} \approx 322.0 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

CBOD at $t = 10$ days:

$$
\text{CBOD}_{10} = 322.0 \times (1 - e^{-0.23 \times 10}) = 322.0 \times (1 - e^{-2.3})
$$

$$
e^{-2.3} \approx 0.1003
$$

$$
\text{CBOD}_{10} = 322.0 \times (1 - 0.1003) = 322.0 \times 0.8997 \approx 289.7 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

Total BOD at $t = 10$ days:

$$
\text{Total BOD}_{10} = \text{CBOD}_{10} + \text{NBOD}_{10} = 289.7 + 69.7 = 359.4 \, \text{mg/L}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the lag time:** Students often plug $t = 10$ directly into the NBOD equation without subtracting $t_N = 6$ days. The correct form is $L_N(1 - e^{-k_2(t - t_N)})$.
> - **Confusing $k$ and $k'$:** Make sure the question specifies the base (base $e$ or base 10). KTU 2024 papers mostly use base $e$.
> - **Unit slip-ups:** $k$ is in day⁻¹; $L$ in mg/L; $t$ in days. Mixing units (e.g., hours) forfeits 1 mark.
> - **Missing stoichiometric ratio 4.57:** Always state the NH₃-N to O₂ ratio when dealing with nitrification; it is worth 1 mark in itself.
> - **No nitrification inhibitor mentioned in BOD₅:** Always clarify in the answer that BOD₅ (with ATU) measures only CBOD, while BOD₅ (without ATU) also includes a partial NBOD contribution if nitrifiers are seeded.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **BOD₅ = ~68% of Ultimate CBOD** at $k_1 = 0.23$/day (rule of thumb).
- **Stoichiometric flag:** **4.57 mg O₂ per mg NH₃-N** — memorize this for any nitrification question.
- **Equation of BOD exerted:** $y = L_0(1 - e^{-k_1 t})$ — this *one* equation solves 80% of KTU problems.
- **Two humps on the curve:** First hump = CBOD (fast, days 0–5); second hump = NBOD (slow, days 7–20+).
- **Nitrification lag:** Typically **5–8 days** in unseeded samples; virtually absent in seeded/seeded-with-nitrifier samples.
- **BOD test conditions:** 20 °C, dark, 5 days, sealed bottle — examiner's favorite viva question.
- **$k$ is temperature-sensitive:** Use $\theta^{(T-20)}$ with $\theta = 1.047$ (CBOD) and $\theta = 1.10$ (NBOD).
- **Bottle dilution technique:** Used for high-strength wastewaters; final BOD = (DO loss × Dilution factor).
- **Allyl Thiourea (ATU):** Inhibits nitrification — ensures BOD₅ measures only CBOD.
- **Nitrification rate is slower:** $k_2 \approx 0.05\text{–}0.15$/day vs. $k_1 \approx 0.1\text{–}0.5$/day.
- **Denitrification is the reverse:** NO₃⁻ → N₂ gas (anaerobic, no O₂ consumed; instead, O₂ is *returned*).
- **Streeter-Phelps sag equation** (advanced): Combines BOD exertion with reaeration to predict minimum DO in a polluted river.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics**
> - "**C**arbon before **N**itrogen" → CBOD before NBOD
> - "**47**" → 4.57 ratio
> - "**6 8**" → BOD₅ ≈ 68% of ultimate
> - "**2.303**" → log-to-natural base converter for rate constants

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BOD Measurement by Dilution Method, COD Measurement, Dissolved Oxygen Sag Curve, BOD Removal Kinetics, Wastewater Treatment: Aerobic Biofilm Processes - Basic Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** BOD Measurement by Dilution Method, COD Measurement, Dissolved Oxygen Sag Curve, BOD Removal Kinetics, Wastewater Treatment: Aerobic Biofilm Processes - Basic Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) — Dilution Method

**Formal Definition:**
Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) is the standard quantitative measure of the amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the organic matter present in a water/wastewater sample under controlled conditions of temperature (**20 °C**) and time (**5 days**), as standardized by APHA Standard Methods.

The **Dilution Method (BOD₅)** is the universally accepted technique where the wastewater sample is diluted with oxygen-saturated, nutrient-buffered dilution water, sealed in a BOD bottle, and incubated for 5 days at 20 °C. The difference between initial and final DO, multiplied by the dilution factor, gives the BOD₅.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The Hunger Test for Bacteria"**
> Imagine a sealed terrarium (BOD bottle) containing bacteria and food (organic waste). The only source of oxygen is the air trapped at the top. If the bacteria are "very hungry" (high organic load), they consume oxygen rapidly, and the oxygen level drops drastically. If the food is scarce, oxygen remains high. BOD essentially measures *how much oxygen the bacteria ate in 5 days* — it is an indirect measure of the "food" (pollution) present.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The BOD₅ subscript "5" is non-negotiable in exam answers. Always specify **5 days at 20 °C** to score full marks.

---

### 1.2 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)

**Formal Definition:**
Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) is the measure of the total quantity of oxygen required to chemically oxidize both **biodegradable and non-biodegradable** organic matter present in water, using a strong chemical oxidant (**potassium dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇**, in a 50% H₂SO₄ medium), under reflux for 2 hours, with silver sulfate (Ag₂SO₄) as a catalyst and mercuric sulfate (HgSO₄) to prevent chloride interference.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "Burn It All Test"**
> If BOD is the "slow-cooked meal the bacteria can digest in 5 days," COD is the "burn the entire food in a furnace" test. A chemical oven (dichromate) oxidizes EVERYTHING organic, including substances bacteria cannot touch (e.g., plastics, lignin, cellulose fibers). Hence, **COD is always greater than BOD** for the same wastewater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The ratio **BOD/COD** indicates the **biodegradability** of wastewater. A ratio > 0.5 is highly biodegradable; < 0.3 suggests poor biodegradability.

---

### 1.3 Dissolved Oxygen Sag Curve (Streeter-Phelps Equation)

**Formal Definition:**
The Dissolved Oxygen Sag Curve describes the variation of dissolved oxygen concentration along the length/downstream distance of a river, downstream of a point source of organic pollution discharge. It quantitatively predicts the **spatial depletion and recovery of DO** using the classic **Streeter-Phelps model**, which balances the deoxygenation rate (k₁) against the reaeration rate (k₂).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The River's Breathing Pattern"**
> Picture a healthy river receiving sewage. Just below the discharge point, oxygen is high (fresh water mixing). As bacteria feast on the sewage, oxygen plummets — the river "holds its breath." Further downstream, atmospheric oxygen re-enters via turbulence, and the river "breathes again," recovering to saturation. The sag curve plots this exact "breath cycle."

---

### 1.4 BOD Removal Kinetics

**Formal Definition:**
BOD removal kinetics mathematically describes the rate at which organic substrate (BOD) is consumed by microbial populations in biological treatment systems. The standard model follows **first-order substrate utilization kinetics**, expressed as:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = -k_1 L$$

where L is the remaining BOD at time t, and k₁ is the deoxygenation rate constant (base e, day⁻¹).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "Half-Life of Pollution"**
> Like radioactive decay, organic matter doesn't vanish instantly — it decays exponentially. The "half-life" of pollution is governed by k₁. A larger k₁ means the bacteria are more efficient (faster cleanup).

---

### 1.5 Aerobic Biofilm Processes — Basic Principle

**Formal Definition:**
Aerobic biofilm processes are attached-growth biological treatment systems in which microorganisms grow as a **slimy, layered biological film (biofilm)** attached to an inert solid support medium (e.g., rocks, plastic media, ceramic). Wastewater flows over the biofilm, and as organic matter diffuses into the film, aerobic microbes (in the outer layers) oxidize it using dissolved oxygen supplied by the air/water interface.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The City on a Rock"**
> Imagine a slimy "microbial city" growing on a pebble in a stream. Outer city-dwellers (aerobic bacteria) get oxygen from the flowing water. Inner dwellers (anaerobic) live in oxygen-free zones. The "city" feeds on the organic pollution in the water, effectively filtering it. Trickling filters, RBCs, and MBBRs are all variants of this "city on a rock" concept.

---

### 1.6 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - Standard BOD incubation: **5 days at 20 °C**
> - Standard COD oxidant: **K₂Cr₂O₇ in 50% H₂SO₄**, reflux 2 hours
> - Typical k₁ (deoxygenation): **0.1 – 0.3 day⁻¹ (base e)** at 20 °C
> - Typical k₂ (reaeration): **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹ (base e)** at 20 °C (varies with river depth/velocity)
> - Saturation DO at 20 °C, 1 atm: **9.2 mg/L**
