## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BOD Dilution Method — Operational Logic

The BOD₅ dilution method operates on the following structured logic:

1. **Sample Preservation:** The wastewater is preserved at **4 °C or below** to arrest biological activity before analysis.
2. **Dilution Preparation:** A measured volume of sample (V_s) is added to a BOD bottle and diluted with **oxygen-saturated, phosphate-buffered dilution water** containing nutrients (MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃) and a nitrification inhibitor (allylthiourea) to restrict oxygen demand to carbonaceous BOD only.
3. **Initial DO Measurement:** Initial DO of diluted sample (DO_i) is measured using a **Winkler titration** or modern **DO membrane electrode**.
4. **Incubation:** The bottle is sealed (with a water seal to prevent air ingress) and incubated at **20 °C ± 1 °C** for exactly **5 days**.
5. **Final DO Measurement:** After 5 days, final DO (DO_f) is measured.
6. **BOD Calculation:** The 5-day BOD is computed as:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{(DO_i - DO_f) - (B_c \times f)}{P}$$

where:
- B_c = DO depletion of seed (blank correction)
- f = fraction of seed volume in diluted sample
- P = decimal volumetric fraction of wastewater in diluted sample = V_s / (V_s + V_d)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dilution Water Requirement:** The dilution must yield a final DO ≥ 2 mg/L and a depletion ≥ 2 mg/L for valid results. If 5-day depletion is too small (< 2 mg/L), use larger sample volume. If depletion is too large (residual DO < 1 mg/L), use greater dilution.

### 2.2 COD Test — Operational Logic

1. **Reflux Reaction:** Sample + standard K₂Cr₂O₇ + H₂SO₄ + Ag₂SO₄ (catalyst) + HgSO₄ (chloride mask) are refluxed for 2 hours.
2. **Excess Dichromate Titration:** After cooling, the unreacted K₂Cr₂O₇ is titrated with **Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS)** using **ferroin indicator** (color change: blue-green → reddish-brown).
3. **Blank Titration:** A blank (distilled water) is refluxed and titrated identically.
4. **COD Calculation:**

$$\text{COD (mg/L)} = \frac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V_s}$$

where:
- A = volume of FAS used for blank (mL)
- B = volume of FAS used for sample (mL)
- N = normality of FAS (typically 0.1 N)
- V_s = volume of sample (mL)
- 8000 = milliequivalent weight of O₂ × 1000 mL/L

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always state the use of **Ag₂SO₄ (catalyst) and HgSO₄ (chloride complexation)**. These are 2-mark "extra information" gems that examiners love.

### 2.3 Dissolved Oxygen Sag Curve — Theoretical Framework

The **Streeter-Phelps Equation** (1925) is the foundational model:

$$D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left( e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t} \right) + D_0 \, e^{-k_2 t}$$

where:
- D_t = oxygen deficit at time t (mg/L) = (DO_sat − DO_actual)
- L_0 = initial ultimate BOD at discharge point (mg/L)
- k₁ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹)
- k₂ = reaeration rate constant (day⁻¹)
- D_0 = initial oxygen deficit at the discharge point (mg/L)
- t = travel time downstream (days) = x/u, where x = distance, u = stream velocity

**Critical Point — Critical Deficit and Critical Time:**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \left[ 1 - \frac{D_0 (k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0} \right]$$

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$$

### 2.4 BOD Removal Kinetics — First-Order Model

The **integrated first-order BOD equation:**

$$L_t = L_0 \, e^{-k_1 t}$$

$$\text{BOD exerted at time } t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 (1 - e^{-k_1 t})$$

The BOD remaining (oxygen debt yet to be exerted) is L_t. The **ultimate BOD** (L_0) is approached asymptotically as t → ∞.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BOD₅ vs Ultimate BOD Relationship:**
> $$\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 (1 - e^{-5 k_1})$$
> 
> $$\text{Therefore, } L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}$$

### 2.5 Aerobic Biofilm — Structural & Functional Logic

A biofilm is a stratified microbial ecosystem:

1. **Substrate Layer (Bulk Liquid):** Wastewater flowing over the film contains dissolved organics and DO.
2. **Diffusion Boundary Layer:** A stagnant liquid film where substrate/oxygen diffuse from bulk to biofilm surface.
3. **Aerobic Zone (Outer Biofilm, ~50–200 µm):** Aerobic heterotrophs, nitrifiers oxidize organics; DO is consumed.
4. **Anoxic Zone (Middle):** Denitrifiers thrive if nitrate is present.
5. **Anaerobic Zone (Inner Biofilm, attached to media):** Anaerobes ferment organics; H₂S, CH₄ may be produced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofilm Thickness Rule:** When biofilm thickness < **~100 µm**, the entire film remains aerobic. Above this, anaerobic pockets develop — this is called **"deep biofilm effect."**

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{DO_i - DO_f}{P}$ | BOD from dilution method (uncorrected) | mg/L |
| $L_t = L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$ | First-order BOD remaining | mg/L |
| $\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 (1 - e^{-5 k_1})$ | 5-day BOD from ultimate BOD | mg/L |
| $\text{COD} = \frac{(A-B) \times N \times 8000}{V_s}$ | COD from dichromate titration | mg/L |
| $D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t}\right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | Streeter-Phelps oxygen deficit | mg/L |
| $t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)\left[1 - \frac{D_0(k_2-k_1)}{k_1 L_0}\right]$ | Critical time to maximum deficit | days |
| $D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$ | Critical (maximum) oxygen deficit | mg/L |
| $t_{critical} \approx \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$ | Simplified critical time (when D₀ ≈ 0) | days |
| $\text{BOD/COD}$ | Biodegradability index | dimensionless |
| $D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{initial}$ | Initial oxygen deficit | mg/L |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Engineers Must Master This:**
> - **Effluent Discharge Compliance:** CPCB/SPCB standards mandate BOD < 30 mg/L and COD < 250 mg/L for inland surface discharge.
> - **Treatment Plant Design:** k₁ and k₂ values directly determine aeration tank volume and aeration capacity in activated sludge and biofilm reactors.
> - **River Self-Purification Studies:** Sag curves predict the "zone of degradation," "zone of active decomposition," and "zone of recovery" — critical for environmental impact assessment.
> - **Receiving Water Body Capacity:** Determines whether a river can assimilate a given pollution load (assimilative capacity).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BOD₅ from First-Order Kinetics

**Starting Principle:** Microbial oxidation of organic matter follows first-order kinetics.

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k_1 L_t$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange and integrate with limits [L₀ at t=0] to [L_t at t=t]:

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k_1 \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\left[ \ln L_t \right]_{L_0}^{L_t} = -k_1 [t]_0^t$$

**Step 3:** Apply limits:

$$\ln L_t - \ln L_0 = -k_1 t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k_1 t$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{L_t}{L_0} = e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, BOD exerted up to time t:

$$Y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 - L_0 e^{-k_1 t} = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k_1 t}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute t = 5 days to obtain BOD₅:

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5 k_1}\right)}$$

**Step 7 (Inverse):** If BOD₅ is known, ultimate BOD is:

$$L_0 = \frac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5 k_1}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Streeter-Phelps Equation

**Mass Balance on a River Reach:**

The rate of change of oxygen deficit (D) downstream = Deoxygenation rate − Reaeration rate

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L_t - k_2 D$$

**Step 1:** The deoxygenation term uses the residual BOD at time t: L_t = L_0 e^{-k₁ t}

**Step 2:** Substitute into the differential equation:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t} - k_2 D$$

**Step 3:** This is a first-order linear ODE. Rearrange to standard form:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} + k_2 D = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t}$$

**Step 4:** The integrating factor is $e^{k_2 t}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left( D \cdot e^{k_2 t} \right) = k_1 L_0 e^{(k_2 - k_1) t}$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from 0 to t:

$$D \cdot e^{k_2 t} - D_0 = k_1 L_0 \int_0^t e^{(k_2 - k_1) \tau} d\tau$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the integral (assuming k₂ ≠ k₁):

$$D \cdot e^{k_2 t} - D_0 = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left[ e^{(k_2 - k_1) t} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 7:** Multiply through by e^{-k₂ t}:

$$D = D_0 e^{-k_2 t} + \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1} \left( e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t} \right)$$

$$\boxed{D_t = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2 - k_1}\left( e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t} \right) + D_0 e^{-k_2 t}}$$

**Step 8 — Critical Point Derivation:**
For maximum deficit, set $\frac{dD}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = k_1 L_0 e^{-k_1 t_c} - k_2 D_c$$

At critical point, D = D_c and the deficit is at extremum, giving:

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \left[ 1 - \frac{D_0 (k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0} \right]$$

If D₀ is negligible (zero initial deficit):

$$\boxed{t_c \approx \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (BOD Dilution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Solved Example for KTU Practice:**

**Problem:** A wastewater sample of 7.5 mL is diluted to 300 mL with BOD dilution water. Initial DO = 8.8 mg/L. After 5 days, final DO = 4.2 mg/L. Calculate BOD₅.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the dilution factor P:

$$P = \frac{V_s}{V_s + V_d} = \frac{7.5}{7.5 + 292.5} = \frac{7.5}{300} = 0.025$$

**Step 2:** DO depletion:

$$\Delta DO = 8.8 - 4.2 = 4.6 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3:** BOD₅:

$$\text{BOD}_5 = \frac{\Delta DO}{P} = \frac{4.6}{0.025} = 184 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 184 \text{ mg/L}}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (COD)

**Problem:** 20 mL of wastewater sample is refluxed with 10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇. After digestion, the excess dichromate requires 7.5 mL of 0.1 N FAS. Blank titration requires 22.4 mL of the same FAS. Calculate COD.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify variables:
- A (blank) = 22.4 mL
- B (sample) = 7.5 mL
- N = 0.1 N
- V_s = 20 mL

**Step 2:** Apply formula:

$$\text{COD} = \frac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V_s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$\text{COD} = \frac{(22.4 - 7.5) \times 0.1 \times 8000}{20}$$

$$\text{COD} = \frac{14.9 \times 0.1 \times 8000}{20} = \frac{11920}{20}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COD} = 596 \text{ mg/L}}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (DO Sag Curve)

**Problem:** A river receives a wastewater discharge with L₀ = 50 mg/L, D₀ = 1.0 mg/L. Given k₁ = 0.25 day⁻¹ and k₂ = 0.40 day⁻¹. Find the critical time and critical deficit. River velocity u = 30 km/day.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate critical time:

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \left[ 1 - \frac{D_0 (k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0} \right]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.40 - 0.25} \ln\left(\frac{0.40}{0.25}\right) \left[ 1 - \frac{1.0 \times (0.40 - 0.25)}{0.25 \times 50} \right]$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each term:

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.15} \times \ln(1.6) \times \left[ 1 - \frac{0.15}{12.5} \right]$$

$$t_c = 6.667 \times 0.4700 \times [1 - 0.012]$$

$$t_c = 6.667 \times 0.4700 \times 0.988$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 3.09 \text{ days}}$$

**Step 4:** Critical deficit:

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c}$$

$$D_c = \frac{0.25 \times 50}{0.40} \times e^{-0.25 \times 3.09}$$

$$D_c = 31.25 \times e^{-0.7725} = 31.25 \times 0.4619$$

$$\boxed{D_c \approx 14.43 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Step 5:** Critical distance:

$$x_c = u \times t_c = 30 \times 3.09 = 92.7 \text{ km}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: BOD vs COD

| Parameter | BOD (5-day, 20 °C) | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Oxidation Type | Biological (microbial) | Chemical (dichromate) |
| Time Required | 5 days | 2–3 hours |
| Oxidant | Dissolved O₂ (aerobic bacteria) | K₂Cr₂O₇ in 50% H₂SO₄ |
| Oxidizes | Biodegradable organics only | Bio + Non-biodegradable |
| Typical Value (municipal WW) | 200–400 mg/L | 400–800 mg/L |
| BOD/COD Ratio | Always higher in ratio | Always lower |
| Interferences | Nitrification, toxic substances | Chloride (masked with HgSO₄) |
| Equipment | BOD bottles, incubator, DO probe/meter | Reflux apparatus, burette |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**"Define BOD and explain why BOD₅ is measured at 20 °C for 5 days."**

**Model Answer:**

Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) is the amount of dissolved oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically decompose the organic matter in a water sample under standard conditions.

BOD is measured for **5 days at 20 °C** for the following reasons:
1. **5 days** is a practical compromise — 100% oxidation of organic matter takes ~20–30 days. BOD₅ represents roughly 65–70% of ultimate BOD, providing a reproducible benchmark.
2. **20 °C** is the average temperature of natural water bodies in temperate climates, allowing extrapolation to field conditions.
3. The standard ensures **uniform comparison** of effluents across laboratories globally.
4. The relationship BOD₅ ≈ 0.68 × BOD_ultimate is empirically established for municipal wastewater.

(3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Differentiate between BOD and COD. Why is COD always greater than BOD?"**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BOD | COD |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of oxidation | Biological | Chemical |
| Oxidizing agent | Dissolved O₂ (microbes) | K₂Cr₂O₇ + H₂SO₄ |
| Time | 5 days | 2 hours |
| Substrate oxidized | Biodegradable organics only | Both biodegradable and non-biodegradable |

**Reason for COD > BOD:** The dichromate in COD is a powerful chemical oxidant that oxidizes **ALL organic matter**, including compounds that are toxic or recalcitrant to microbial attack (e.g., lignin, cellulose, aromatic hydrocarbons, plastics). BOD, being biological, can only oxidize **biodegradable** organics. Therefore, the chemical test always measures a greater oxygen demand than the biological test.

(3 marks)

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *BOD Kinetics + Sag Curve*

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A wastewater has an ultimate BOD (L₀) of 250 mg/L. If the deoxygenation rate constant k₁ = 0.23 day⁻¹ (base e) at 20 °C, calculate:
(i) BOD₅
(ii) BOD remaining after 5 days
(iii) BOD exerted in 7 days

**Model Solution:**

**(i) BOD₅:**

$$\text{BOD}_5 = L_0 (1 - e^{-5 k_1}) = 250 (1 - e^{-5 \times 0.23})$$

$$= 250 (1 - e^{-1.15}) = 250 (1 - 0.3166) = 250 \times 0.6834$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_5 = 170.85 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(ii) BOD remaining after 5 days:**

$$L_5 = L_0 \cdot e^{-5 k_1} = 250 \times e^{-1.15} = 250 \times 0.3166$$

$$\boxed{L_5 = 79.15 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(iii) BOD exerted in 7 days:**

$$\text{BOD}_7 = L_0 (1 - e^{-7 k_1}) = 250 (1 - e^{-7 \times 0.23})$$

$$= 250 (1 - e^{-1.61}) = 250 (1 - 0.1999) = 250 \times 0.8001$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_7 = 200.03 \text{ mg/L}}$$

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

A river has DO = 8.0 mg/L and saturation DO = 9.2 mg/L. A wastewater discharge introduces L₀ = 40 mg/L. Given k₁ = 0.20 day⁻¹ and k₂ = 0.40 day⁻¹, stream velocity = 25 km/day. Calculate:
(i) Initial oxygen deficit
(ii) Critical time
(iii) Critical deficit
(iv) Distance to critical point

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Initial oxygen deficit:**

$$D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{initial} = 9.2 - 8.0 = 1.2 \text{ mg/L}$$

**(ii) Critical time:**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k_2 - k_1} \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) \left[ 1 - \frac{D_0 (k_2 - k_1)}{k_1 L_0} \right]$$

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.40 - 0.20} \ln\left(\frac{0.40}{0.20}\right) \left[ 1 - \frac{1.2 \times 0.20}{0.20 \times 40} \right]$$

$$= 5 \times \ln(2) \times \left[ 1 - \frac{0.24}{8.0} \right]$$

$$= 5 \times 0.6931 \times [1 - 0.03]$$

$$= 5 \times 0.6931 \times 0.97$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 3.36 \text{ days}}$$

**(iii) Critical deficit:**

$$D_c = \frac{k_1 L_0}{k_2} e^{-k_1 t_c} = \frac{0.20 \times 40}{0.40} \times e^{-0.20 \times 3.36}$$

$$= 20 \times e^{-0.672} = 20 \times 0.5106$$

$$\boxed{D_c = 10.21 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**(iv) Distance to critical point:**

$$x_c = u \times t_c = 25 \text{ km/day} \times 3.36 \text{ days}$$

$$\boxed{x_c = 84.0 \text{ km}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *COD + Aerobic Biofilm*

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

A 25 mL wastewater sample is refluxed with 10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇ in the COD test. After digestion, the excess dichromate requires 8.2 mL of 0.1 N FAS. Blank titration requires 24.0 mL of the same FAS. Calculate the COD. Also compute the BOD/COD ratio if BOD₅ is 180 mg/L and comment on biodegradability.

**Model Solution:**

**COD Calculation:**

$$\text{COD} = \frac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V_s}$$

$$= \frac{(24.0 - 8.2) \times 0.1 \times 8000}{25}$$

$$= \frac{15.8 \times 0.1 \times 8000}{25} = \frac{12640}{25}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COD} = 505.6 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**BOD/COD Ratio:**

$$\text{BOD/COD} = \frac{180}{505.6} = 0.356$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD/COD} = 0.36}$$

**Comment on Biodegradability:**
A BOD/COD ratio of 0.36 lies between 0.3 and 0.5, indicating **moderately biodegradable** wastewater. Biological treatment (e.g., activated sludge, biofilm reactors) is feasible but may require longer retention times. Effluent is suitable for biological treatment with appropriate design.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Explain the basic principle of aerobic biofilm processes. With a neat sketch, describe the structure of a biofilm and the role of each microbial layer."**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
Aerobic biofilm processes are attached-growth biological treatment systems where microorganisms grow as a **biological film** on an inert support medium (rocks, plastic, ceramic). Wastewater flows over the biofilm, transferring dissolved organics and oxygen to the microbial community. The outer aerobic layer oxidizes organics, while inner layers exhibit anoxic/anaerobic metabolism. Examples include **trickling filters, rotating biological contactors (RBC), and moving bed biofilm reactors (MBBR).**

**Biofilm Structure (with diagram):**

1. **Bulk Liquid Layer:** Wastewater containing dissolved organics and DO flows over the biofilm. Mass transfer occurs by diffusion through the liquid boundary layer.

2. **Aerobic Zone (Outer Layer, ~50–200 µm):**
   - Dominated by **aerobic heterotrophs** that oxidize organic matter (BOD) using molecular O₂.
   - **Nitrifying bacteria** (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter) oxidize ammonia → nitrite → nitrate.
   - DO concentration is moderate to high.

3. **Anoxic Zone (Middle Layer):**
   - DO is depleted; nitrate is used as the electron acceptor.
   - **Denitrifying bacteria** reduce NO₃⁻ → N₂ gas.
   - Important for nitrogen removal in combined systems.

4. **Anaerobic Zone (Innermost Layer, attached to media):**
   - Strict anaerobic bacteria ferment organics.
   - Production of H₂S, CH₄, organic acids.
   - Causes sloughing (detachment of old biofilm) when biofilm thickens beyond ~200–300 µm.

5. **Support Media:** Provides surface for attachment; materials include PVC, polyethylene, clay, rock, polyurethane foam.

**Significance of Layered Structure:**
The simultaneous presence of aerobic, anoxic, and anaerobic zones in a single biofilm enables **simultaneous carbon, nitrogen, and partial sulfur removal** — a major engineering advantage over suspended-growth systems like activated sludge, which require separate anoxic/anaerobic tanks.

(7 marks)

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to specify 5 days at 20 °C in BOD definition** → Lose 1 mark. Always include "5 days, 20 °C."
> 2. **Confusing BOD and COD oxidants** → BOD uses O₂ (biological), COD uses K₂Cr₂O₇ (chemical). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for interchange.
> 3. **Sign errors in Streeter-Phelps equation** → The sign inside the bracket is $(e^{-k_1 t} - e^{-k_2 t})$, NOT $(e^{-k_2 t} - e^{-k_1 t})$. Reversing gives negative deficit.
> 4. **Using base-10 k₁ instead of base-e** → KTU 2024 questions may use either, but state clearly which base. If k₁ is in base 10, convert: $k_1 (base\, e) = 2.303 \times k_1 (base\, 10)$.
> 5. **Forgetting to use the factor 8000 in COD calculation** → This represents milliequivalent weight of O₂ (8 g/eq × 1000 mg/g). Without it, units collapse.
> 6. **Not specifying catalysts in COD** → Always mention **Ag₂SO₄ (catalyst) and HgSO₄ (chloride mask)** — extra 1 mark.
> 7. **Drawing biofilm without labels** → KTU examiners want labeled sketches: bulk liquid, diffusion layer, aerobic, anoxic, anaerobic zones, and support media.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**BOD Dilution Method:**
- ☐ Definition: O₂ consumed by microbes in 5 days at 20 °C
- ☐ Formula: BOD₅ = (DO_i − DO_f) / P, where P = V_s / (V_s + V_d)
- ☐ Winkler titration or DO probe used
- ☐ Nitrification inhibitor (allylthiourea) used for carbonaceous BOD
- ☐ Valid test: residual DO ≥ 2 mg/L, depletion ≥ 2 mg/L
- ☐ k₁ typical: 0.1–0.3 day⁻¹ (base e) at 20 °C
- ☐ BOD₅ ≈ 0.68 × BOD_ultimate (for typical municipal WW)

**COD Measurement:**
- ☐ Oxidant: K₂Cr₂O₇ in 50% H₂SO₄, reflux 2 hours
- ☐ Catalyst: Ag₂SO₄; Chloride mask: HgSO₄
- ☐ Titrant: Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS); Indicator: Ferroin
- ☐ Formula: COD = [(A − B) × N × 8000] / V_s
- ☐ COD > BOD always; COD/BOD ratio indicates recalcitrance

**DO Sag Curve (Streeter-Phelps):**
- ☐ D_t = [k₁L₀/(k₂−k₁)](e^(−k₁t) − e^(−k₂t)) + D₀e^(−k₂t)
- ☐ Critical time: t_c = [1/(k₂−k₁)] ln(k₂/k₁) [1 − D₀(k₂−k₁)/(k₁L₀)]
- ☐ Critical deficit: D_c = (k₁L₀/k₂) e^(−k₁t_c)
- ☐ Three zones: degradation, recovery, clean water
- ☐ k₁ (deoxygenation) vs k₂ (reaeration) — k₂ > k₁ for self-purification

**BOD Removal Kinetics:**
- ☐ First-order: L_t = L₀ e^(−k₁t)
- ☐ BOD exerted: Y_t = L₀ (1 − e^(−k₁t))
- ☐ Ultimate BOD (L₀) is the asymptote
- ☐ k₁ base e: 0.1–0.3 day⁻¹; base 10: 0.05–0.10 day⁻¹

**Aerobic Biofilm:**
- ☐ Attached-growth system on inert support
- ☐ Stratified: aerobic (outer) → anoxic → anaerobic (inner)
- ☐ Biofilm thickness critical ~100–200 µm
- ☐ Examples: Trickling Filter, RBC, MBBR
- ☐ Sloughing controls biofilm thickness
- ☐ Mass transfer limitation via diffusion boundary layer

**Numericals — Always Remember:**
- ☐ 8000 = 8 × 1000 (milliequivalent weight of O₂ × unit conversion)
- ☐ 9.2 mg/L = DO saturation at 20 °C, 1 atm
- ☐ 5 days = standard BOD incubation
- ☐ 2 hours = standard COD reflux
- ☐ k₁ and k₂ always positive; check if given in base e or base 10
- ☐ t_c positive ⇒ k₂ > k₁ required for stable river recovery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: (1) Definition with formula, (2) Step-by-step numerical with units, (3) Engineering interpretation. Examiners reward the third component generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biofilm-based Bioreactors - Trickling Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biofilm-based Bioreactors - Trickling Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Trickling Filter (TF)** is a fixed-film, attached-growth biological wastewater treatment reactor in which wastewater is distributed over a bed of coarse, inert packing media (traditionally rocks, gravel, slag, or modern plastic media), allowing the liquid to *trickle* downward by gravity through the bed. As the wastewater percolates, it contacts a *biofilm* — a stratified microbial consortium (bacteria, protozoa, fungi, algae, worms) immobilized on the media surface — which oxidizes dissolved organic matter, nitrifies ammonia, and partially removes biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) before the effluent collects in an underdrain system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEBBT864, Module 3):** *Trickling filters are non-submerged attached-growth biofilm reactors in which biological oxidation of organic matter is achieved as wastewater flows over a fixed microbial film supported on a stationary packing medium, with oxygen supplied naturally by air convection through the void spaces.*

The system is supplemented by an effluent recirculation loop and a secondary clarifier to capture sloughed biomass (*humus sludge*). Trickling filters are classified by KTU syllabus into **Standard-rate**, **High-rate**, and **Roughing filters** based on hydraulic and organic loading rates.

## 1.2 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Standard Constants)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| BOD Removal Efficiency | **65 – 90 %** | % |
| Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR) | **1 – 200** | m³/m²·d |
| Organic Loading Rate (OLR) | **0.1 – 4.8** | kg BOD/m³·d |
| Recirculation Ratio (R) | **0.5 – 4.0** | dimensionless |
| Media depth | **1 – 3 m** | m |
| Plastic media specific surface area | **100 – 250** | m²/m³ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Rocky Waterfall in Your Backyard

Imagine a moss-covered garden waterfall: as water cascades down the rocks, the green moss (your *biofilm*) absorbs nutrients from the water, the falling droplets pick up fresh oxygen from the surrounding air, and any dead moss flakes get washed into a small pond below (your *secondary clarifier*). 

- **The rocks** = trickling filter media (surface for microbial attachment).
- **The falling water** = distributed wastewater (substrate supply).
- **The green moss** = aerobic biofilm (carbonaceous BOD oxidizers).
- **The droplets contacting air** = passive oxygen transfer (no aeration tank needed).
- **The pond at the bottom** = humus clarifier (separates sloughed biomass).
- **The dead moss flakes** = excess sludge (*humus*).

This analogy captures the **passive, aerobic, attached-growth** nature of the reactor — the cornerstone of all biofilm systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Trickling Filters Matter in Kerala (KTU Context):** Kerala's municipal corporations (Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, Kozhikode) operate several trickling filters at STP scale because the technology is energy-cheap (no blowers), tolerant of hydraulic shocks, and ideal for the moderate BOD loads typical of domestic sewage (200–400 mg/L).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

The trickling filter performs **aerobic biological oxidation** in a sequence of well-defined mass-transfer and biochemical steps. Below is the KTU-mapped logic:

### Step 1 — Wastewater Distribution
- Influent is pumped to a **rotary distributor arm** (motor-driven, multi-nozzle) or a fixed-nozzle system.
- The distributor rotates at **1 – 4 rpm**, ensuring uniform application over the entire media surface.

### Step 2 — Biofilm Attachment & Substrate Diffusion
- Organic substrates (glucose, proteins, fats) diffuse from the bulk liquid **into the biofilm** following Fick's Law.
- A concentration gradient forms: high at the liquid interface, low near the media surface.

### Step 3 — Biological Oxidation (Aerobic Respiration)
- Heterotrophic bacteria oxidize organics using dissolved oxygen:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 + 6\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$$
- Nitrification may occur in deeper, ammonia-rich zones once most carbonaceous BOD is removed.

### Step 4 — Oxygen Replenishment (Counter-Current Airflow)
- Ventilation is maintained by **natural draft** (chimney effect) plus temperature differential (warm effluent rises, cool air enters from underdrains).
- Oxygen transfer is *passive* — no mechanical aeration required.

### Step 5 — Sloughing & Effluent Collection
- As the biofilm thickens (typically > **100–200 µm**), anaerobic inner zones weaken attachment.
- Excess biomass (*sloughing*) falls into the underdrain system → flows to the secondary clarifier.

### Step 6 — Recirculation
- A portion of clarified effluent is returned to the distributor.
- **Recirculation ratio (R)** = Q_r / Q_influent; improves wetting, dilutes toxic shocks, and boosts oxygen transfer.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are **board-favorite** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on trickling filters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)} \quad & HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A} \;\; \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}\right] \\[8pt]
\textbf{2. Organic Loading Rate (OLR)} \quad & OLR = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} \;\; \left[\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{d}}\right] \\[8pt]
\textbf{3. Recirculation Ratio} \quad & R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q} \\[8pt]
\textbf{4. NRC Efficiency Equation} \quad & E_{20} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561\,(F/M)} \cdot 100 \,[\%] \\[8pt]
\textbf{5. Velz Formula (Modified)} \quad & \dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \exp\!\left[-k\,D\,(Q^{-n})\right] \\[8pt]
\textbf{6. Eckenfelder Equation} \quad & \dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k\,(D/Q)^{n}} \\[8pt]
\textbf{7. Temperature Correction} \quad & k_T = k_{20}\,\theta^{(T-20)} \quad \theta = 1.035 \\[8pt]
\textbf{8. Recirculated Loading Factor} \quad & F/M = \dfrac{(Q + Q_r)\,S_0}{V \cdot X} \\[8pt]
\textbf{9. Surface Loading (clarifier)} \quad & SLR = \dfrac{Q}{A_{clarifier}} \;\; \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}\right] \\[8pt]
\textbf{10. Oxygen Requirement} \quad & O_2 = 1.42 \cdot (S_0 - S_e) \cdot Q \;\; \left[\dfrac{\text{kg O}_2}{\text{d}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Variable Glossary (Quick Reference)

| Symbol | Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Influent flow rate | m³/d |
| $A$ | Plan area of filter bed | m² |
| $V$ | Volume of filter media (A·D) | m³ |
| $D$ | Depth of filter media | m |
| $S_0, S_e$ | Influent / Effluent BOD₅ | mg/L |
| $k$ | Treatability constant | depends on model |
| $n$ | Empirical exponent (typically 0.5–0.7) | dimensionless |
| $X$ | Active biomass concentration | mg/L |
| $\theta$ | Temperature correction factor | 1.035 (standard) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Board:** "**NRC = Nuclear Reactors Chase**" — the *NRC formula* is the **most commonly asked** efficiency equation (one-line, high-yield, worth memorizing verbatim).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost-Effective Secondary Treatment:** Trickling filters consume ~**50% less energy** than activated sludge (no blowers). Critical for Indian municipalities with carbon-budget constraints.
- **Operational Robustness:** Can withstand 4–6 hour power outages without biomass washout (biomass is *attached*).
- **Nitrification Capability:** Low-loaded filters are excellent tertiary nitrifiers — increasingly relevant for Kerala's *river-rejuvenation* projects (e.g., Periyar, Pampa action plans).
- **Sludge Reduction:** Biosolids production is **20–40 % lower** than activated sludge for the same BOD load, easing downstream sludge management.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)

**Starting from the definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Definition: } & HLR \equiv \dfrac{\text{Hydraulic flow applied per unit surface area}}{\text{of the filter per day}} \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad & HLR = \dfrac{Q}{A} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^3/\text{d}}{\text{m}^2} = \dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{d}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

For a circular filter of diameter $D_f$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi\,D_f^2}{4} \\[6pt]
HLR &= \dfrac{4Q}{\pi\,D_f^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters:** HLR controls the *wetting rate* of the biofilm. Below HLR ≈ **30 m³/m²·d**, the media dries and microbial activity stalls — a key design constraint emphasized in KTU Module 3.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Organic Loading Rate (OLR) and the F/M Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of BOD applied per day} &= Q \cdot S_0 \quad \text{(kg/d)} \\[6pt]
\text{Volume of reactor} &= V = A \cdot D \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad OLR &= \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A \cdot D} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{d}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

When recirculation is included, the effective F/M ratio becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F/M &= \dfrac{\text{Total substrate applied}}{\text{Mass of active biomass}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{(Q + Q_r) \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{(1 + R)\,Q\,S_0}{A \cdot D \cdot X}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Students often forget the $(1 + R)$ factor in the F/M ratio when recirculation exists. Always show it explicitly to score full marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NRC Efficiency Equation (National Research Council, USA)

The **NRC equation** assumes a **completely mixed biofilm reactor** at steady state. The substrate balance on the biofilm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(Substrate in)} - \text{(Substrate out)} - \text{(Substrate consumed)} &= 0 \\[4pt]
Q \cdot S_0 + Q_r \cdot S_e - (Q + Q_r)\,S_e - r_{su}\cdot V &= 0 \\[4pt]
\text{(since effluent of mixed reactor equals reactor substrate, } S_e) \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad r_{su}\cdot V &= Q\,(S_0 - S_e)
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming first-order biofilm kinetics, $r_{su} = k\,S_e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k\,S_e\,V &= Q\,(S_0 - S_e) \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad S_e\left[k\,V + Q\right] &= Q\,S_0 \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad S_e &= \dfrac{Q\,S_0}{Q + k\,V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **removal efficiency** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} = 1 - \dfrac{S_e}{S_0} \\[6pt]
  &= 1 - \dfrac{Q}{Q + k\,V} = \dfrac{k\,V}{Q + k\,V} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{Q}{k\,V}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha\,(F/M)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For standard-rate filters operating at 20 °C, the empirical constant $\alpha = 0.0561$ was determined experimentally by NRC:

$$
\boxed{E_{20} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561\,(F/M)}}
$$

**Temperature correction** for non-20 °C operation:

$$
E_T = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561\,(F/M)}{1.035^{\,(T-20)}}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Eckenfelder Plug-Flow Equation

This equation models the trickling filter as a *plug-flow biofilm reactor* (more accurate than NRC for high-rate filters).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Substrate balance over } dV: \quad & -Q\cdot dS = k \cdot S^n \cdot dV \\[6pt]
\text{For } n = 1 \text{ (first order):} \quad & -Q\dfrac{dS}{S} = k\,dV \\[6pt]
\text{Integrating from } S_0 \text{ to } S_e \text{ over } 0 \text{ to } V: \quad & -Q\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_e}{S_0}\right) = k\,V \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad & \ln\!\left(\dfrac{S_e}{S_0}\right) = -\dfrac{k\,V}{Q} \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad & \dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = e^{-k\,V/Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **generalized Eckenfelder form** with empirical exponent $n$:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k\left(\dfrac{D}{Q}\right)^{n}}}
$$

where $k$ and $n$ are empirical coefficients (often $n = 0.5$ for plastic media, $n = 0.67$ for rock media).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Model)

**Problem:** A trickling filter treats domestic wastewater with the following parameters:
- Influent flow: $Q = 5000$ m³/d
- Influent BOD₅: $S_0 = 250$ mg/L
- Filter diameter: $D_f = 20$ m
- Filter depth: $D = 2.5$ m
- Recirculation ratio: $R = 1.5$
- Temperature: $T = 25$ °C
- Active biomass: $X = 4000$ mg/L

**Find:** (a) HLR, (b) OLR, (c) F/M ratio, (d) Efficiency using NRC.

### Solution (a) — HLR

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi\,D_f^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi\,(20)^2}{4} = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \;\text{m}^2 \\[6pt]
HLR &= \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{5000}{314.16} \approx 15.92 \;\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution (b) — OLR

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= A \cdot D = 314.16 \times 2.5 = 785.4 \;\text{m}^3 \\[6pt]
Q\cdot S_0 &= 5000 \times 250 \times 10^{-3} = 1250 \;\text{kg BOD/d} \\[6pt]
OLR &= \dfrac{1250}{785.4} \approx 1.59 \;\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution (c) — F/M Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
F/M &= \dfrac{(1+R)\,Q\,S_0}{V \cdot X} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{(1+1.5) \times 1250}{785.4 \times 4.0} \quad \text{(X in kg/m}^3\text{)} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{3125}{3141.6} \approx 0.995 \;\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{kg biomass}\cdot\text{d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution (d) — NRC Efficiency at 25 °C

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{20} &= \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \times 0.995} = \dfrac{1}{1.0558} \approx 0.947 \\[6pt]
E_{25} &= \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561 \times 0.995}{1.035^{5}}} \\[6pt]
1.035^5 &\approx 1.188 \\[6pt]
E_{25} &= \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0559/1.188} = \dfrac{1}{1.0471} \approx 0.955
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\approx \mathbf{95.5\%}$ BOD removal — consistent with a well-designed high-rate trickling filter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Trickling Filter System

The diagram below maps all major process units, recycle streams, and gas-phase oxygen transfer pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Influent["INFLUENT WASTEWATER"] --> Pump["INFLUENT PUMP"]
    Pump --> DistArm["ROTARY DISTRIBUTOR ARM"]
    RecircReturn["RECIRCULATION LINE FROM CLARIFIER"] --> DistArm

    DistArm --> MediaBed["PACKED MEDIA BED WITH BIOFILM"]
    AtmoAir["AMBIENT AIR DRAFT"] --> MediaBed
    MediaBed --> UnderDrain["UNDERDRAIN SYSTEM"]

    UnderDrain --> HumusClarifier["HUMUS SECONDARY CLARIFIER"]
    HumusClarifier --> HumusSludge["HUMUS SLUDGE TO SLUDGE TREATMENT"]
    HumusClarifier --> Effluent["TREATED EFFLUENT DISCHARGE"]
    HumusClarifier --> RecircPump["RECIRCULATION PUMP"]
    RecircPump --> RecircReturn

    DistArm -.-> SprayNozzles["REVOLVING SPRAY NOZZLES"]
    MediaBed -.-> BiofilmLayer["STRATIFIED AEROBIC BIOFILM"]
    UnderDrain -.-> VentStack["VENT STACK CHIMNEY EFFECT"]
```

## 4.2 Process-Flow Logic Table

| Block ID | Function | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|
| Influent | Raw sewage inlet | Source of BOD, TSS, pathogens |
| Pump | Lifts wastewater to distributor | Overcomes hydraulic head |
| DistArm | Rotates to spread wastewater | Provides uniform media coverage |
| MediaBed | Houses attached biofilm | Site of biological oxidation |
| AtmoAir | Natural-draft ventilation | Supplies O₂ to biofilm |
| UnderDrain | Collects treated water | Channels to clarifier |
| HumusClarifier | Settles sloughed biomass | Polishes effluent + recycles |
| RecircPump | Returns clarified effluent | Maintains wetting rate |
| VentStack | Stack effect enhancer | Boosts passive aeration |

## 4.3 Biofilm Stratification — Cross-Sectional View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BulkLiq["BULK LIQUID FILM"] --> OuterAero["OUTER AEROBIC LAYER - Heterotrophs - Nitrifiers"]
    OuterAero --> MidFac["MIDDLE FACULTATIVE LAYER"]
    MidFac --> InnerAnaero["INNER ANAEROBIC LAYER"]
    InnerAnaero --> MediaSurf["MEDIA SURFACE - Inert Substrate"]

    Sub1["Substrate Diffusion - DO penetration ~ 100 to 200 microns"] -.-> OuterAero
    Sub2["Organic acids fermentation"] -.-> MidFac
    Sub3["Gas pockets form - Sloughing trigger"] -.-> InnerAnaero
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofilm Thickness Insight:** When biofilm exceeds **~ 200 µm**, the inner zones become anaerobic, gas (N₂, CO₂) accumulates, and a sloughing event occurs. This natural self-regulation prevents clogging and is exploited in design (no excess sludge handling at the surface).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a trickling filter. List any four advantages of trickling filters over the activated sludge process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A trickling filter is a **fixed-film, attached-growth biological wastewater treatment reactor** in which wastewater is distributed over a bed of inert packing media (rocks, plastic modules) supporting a microbial biofilm. Organic matter is oxidized as wastewater trickles down by gravity, while oxygen is supplied by natural air draft.

**Four advantages over activated sludge:**
1. **Lower energy consumption** — no aeration blowers required; passive air convection is sufficient.
2. **Lower sludge production** — biosolids yield is 20–40% less due to endogenous respiration in deeper biofilm.
3. **Operational stability** — biomass is attached, hence tolerant of hydraulic and toxic shock loads.
4. **Lower maintenance** — no diffused aerators, no return activated sludge (RAS) pumping complexity.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition = 1.5 marks; 4 advantages (0.375 each) = 1.5 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by the term "Recirculation Ratio (R)" in a trickling filter? State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Recirculation Ratio (R)** is defined as the ratio of the recirculated effluent flow to the fresh influent flow:

$$R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q}$$

**Significance:**
1. **Maintains minimum wetting rate** — prevents media drying between doses.
2. **Dilutes influent shocks** — smooths out peak BOD or pH variations.
3. **Improves oxygen transfer** — fresh, oxygen-rich effluent recontacts biofilm.
4. **Boosts contact time** — increases effective hydraulic residence without expanding filter volume.

Typical values range from **R = 0.5** (standard rate) to **R = 4.0** (high-rate).

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Mark Loss #1:** Writing only the *formula* without defining symbols. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if $Q$, $S_0$, $D$, $V$ are not explicitly defined.
> - **Mark Loss #2:** Using **NRC** for a high-rate filter or **Eckenfelder** for a standard-rate filter without justification. Always state the *assumptions* of the chosen model.
> - **Mark Loss #3:** Forgetting to apply the **temperature correction** $\theta^{(T-20)}$ when $T \neq 20$ °C. This is a frequent trap in numerical questions.
> - **Mark Loss #4:** In sludge-yield questions, failing to write the **stoichiometric equation** (e.g., C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O) — examiners expect both *carbon balance* and *O₂ balance*.

---

### **Question A — Part B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the components and working of a **standard-rate trickling filter** used for municipal wastewater treatment. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A standard-rate trickling filter has the following design data:
- Influent flow $Q = 4000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 300$ mg/L
- Filter diameter $D_f = 18$ m, depth $D = 2$ m
- Recirculation ratio $R = 0.5$
- Temperature $T = 28$ °C, $k_{20} = 0.20$ /d, $X = 5000$ mg/L

Calculate the **(i) HLR**, **(ii) OLR**, **(iii) F/M ratio**, and **(iv) BOD removal efficiency using the NRC equation at 28 °C**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Standard-Rate Trickling Filter**

A standard-rate trickling filter operates at low hydraulic and organic loading rates with **no effluent recirculation**. The major components are:

1. **Rotary Distributor Arm** — distributes influent evenly via 2–4 rotating nozzles; powered by hydraulic head reaction or electric motor.
2. **Filter Media (Rock/Slag/Plastic)** — provides surface for biofilm attachment; void ratio ~ 50–60% ensures air circulation.
3. **Underdrain System** — collects treated effluent and supports the media; acts as a plenum for ventilation.
4. **Vent Stack / Natural Draft** — chimney effect drives air upward through the bed.
5. **Secondary Clarifier (Humus Tank)** — settles sloughed biofilm; clarifies effluent before discharge.

**Working:** Wastewater enters the distributor → sprays uniformly → trickles down through biofilm-coated media → organics are oxidized → oxygen transfers via passive air draft → effluent + humus sludge flows to clarifier → sludge is removed → clarified effluent discharged.

(7 marks — diagram 3 + explanation 4)

#### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical**

**(i) Hydraulic Loading Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi\,(18)^2}{4} = 254.47 \;\text{m}^2 \\[6pt]
HLR &= \dfrac{4000}{254.47} \approx 15.72 \;\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{m}^2\cdot\text{d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Organic Loading Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 254.47 \times 2 = 508.94 \;\text{m}^3 \\[6pt]
Q \cdot S_0 &= 4000 \times 300 \times 10^{-3} = 1200 \;\text{kg BOD/d} \\[6pt]
OLR &= \dfrac{1200}{508.94} \approx 2.36 \;\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{m}^3\cdot\text{d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) F/M Ratio (with recirculation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F/M &= \dfrac{(1+0.5) \times 1200}{508.94 \times 5.0} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{1800}{2544.7} \approx 0.707 \;\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{kg}\cdot\text{d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) NRC Efficiency at 28 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{20} &= \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561 \times 0.707} = \dfrac{1}{1.0397} \approx 0.962 \\[6pt]
1.035^{(28-20)} &= 1.035^8 \approx 1.319 \\[6pt]
E_{28} &= \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.0561 \times 0.707}{1.319}} \\[6pt]
      &= \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0301} = \dfrac{1}{1.0301} \approx 0.971
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** **(i)** $HLR \approx 15.72$ m³/m²·d, **(ii)** $OLR \approx 2.36$ kg BOD/m³·d, **(iii)** $F/M \approx 0.707$, **(iv)** $E_{28} \approx 97.1\%$

---

### **Question B — Part B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Alternate Module Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Eckenfelder model** for trickling filter design. Compare it with the **NRC model** and state when each is applicable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plastic-media trickling filter treats industrial wastewater. Given $Q = 8000$ m³/d, $S_0 = 400$ mg/L, $V = 1000$ m³, $T = 30$ °C, $k_{20} = 0.25$ /d, $n = 0.5$, $D_f = 22$ m, $D = 2.5$ m.

Compute: **(i) OLR**, **(ii) Effluent BOD $S_e$** using the generalized Eckenfelder equation with temperature correction, and **(iii) Percentage BOD removal**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — Eckenfelder vs NRC**

**Eckenfelder Model:**

$$
\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} = \dfrac{1}{1 + k\left(\dfrac{D}{Q}\right)^{n}}
$$

- Treats filter as **plug-flow** biofilm reactor with first-order kinetics.
- Empirical constants $k, n$ depend on media type.
- Most accurate for **high-rate** filters and plastic media.

**NRC Model:**

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561\,(F/M)}
$$

- Treats filter as **completely mixed** reactor.
- Empirical constant $0.0561$ derived for **standard-rate rock filters** at 20 °C.
- Better for low-rate domestic wastewater.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Eckenfelder | NRC |
|---|---|---|
| Flow regime | Plug-flow | Completely mixed |
| Best suited for | High-rate filters | Standard-rate filters |
| Media type | Plastic / high surface area | Rock / slag |
| Empirical constant | $k$, $n$ (media-specific) | $0.0561$ (universal) |
| Recirculation handling | Built-in via $D/Q$ | External via $F/M$ |

(7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical**

**(i) Organic Loading Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \cdot S_0 &= 8000 \times 400 \times 10^{-3} = 3200 \;\text{kg BOD/d} \\[6pt]
OLR &= \dfrac{3200}{1000} = 3.20 \;\dfrac{\text{kg BOD}}{\text{m}^3\cdot\text{d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Effluent BOD at 30 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{30} &= k_{20} \cdot 1.035^{(30-20)} = 0.25 \times 1.035^{10} \\[6pt]
1.035^{10} &\approx 1.411 \\[6pt]
k_{30} &\approx 0.25 \times 1.411 = 0.353 \;\text{/d}
\end{aligned}
$$

For Eckenfelder with $n = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{\pi\,(22)^2}{4} = 380.13 \;\text{m}^2 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{D}{Q} &= \dfrac{2.5}{8000} = 3.125 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{d/m}^2 \\[6pt]
\left(\dfrac{D}{Q}\right)^{0.5} &= \sqrt{3.125 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01768 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{S_e}{S_0} &= \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.353 \times 0.01768} = \dfrac{1}{1.00624} \approx 0.9938 \\[6pt]
S_e &= 0.9938 \times 400 \approx 397.5 \;\text{mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage BOD Removal:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E\% &= \left(1 - \dfrac{S_e}{S_0}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.9938) \times 100 \approx 0.62\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** This *very low* efficiency indicates that the filter is **severely overloaded** (OLR = 3.2 kg BOD/m³·d is too high for plastic-media at this flow). In practice, the design would require **increased media volume or reduced flow** to achieve acceptable BOD removal.

> **Final Answers:** **(i)** $OLR = 3.20$ kg BOD/m³·d, **(ii)** $S_e \approx 397.5$ mg/L, **(iii)** $E \approx 0.62\%$ (filter overloaded — redesign needed)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3)**

- ✅ A **trickling filter** is an **attached-growth, non-submerged** biofilm reactor — *not* a suspended-growth system.
- ✅ The **biofilm** is the active phase; **media** is merely a *support structure*.
- ✅ **Three classifications:** Standard-rate (low load), High-rate (with recirculation), Roughing (very high load, partial BOD removal only).
- ✅ **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR)** = $Q/A$ in m³/m²·d.
- ✅ **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)** = $Q \cdot S_0 / V$ in kg BOD/m³·d.
- ✅ **Recirculation Ratio (R)** = $Q_r / Q$; essential for high-rate filters.
- ✅ **NRC equation:** $E = 1 / (1 + 0.0561 \cdot F/M)$ — for *standard-rate* completely-mixed assumption.
- ✅ **Eckenfelder equation:** $S_e/S_0 = 1/(1 + k(D/Q)^n)$ — for *high-rate plug-flow* assumption.
- ✅ **Temperature correction factor:** $\theta = 1.035$ for $k$; always apply when $T \neq 20$ °C.
- ✅ **Sloughing** occurs when biofilm thickness > 200 µm; natural self-regulation.
- ✅ **Oxygen transfer** is *passive* — via natural draft / vent stack; no blower needed.
- ✅ **Advantages:** low energy, low sludge, shock-tolerant.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Odor nuisance from rotating distributor, large land footprint, risk of filter flies (*Psychoda*).
- ✅ **Effluent polishing:** Always followed by a **secondary clarifier** for humus removal.
- ✅ **Design equation you MUST memorize:** $\boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.0561(F/M)}}$ — KTU board classic.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Trickling Filters**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activated Sludge Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activated Sludge Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activated Sludge Process (ASP):**
> The Activated Sludge Process is a **biological wastewater treatment process** in which a mixture of wastewater and a concentrated suspension of **heterotrophic microorganisms** (the *activated sludge*) is aerated to biologically degrade dissolved organic matter (measured as BOD/COD) into simpler end-products such as CO₂, H₂O, and new microbial biomass.

The term *"activated"* refers to the sludge being **enriched, aerated, and acclimatized** with a thriving microbial consortium capable of oxidizing organic pollutants in the presence of dissolved oxygen (DO). The process is operated under **aerobic conditions** and is the most widely used **secondary treatment** process in municipal sewage treatment plants (STPs) worldwide.

### Key Components of an ASP

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Aeration Tank (Reactor)** | Where biological oxidation of organic matter occurs |
| **Aeration System** | Supplies O₂ and keeps sludge in suspension |
| **Secondary Clarifier** | Separates biomass from treated effluent |
| **Return Activated Sludge (RAS)** | Recycled biomass back to aeration tank |
| **Waste Activated Sludge (WAS)** | Excess sludge removed for disposal |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Imagine a busy cafeteria..." 🍽️**
> Think of the aeration tank as a giant, oxygen-filled cafeteria:
> - The **food (organic waste/BOD)** arrives continuously through one door.
> - The **diners (microorganisms)** sit at tables and consume the food.
> - **Air bubbles** from diffusers act like waiters constantly serving fresh oxygen so the diners keep eating.
> - The **used plates (new biomass)** and undigested leftovers are sent to the **secondary clarifier**, where they settle out as **activated sludge**.
> - Most of this sludge is **recycled back** to the cafeteria (RAS) so the same microbial community can keep working, while a small portion (**WAS**) is discarded.
> - The clean water (supernatant) flows out the other side as **treated effluent**.

This continuous "eat, settle, recycle" cycle is the heart of the Activated Sludge Process.

---

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics (Bolded Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Standard Metrics in ASP:**
> - **MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids):** 1500 – 4000 mg/L *(typical range)*
> - **F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism):** 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹ *(conventional)*
> - **SVI (Sludge Volume Index):** 50 – 150 mL/g *(good settling)*
> - **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time):** 4 – 8 hours
> - **SRT (Mean Cell Residence Time):** 5 – 15 days
> - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** 1.5 – 2.5 mg/L

These are the *vital signs* a KTU examiner expects every ASP student to know by heart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Flow — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

The ASP operates as a continuous-flow system with five sequential operations:

**Step 1 — Primary Treatment (Pre-treatment):**
Raw sewage first passes through screens, grit chambers, and primary clarifiers to remove settleable solids and floating debris. The **primary effluent** (with most suspended solids removed) is the feed to the ASP.

**Step 2 — Aeration / Biological Reaction:**
Primary effluent enters the **aeration tank**, where it mixes with **return activated sludge (RAS)**. Compressed air is diffused through fine bubble or coarse bubble diffusers, supplying **dissolved oxygen (DO ≥ 1.5 mg/L)** and maintaining complete mixing.

- Microbes oxidize organic matter:
  $$\text{Organic Matter (BOD)} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells}$$
- Microbes also undergo **endogenous respiration** (self-digestion) under prolonged aeration.

**Step 3 — Solid-Liquid Separation (Secondary Clarification):**
The **mixed liquor** (effluent + biomass) flows to the **secondary clarifier**, where biomass settles under gravity as **activated sludge**, producing a clarified supernatant as the final effluent.

**Step 4 — Sludge Recirculation:**
A portion of settled sludge (typically 25–75% of inflow) is pumped back to the aeration tank as **Return Activated Sludge (RAS)** to maintain a desired **MLSS concentration**.

**Step 5 — Waste Sludge Disposal:**
Excess sludge (**WAS**) is withdrawn to maintain the required **SRT** and is sent to sludge digestion/thickening units for further treatment.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Design Decision

- **Why recirculate sludge?** → To maintain a **high microbial population** in the aeration tank independent of the hydraulic flow rate. Without RAS, microbes would wash out faster than they can grow.
- **Why control DO at 1.5–2.5 mg/L?** → Below 1.5 mg/L, aerobic microbes become stressed (filamentous bulking risk); above 3 mg/L, energy is wasted.
- **Why measure F/M ratio?** → It dictates whether the system operates as **high-rate (F/M high)**, **conventional (F/M ~0.3)**, or **extended aeration (F/M low)**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and frequently tested in KTU ESE.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **F/M Ratio** | $\dfrac{F}{M} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | 0.05 – 0.5 |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours | 4 – 8 |
| 3 | **Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}$ | days | 5 – 30 |
| 4 | **Sludge Recirculation Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q}$ | dimensionless | 0.25 – 1.0 |
| 5 | **Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** | $\text{SVI} = \dfrac{V_{30} \times 1000}{\text{MLSS}}$ | mL/g | 50 – 150 |
| 6 | **Volumetric BOD Loading** | $\text{BLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | kg BOD/m³·d | 0.3 – 0.6 |
| 7 | **Specific Substrate Utilization (U)** | $U = \dfrac{Q(S_0 - S)}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | 0.05 – 0.3 |
| 8 | **Observed Yield (Y_obs)** | $Y_{obs} = \dfrac{Y}{1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c}$ | mg VSS/mg BOD | 0.2 – 0.4 |

### Variable Definitions (Refer throughout)

- $Q$ = Influent flow rate (m³/day)
- $Q_r$ = Return sludge flow rate (m³/day)
- $Q_w$ = Waste sludge flow rate (m³/day)
- $Q_e$ = Effluent flow rate (m³/day)
- $S_0$ = Influent substrate (BOD) concentration (mg/L)
- $S$ = Effluent substrate (BOD) concentration (mg/L)
- $V$ = Aeration tank volume (m³)
- $X$ = MLSS concentration (mg/L)
- $X_w$ = Waste sludge concentration (mg/L)
- $X_e$ = Effluent suspended solids (mg/L)
- $V_{30}$ = Volume of settled sludge in 30 minutes (mL)
- $Y$ = True yield coefficient
- $k_d$ = Endogenous decay coefficient (day⁻¹)
- $k$ = Maximum specific substrate utilization rate (day⁻¹)
- $K_s$ = Half-velocity constant (mg/L)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ASP is the **backbone of municipal wastewater treatment** for cities across the globe. It is also adapted for treating **industrial effluents** from food processing, pharmaceuticals, textiles, and petrochemicals. Advanced modifications (e.g., **SBR, MBR, MBBR**) build directly on ASP principles — making mastery of this topic essential for careers in **environmental consultancy, public health engineering, and STPs operation**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between X, θ, θ_c and Recirculation Ratio (R)

This is the **most important KTU derivation** for the ASP topic.

### Step 1 — Mass Balance on Biomass in the Aeration Tank (Steady-State)

At steady state, the accumulation of biomass in the aeration tank equals growth minus losses.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Accumulation} &= \text{Growth} - \text{Loss (effluent + wastage)} \\
0 &= Y \cdot Q(S_0 - S) - k_d \cdot V \cdot X - Q_w \cdot X_w - Q_e \cdot X_e
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $Y \cdot Q(S_0 - S)$ is biomass synthesized per unit time from substrate removed; $k_d V X$ is endogenous decay; and the last two terms represent biomass leaving via WAS and effluent respectively.

### Step 2 — Mass Balance on Biomass Considering the Entire System

Total biomass in the system = biomass leaving (wasted + effluent).

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dX_{total}}{dt} &= 0 = Y \cdot Q(S_0 - S) - k_d \cdot V \cdot X - Q_w \cdot X_w - Q_e \cdot X_e \\
\therefore \theta_c &= \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_w + Q_e \cdot X_e}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Solving for the inverse gives the **Mean Cell Residence Time (SRT)** — the average time a microbe spends in the system.

### Step 3 — Assume Sludge Wastage from Return Line (Standard Simplification)

If WAS is taken from the return sludge line ($X_w = X_r$), and effluent SS is negligible ($X_e \approx 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_c &= \frac{V \cdot X}{Q_w \cdot X_r} \\
\Rightarrow Q_w &= \frac{V \cdot X}{\theta_c \cdot X_r}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Solids Mass Balance Around the Secondary Clarifier

The sludge entering the clarifier = sludge leaving (RAS + WAS) + effluent solids.

$$\begin{aligned}
(Q + Q_r) \cdot X &= (Q_r + Q_w) \cdot X_r + Q \cdot X_e \\
(Q + Q_r) \cdot X &= (Q_r + Q_w) \cdot X_r \quad (\text{if } X_e \approx 0)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Recirculation Ratio (R)

Dividing throughout by $Q$:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1 + R) \cdot X &= (R + Q_w/Q) \cdot X_r \\
\text{Substituting } Q_w &= \frac{V \cdot X}{\theta_c \cdot X_r}: \\
(1 + R) \cdot X &= R \cdot X_r + \frac{V \cdot X}{\theta_c \cdot X_r} \cdot \frac{X_r}{Q}
\end{aligned}$$

After simplification:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\theta_c} &= \frac{Q}{V} \cdot \left[\frac{(1+R) X - R X_r}{X \cdot X_r}\right] \cdot X_r \quad \text{...} \\
\Rightarrow R &= \frac{X}{\theta_c \cdot (Q/V) \cdot (X_r - X)} - 1
\end{aligned}$$

Or equivalently, the **classical relation**:

$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{X}{\theta_c \cdot (1/\theta) \cdot (X_r - X)} = \frac{X \cdot \theta}{X_r - X} \cdot \frac{1}{\theta_c}\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This key result shows that the recirculation ratio $R$ is controlled by the desired MLSS ($X$), the desired SRT ($\theta_c$), the hydraulic retention time ($\theta$), and the underflow concentration $X_r$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effluent Substrate Concentration (S) Using Monod Kinetics

For a **complete-mix reactor** at steady state:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} - \frac{Q \cdot S}{V} - \frac{k \cdot X \cdot S}{K_s + S} &= 0 \\
\frac{S_0 - S}{\theta} &= \frac{k \cdot X \cdot S}{K_s + S}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Substrate mass in − substrate mass out − substrate consumed by biomass = 0 (steady state).

Rearranging and applying $\theta_c = \theta \cdot Y \cdot U / (1 + k_d \theta_c)$ relationships:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\theta_c} &= \frac{Y \cdot k \cdot S}{K_s + S} - k_d \\
\Rightarrow S &= \frac{K_s \cdot (1 + k_d \cdot \theta_c)}{\theta_c \cdot (Y \cdot k - k_d) - 1}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the famous **design equation** predicting effluent BOD based on kinetic parameters and SRT.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A wastewater treatment plant has an influent flow of **20,000 m³/day** with **BOD₅ = 250 mg/L**. The aeration tank has a volume of **4000 m³** and an **MLSS = 3000 mg/L**. Calculate:
> (a) Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)
> (b) F/M Ratio
> (c) Volumetric BOD Loading

### (a) Hydraulic Retention Time (θ)

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{V}{Q} = \frac{4000 \text{ m}^3}{20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}} \\
\theta &= 0.2 \text{ day} = 4.8 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Dividing volume by flow gives the average time fluid spends in the reactor.

### (b) F/M Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{F}{M} &= \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 250}{4000 \times 3000} \\
\frac{F}{M} &= \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{12{,}000{,}000} = 0.417 \text{ day}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Numerator is the daily BOD mass load; denominator is the total microbial mass in the tank.

### (c) Volumetric BOD Loading

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BLR} &= \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 250}{4000 \times 1000} \\
\text{BLR} &= 1.25 \text{ kg BOD/m}^3\cdot\text{day}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Converting mg to kg (×10⁻⁶) and m³/day gives kg/m³·day.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Activated Sludge Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[node1 Raw Sewage Influent] --> B[node2 Primary Clarifier]
    B --> C[node3 Primary Effluent]
    C --> D[node4 Aeration Tank with Diffused Air Supply]
    E[node5 Air Blower Unit] --> D
    D --> F[node6 Mixed Liquor Flow to Secondary Clarifier]
    F --> G[node7 Secondary Clarifier Settling Zone]
    G --> H[node8 Clarified Effluent to Disinfection]
    G --> I[node9 Return Activated Sludge Pump]
    I --> D
    G --> J[node10 Waste Activated Sludge to Sludge Handling]
    H --> K[node11 Treated Effluent Discharge]
    J --> L[node12 Sludge Thickening and Digestion]
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram traces the **mass flow** of the wastewater stream (left to right) and the **biomass recycling loop** (clarifier → RAS pump → aeration tank). Air supply from the blower unit sustains aerobic conditions. WAS is diverted for sludge processing, and clarified effluent proceeds to disinfection.

---

## 4.2 Microbial Ecology Within the Activated Sludge Floc

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[nodeA Floc Forming Bacteria Zooglea ramigera] --> N[nodeB Substrate Removal BOD Oxidation]
    N --> O[nodeC Filamentous Bacteria Microthrix parvicella]
    O --> P[nodeD Protozoa Vorticella Ciliates]
    P --> Q[nodeE Rotifers and Nematodes Higher Organisms]
    Q --> R[nodeF Stable Effluent and Good Sludge Settling]
    M --> S[nodeG Excess Sludge Production]
    S --> T[nodeH WAS Wasting and Digestion]
```

> **Interpretation:** A healthy ASP floc consists of a **microbiological food chain** — bacteria oxidize organics, filamentous organisms provide floc structure, protozoa consume free bacteria polishing the effluent, and rotifers/nematodes serve as bioindicators of treatment stability.

---

## 4.3 Process Modification Variants (Text-Based Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE[nodeX Conventional Plug Flow ASP] --> MOD1[nodeY Complete Mix Reactor]
    BASE --> MOD2[nodeZ Extended Aeration Oxidation Ditch]
    BASE --> MOD3[nodeAA Sequencing Batch Reactor SBR]
    BASE --> MOD4[nodeAB Membrane Bioreactor MBR]
    BASE --> MOD5[nodeAC Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor MBBR]
```

> **Interpretation:** All variants originate from **Conventional ASP** but differ in hydraulic regime, biomass retention mechanism, or aeration strategy. KTU often asks to compare any two of these variants.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Define MLSS and F/M Ratio. State their typical values in a conventional activated sludge process.

**Model Answer:**

**MLSS (Mixed Liquor Suspended Solids):** MLSS is the concentration of suspended solids (mostly microbial biomass) in the mixed liquor of the aeration tank. It is measured in **mg/L** and typically ranges from **1500 – 4000 mg/L** in conventional systems. MLSS represents the active biological mass responsible for substrate degradation.

**F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism Ratio):** It is the ratio of the daily BOD load applied to the aeration tank to the total mass of microorganisms present:

$$\frac{F}{M} = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$$

Its unit is **day⁻¹**, and the typical value for conventional ASP is **0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹**. A high F/M indicates a *high-rate* system (poor effluent, excess sludge), while a low F/M indicates *extended aeration* (good effluent, less sludge).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` What is SVI? What does a high SVI indicate?

**Model Answer:**

**Sludge Volume Index (SVI)** is defined as the volume in millilitres occupied by **1 gram** of activated sludge after settling for 30 minutes in a 1-litre graduated cylinder. Mathematically:

$$\text{SVI} = \frac{V_{30} \times 1000}{\text{MLSS}} \;\; (\text{mL/g})$$

A good settling sludge has SVI between **50 – 150 mL/g**.

**Significance of High SVI (> 200 mL/g):** A high SVI indicates **poor sludge settleability**, typically caused by:
- **Filamentous bulking** (excessive growth of filamentous bacteria)
- Low DO, low F/M ratio, or nutrient deficiency (N or P)
- Presence of toxic substances

This leads to sludge washout from the secondary clarifier and poor effluent quality.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

### `Question A (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Describe the Activated Sludge Process with a neat flow diagram. Explain the various design parameters affecting its performance." (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Process Description with Flow Diagram (7 Marks)

The **Activated Sludge Process** is a suspended-growth biological treatment process used as secondary treatment of municipal sewage. The process consists of the following stages:

1. **Primary Treatment:** Raw sewage passes through screens, grit chambers, and primary clarifiers to remove settleable solids. The clarified primary effluent becomes the feed.

2. **Aeration:** The primary effluent enters an **aeration tank** where it is mixed with **return activated sludge (RAS)**. Compressed air supplied through diffusers maintains **DO at 1.5 – 2.5 mg/L** and keeps the contents in suspension. Heterotrophic microorganisms oxidize dissolved organic matter:

$$\text{Organic Matter} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{New Cells}$$

3. **Secondary Clarification:** The mixed liquor flows to a **secondary clarifier** where biomass settles by gravity. The supernatant is the treated effluent.

4. **Sludge Recirculation:** Settled sludge is recycled to the aeration tank (RAS) at a recirculation ratio $R = 0.25 - 1.0$.

5. **Waste Sludge Disposal:** Excess sludge (**WAS**) is withdrawn and sent for sludge treatment.

**Flow Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[node1 Raw Sewage] --> B[node2 Primary Clarifier]
    B --> C[node3 Aeration Tank]
    C --> D[node4 Secondary Clarifier]
    D --> E[node5 Treated Effluent]
    D --> F[node6 RAS Pump]
    F --> C
    D --> G[node7 WAS to Sludge Treatment]
```

#### (b) Design Parameters (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| MLSS | $X$ | 1500 – 4000 mg/L |
| F/M Ratio | $F/M$ | 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹ |
| HRT | $\theta$ | 4 – 8 hours |
| SRT | $\theta_c$ | 5 – 15 days |
| SVI | — | 50 – 150 mL/g |
| Recirculation Ratio | $R$ | 0.25 – 1.0 |
| DO | — | 1.5 – 2.5 mg/L |
| BOD Loading | — | 0.3 – 0.6 kg/m³·d |

**Effect on Performance:**
- **High F/M** → Poor BOD removal, excess sludge, poor nitrification.
- **Low F/M** → Better effluent quality, low sludge production, possible pinpoint floc.
- **High SRT** → Complete nitrification, less sludge.
- **Low DO** → Filamentous growth, bulking, poor settling.

---

### `Question B (14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Explain the different types of modifications of the Activated Sludge Process. Compare Conventional ASP with Extended Aeration." (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Types of ASP Modifications (7 Marks)

| Type | Key Feature |
|---|---|
| **Conventional (Plug Flow)** | Long narrow tank; F/M ~ 0.3; HRT 6 h |
| **Complete Mix** | Tank completely mixed; tolerates shock loads |
| **Extended Aeration** | No primary; F/M < 0.1; SRT 20–30 days |
| **Sequential Batch Reactor (SBR)** | Time-based batch operation in single tank |
| **Membrane Bioreactor (MBR)** | Membranes replace clarifier; high MLSS |
| **Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR)** | Plastic carriers with attached biofilm |
| **Oxidation Ditch** | Closed-loop channel; low energy |
| **Contact Stabilization** | Two-stage; rapid substrate uptake followed by re-aeration |

#### (b) Conventional vs Extended Aeration (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Conventional ASP | Extended Aeration |
|---|---|---|
| **F/M Ratio** | 0.2 – 0.5 day⁻¹ | 0.05 – 0.1 day⁻¹ |
| **HRT** | 4 – 8 h | 18 – 36 h |
| **SRT** | 5 – 15 days | 20 – 40 days |
| **MLSS** | 1500 – 3000 mg/L | 3000 – 6000 mg/L |
| **Primary Clarifier** | Required | Usually omitted |
| **Sludge Production** | High | Very low |
| **Effluent Quality** | Good (BOD ~20 mg/L) | Excellent (BOD <10 mg/L) |
| **Power Consumption** | Moderate | High (longer aeration) |
| **Nitrification** | Partial | Almost complete |
| **Footprint** | Moderate | Large |
| **Best For** | Medium–large STPs | Small communities, package plants |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in ASP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing HRT and SRT** — HRT = hydraulic (volume/flow); SRT = biological (solids residence). Examiners dock **2–3 marks** if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting units in F/M** — Always express F/M in **day⁻¹**, not dimensionless.
> 3. **Drawing incomplete flow diagrams** — Missing the **RAS loop** or **WAS line** results in **loss of 2 marks** in sketch questions.
> 4. **Wrong SVI formula** — Always include the factor of 1000 when MLSS is in mg/L.
> 5. **Ignoring DO range** — Most students write only "high DO"; you must specify **1.5–2.5 mg/L**.
> 6. **Incomplete comparison tables** — When comparing variants, both **qualitative and quantitative** parameters must be tabulated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ASP:**

- **ASP = Suspended-growth aerobic biological treatment**; microbes oxidize BOD in presence of DO.
- **Core equipment:** Aeration tank + Secondary clarifier + RAS pump + Air blower.
- **MLSS** = biomass in aeration tank (1500–4000 mg/L).
- **F/M Ratio** = $Q S_0 / (V X)$; controls sludge age and effluent quality.
- **SRT (θc)** = $V X / (Q_w X_w + Q_e X_e)$; biological residence time.
- **HRT (θ)** = $V / Q$; hydraulic residence time.
- **SVI** = $V_{30} \times 1000 / \text{MLSS}$; < 150 = good settling.
- **Recirculation Ratio (R)** = $Q_r / Q$; keeps biomass in the system.
- **DO** must be maintained at **1.5–2.5 mg/L**; below 1 mg/L → bulking.
- **Conventional ASP:** F/M = 0.3, SRT = 5–15 d.
- **Extended Aeration:** F/M = 0.05–0.1, SRT = 20–40 d, no primary, low sludge.
- **SBR, MBR, MBBR** are modern modifications with compact footprint.
- **Filamentous bulking** causes poor settling (high SVI) → controlled by F/M, DO, nutrients.
- **Nitrification** requires SRT > 7 days at 20 °C.
- **Effluent BOD equation:** $S = K_s(1 + k_d \theta_c)/(\theta_c(Yk - k_d) - 1)$.
- **Observed yield:** $Y_{obs} = Y/(1 + k_d \theta_c)$ — lower at high SRT.
- **Energy consumption** in ASP is dominated by **aeration** (~50–60% of plant power).
- **Microbial consortium** = bacteria + protozoa + rotifers; bioindicators of health.
- **WAS** must be wasted to maintain constant SRT; otherwise biomass accumulates.
- **KTU Favourite Questions:** Sketch+describe (14 marks), Compare variants (14 marks), F/M & SRT numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
