topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rotating Biological Contactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Rotating Biological Contactors (RBC)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under the **Biological Wastewater Treatment – Attached Growth Systems** unit. It is a high-weightage topic for ESE (End Semester Examination) Part B questions (14 marks) and frequently appears in Part A (3-mark definition questions).

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC)** is a secondary biological wastewater treatment device in which a population of microorganisms is attached to an arrangement of rotating discs (the *biomass support media*) that are partially immersed in a flowing wastewater stream. As the discs rotate, the attached biofilm is alternately exposed to the wastewater (for substrate absorption) and to the atmosphere (for oxygen replenishment), enabling **aerobic biological degradation** of organic matter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict KTU Terminology:** The KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education) classifies RBC under **"Attached-Growth / Fixed-Film Bioreactors"**, as opposed to Activated Sludge Process (ASP) which is a *Suspended-Growth* system. The thin microbial layer on the discs is called the **biofilm**, and its thickness typically ranges from **50 µm to 4 mm** depending on substrate concentration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rotating Window-Washer"

Imagine a series of large flat circular plates (discs) mounted on a horizontal shaft, like a giant rotating window-washer brush:

- When the plate dips into the dirty water (wastewater), it **"drinks"** the pollutants — the biofilm absorbs organic matter (BOD).
- When the plate spins out into the air, the biofilm **"breathes"** — oxygen is absorbed, just as a fish gulps air.
- The rotation is **slow (1–2 rpm)** so the gentle motion does not slough off the microbial layer.
- Excess biomass peels off naturally (sloughing) and is carried away for sedimentation in a clarifier.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is it called a "Contactor"?** Because the rotating disc *contacts* two phases alternately: the **liquid phase** (wastewater) and the **gaseous phase** (atmospheric air). This dual contact is the *defining feature* that distinguishes an RBC from a Trickling Filter.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Exam Value) |
|---|---|
| Disc rotation speed | **1.0 – 1.6 rpm** |
| Disc diameter | **1.0 – 3.6 m** |
| Disc spacing | **20 – 40 mm** |
| Submergence | **40 %** of disc area |
| Biofilm thickness | **0.05 – 4.0 mm** |
| BOD removal efficiency | **80 – 95 %** |
| Hydraulic retention time (HRT) | **0.7 – 2.0 hours** |
| Typical MLSS in secondary clarifier | **4,000 – 8,000 mg/L** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The RBC operates on a cyclic **three-stage mechanism** for every fraction of a revolution:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Submerged Contact (Substrate Uptake):**
> As the disc rotates, approximately 40 % of its surface area dips into the wastewater trough. The biofilm (aerobic + facultative microbes) absorbs dissolved organic substrates. The mass transfer follows **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 — Emerging Phase (Aeration / Oxygen Absorption):**
> The remaining ~60 % of the disc emerges into the air. The thin water film clinging to the disc picks up atmospheric oxygen, supplying the **electron acceptor** for microbial respiration (BOD oxidation: $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 — Sloughing (Biomass Detachment):**
> As biofilm thickness exceeds ~4 mm, internal anaerobic conditions weaken the film attachment, and the shear from rotation causes excess biomass to **slough off**. The detached flocs flow into a **secondary clarifier** for separation.

## 2.2 Constructional Components

- **Shaft (Rotor):** A horizontal mild-steel shaft, typically 5–8 m long, supported by bearings.
- **Discs (Media):** Corrugated or flat high-density polyethylene (HDPE) / polystyrene discs, with effective surface area per disc ranging from **5 m² to 25 m²**.
- **Wastewater Trough (Stator):** A semi-cylindrical tank holding the wastewater in which the lower portion of discs rotates.
- **Drive Mechanism:** A motor-gearbox assembly maintaining **1.0 – 1.6 rpm**.
- **Enclosure (Optional):** A fiberglass cover to suppress aerosol release and odor in cold climates.
- **Settlement Tank:** Downstream of the RBC for sludge separation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for KTU Numerical Problems (Module-3 ESE).** Master these six equations — they cover **> 90 %** of all RBC numerical questions asked in KTU university exams.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = N \cdot \pi \cdot D^2$ | Total disc surface area (one side) | m² |
| 2 | $A_s = 2 \cdot N \cdot \pi \cdot D^2$ | Effective wetted area (both sides) | m² |
| 3 | $\text{HLR} = \dfrac{Q}{A_s}$ | Hydraulic Loading Rate | m³/m²·d |
| 4 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{A_s}$ | Organic Loading Rate | g BOD/m²·d |
| 5 | $E_{\text{BOD}} = \dfrac{S_0 - S_e}{S_0} \times 100$ | BOD Removal Efficiency | % |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{V_{\text{disc}} \cdot \pi \cdot D}{60}$ | Peripheral (Tip) Velocity of disc edge | m/min |

Where:
- $N$ = number of discs
- $D$ = disc diameter (m)
- $Q$ = wastewater flow rate (m³/d)
- $S_0$ = influent BOD (mg/L)
- $S_e$ = effluent BOD (mg/L)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Unit Conversion Trap (Board Exam Pitfall):**
> $1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3$. When computing OLR, ensure **$Q$ is in m³/d and $S_0$ is converted to g/m³** before multiplying, otherwise you will get an answer off by a factor of **1000**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** for towns of 5,000 – 50,000 population equivalent (PE).
- **Food & Beverage Industry** effluents (high organic strength, e.g., dairies, breweries).
- **Pulp & Paper Mills** where high BOD and recalcitrant compounds dominate.
- **Nitrification** — RBCs are uniquely capable of staged nitrification (separate discs for carbonaceous BOD removal and ammonia oxidation to nitrate).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The two derivations below are the *most frequently asked numerical problems* for the 14-mark ESE question on RBC. Practice solving them in this exact order to score full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Total Effective Surface Area of an RBC Unit

**Problem Statement:**
An RBC has $N$ circular discs, each of diameter $D$ metres, mounted on a common shaft. Derive an expression for the **total wetted surface area** available for biofilm growth.

**Step 1 — Area of One Side of a Single Disc**

The area of one face of a circular disc is geometrically a circle:

$$A_{\text{one side}} = \pi r^2 = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Area of Both Sides of a Single Disc**

Microbial biofilm grows on *both* faces of the disc:

$$A_{\text{single disc}} = 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi D^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Total Area for $N$ Discs**

For $N$ identical discs in parallel:

$$A_{\text{total}} = N \times \frac{\pi D^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Accounting for Submergence (40 %)**

In practice, only 40 % of the disc is submerged. However, *both* sides still contact air and water alternately. For design purposes, the **effective area** is taken as the full wetted area:

$$A_s = 2 N \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi N D^2}{2}$$

This is the **standard design equation** used in KTU textbooks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Organic Loading Rate Calculation

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2-mark sub-part):**
A municipal STP has an RBC with **120 discs**, each of diameter **2.0 m**. The influent flow rate is **$Q = 1{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{d}$** with an influent BOD of **$S_0 = 220 \text{ mg/L}$**. Compute the **(a)** total wetted surface area, **(b)** Hydraulic Loading Rate, and **(c)** Organic Loading Rate.

### (a) Total Wetted Surface Area

Using the derived formula with $N = 120$, $D = 2.0$ m:

$$A_s = \frac{\pi N D^2}{2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$A_s = \frac{\pi \times 120 \times (2.0)^2}{2}$$

$$A_s = \frac{\pi \times 120 \times 4.0}{2}$$

$$A_s = \frac{480 \pi}{2} = 240 \pi$$

$$\boxed{A_s \approx 753.98 \text{ m}^2}$$

### (b) Hydraulic Loading Rate

$$\text{HLR} = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{1{,}500}{753.98}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HLR} \approx 1.989 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}$$

### (c) Organic Loading Rate

First, convert $S_0$ to g/m³: $S_0 = 220 \text{ mg/L} = 220 \text{ g/m}^3$.

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{Q \times S_0}{A_s} = \frac{1{,}500 \times 220}{753.98}$$

$$\boxed{\text{OLR} \approx 437.7 \text{ g BOD}/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Check:** The computed OLR (~438 g/m²·d) lies within the **standard design range of 5 – 20 g/m²·d for BOD removal** and **1 – 2.5 g/m²·d for combined carbonaceous + nitrification stages**. Since 438 g/m²·d is far too high, this RBC is **undersized** for the given load and would require either *more discs* or *a larger trough* in redesign.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — RBC vs. Trickling Filter (TF)

| Parameter | Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) | Trickling Filter (TF) |
|---|---|---|
| Media type | Rotating plastic discs | Stationary rock/plastic bed |
| Oxygen supply | Mechanical (rotation) | Natural draft (chimney effect) |
| Power consumption | Moderate (motor driven) | Low |
| BOD removal efficiency | 80 – 95 % | 60 – 85 % |
| Sludge production | Low, easy to settle | Moderate, frequent clogging |
| Nitrification capability | Excellent (staged design) | Limited |
| Footprint | Compact | Large |
| Clogging risk | Very low | High |
| KTУ Exam Verdict | **Modern, preferred** | **Conventional, legacy** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA`, `stageB`) as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards. No reserved keywords or markdown formatting used inside labels.

## 4.1 RBC System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    InfluentA[Influent Wastewater Q, S0] --> StatorB[Stator Wastewater Trough]
    StatorB --> RotorC[Rotor Shaft With N Discs]
    RotorC --> MotorD[Drive Motor 1 to 1.6 rpm]
    RotorC --> BiofilmE[Attached Biofilm 50 micrometre to 4 mm]
    BiofilmE --> SubstrateF[Stage 1 Substrate Uptake]
    BiofilmE --> AerationG[Stage 2 Oxygen Absorption]
    BiofilmE --> SloughingH[Stage 3 Biomass Sloughing]
    SubstrateF --> MetabolitesI[CO2 Plus H2O Plus Energy]
    AerationG --> MetabolitesI
    SloughingH --> ClarifierJ[Secondary Clarifier]
    MetabolitesI --> ClarifierJ
    ClarifierJ --> EffluentK[Treated Effluent Se]
    ClarifierJ --> SludgeL[Waste Activated Sludge]
    SludgeL --> DigesterM[Sludge Digester or Drying Bed]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Staged Nitrification Configuration

```mermaid
graph TB
    RawSewageA[Raw Sewage Inlet] --> ScreenB[Bar Screen and Grit Chamber]
    ScreenB --> PrimaryC[Primary Clarifier]
    PrimaryC --> RBCStage1[Stage 1 RBC Carbonaceous BOD Removal]
    RBCStage1 --> RBCStage2[Stage 2 RBC Nitrification]
    RBCStage2 --> FinalClarifierD[Final Clarifier]
    FinalClarifierD --> DisinfectionE[Chlorination or UV]
    DisinfectionE --> PolishedF[Polished Effluent Discharge]
    RBCStage1 --> SludgeG[Sludge Recycle]
    RBCStage2 --> SludgeG
    SludgeG --> PrimaryC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The two-stage RBC architecture (above) is the *gold standard* for combined carbon-and-nitrogen removal. Stage 1 handles BOD oxidation (heterotrophs dominate), while Stage 2 performs **nitrification** by autotrophic *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter* species, which require a low-C environment to thrive — hence the physical separation of stages.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing units** between HLR (m³/m²·d) and OLR (g/m²·d) is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in RBC numericals. Always state units *in your final answer box*.
> 2. **Do not** confuse the wetted area formula $A_s = \frac{\pi N D^2}{2}$ with that of a Trickling Filter. The factor of **2** (for both faces) is the discriminator.
> 3. **Skipping the unit-conversion step** (mg/L → g/m³) is a 2-mark deduction in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Forgetting the sloughing phase** in descriptive answers will cost you **1–2 marks** out of 7 in the "Working Principle" sub-part.
> 5. Many students write "disc rotates faster" — *wrong*. Higher speeds **shear off biofilm prematurely**. Always state **1.0 – 1.6 rpm**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Rotating Biological Contactor. Mention any two advantages over the Activated Sludge Process.**

**Model Answer:**
A Rotating Biological Contactor (RBC) is a fixed-film biological wastewater treatment system in which closely spaced circular discs, mounted on a horizontal rotating shaft, are partially submerged (40 %) in wastewater. The discs are typically made of corrugated plastic (HDPE) and rotate at **1.0 – 1.6 rpm**, supporting a microbial biofilm (~50 µm – 4 mm thick) that oxidizes organic matter using atmospheric oxygen.

**Two advantages over ASP:**
1. **Lower energy consumption** — No aeration tank or diffused air supply; rotation alone provides oxygen transfer.
2. **Higher biomass retention** — Biofilm is immobilized; hence, no sludge washout during hydraulic surges, and the system tolerates toxic shock loads better.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the four main constructional components of an RBC unit.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Rotor shaft** with mounted discs (HDPE / polystyrene).
2. **Stator (wastewater trough)** holding 40 % submergence of discs.
3. **Drive mechanism** (motor + gearbox) for 1.0 – 1.6 rpm rotation.
4. **Secondary clarifier** for sludge separation downstream of the RBC.

*(Acceptable additions: enclosure/cover, baffles, biomass control plates, supporting bearings.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module-3 questions carry 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a Rotating Biological Contactor. Discuss the role of biofilm and sloughing in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The RBC consists of a horizontal **rotor shaft** (typically 5–8 m long, mild steel) on which a large number of **closely spaced circular discs** (1.0–3.6 m diameter, made of corrugated HDPE or polystyrene) are mounted. The shaft is supported on bearings at both ends and is rotated by a **motor-gearbox assembly** maintaining 1.0–1.6 rpm. The lower 40 % of the discs dip into a semi-cylindrical **stator (wastewater trough)**, while the upper 60 % remains exposed to air. A **secondary clarifier** is provided downstream to settle the sloughed biomass.

**Working Principle — Three-Phase Cycle (3 marks):**
1. **Submerged Phase:** The biofilm, composed of aerobic and facultative bacteria, protozoa, and fungi, absorbs dissolved organic substrates from the wastewater.
2. **Aerial Phase:** As the disc emerges, the thin water film on its surface picks up atmospheric oxygen, replenishing the dissolved oxygen within the biofilm for respiration: $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$.
3. **Sloughing Phase:** When biofilm thickness exceeds ~4 mm, anaerobic conditions weaken the inner layer and the disc's shear detaches excess biomass, which is carried into the clarifier.

**Role of Sloughing (1 mark):** Sloughing prevents excessive biofilm buildup that would otherwise reduce oxygen diffusion. It also serves as a *natural biomass control mechanism*, eliminating the need for controlled wasting pumps (unlike ASP).

---

**(b) An RBC plant has 80 discs, each of diameter 2.5 m. The wastewater flow is 800 m³/d with an influent BOD of 250 mg/L. Calculate (i) the effective wetted area, (ii) the Hydraulic Loading Rate, and (iii) the Organic Loading Rate. Comment on whether the design OLR is within the standard range. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Effective Wetted Area:**

$$A_s = \frac{\pi N D^2}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times (2.5)^2}{2}$$

$$A_s = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 6.25}{2} = \frac{500 \pi}{2} = 250 \pi$$

$$\boxed{A_s \approx 785.4 \text{ m}^2}$$

**(ii) Hydraulic Loading Rate:**

$$\text{HLR} = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{800}{785.4}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HLR} \approx 1.019 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}$$

**(iii) Organic Loading Rate:**

Convert: $S_0 = 250 \text{ mg/L} = 250 \text{ g/m}^3$.

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{Q \times S_0}{A_s} = \frac{800 \times 250}{785.4}$$

$$\boxed{\text{OLR} \approx 254.6 \text{ g BOD}/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{d}}$$

**Comment:** The standard design OLR for an RBC is **5 – 20 g/m²·d** for carbonaceous BOD removal. Since 254.6 g/m²·d far exceeds the upper limit, the RBC is **severely underdesigned** for the given organic load. **Remedial measures:** (1) increase the number of discs to ~800, (2) reduce the influent BOD via upstream pre-treatment, or (3) add a parallel RBC train.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Differentiate between a Rotating Biological Contactor and a Trickling Filter. Discuss the design parameters of an RBC with their standard ranges. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Rotating Biological Contactor | Trickling Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Media | Rotating discs | Stationary bed |
| Oxygen Supply | Mechanical rotation | Natural draft |
| Energy Use | Higher | Lower |
| Footprint | Compact | Large |
| Nitrification | Excellent | Limited |
| Clogging | Negligible | Frequent |

**Design Parameters (Standard Ranges):**
- **Disc diameter:** 1.0 – 3.6 m
- **Disc spacing:** 20 – 40 mm
- **Rotation speed:** 1.0 – 1.6 rpm
- **Submergence:** ~40 % of disc area
- **Hydraulic Loading Rate (HLR):** 0.04 – 0.16 m³/m²·d
- **Organic Loading Rate (OLR):** 5 – 20 g BOD/m²·d (carbonaceous stage); 1 – 2.5 g/m²·d (nitrification stage)
- **Biofilm thickness:** 0.05 – 4.0 mm
- **HRT:** 0.7 – 2.0 hours

---

**(b) An RBC with 60 discs of diameter 2.0 m treats a flow of 1,200 m³/d with influent BOD 300 mg/L. If the design OLR should not exceed 20 g/m²·d, determine whether the existing design is adequate. If not, find the required number of discs. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Existing Wetted Area:**

$$A_s = \frac{\pi \times 60 \times (2.0)^2}{2} = \frac{240 \pi}{2} = 120 \pi \approx 376.99 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Existing OLR:**

$S_0 = 300 \text{ g/m}^3$

$$\text{OLR} = \frac{1{,}200 \times 300}{376.99} \approx 954.9 \text{ g/m}^2 \cdot \text{d}$$

**Step 3 — Adequacy Check:**

Since 954.9 g/m²·d ≫ 20 g/m²·d, the existing design is **grossly inadequate**.

**Step 4 — Required Area:**

$$A_{s,\text{req}} = \frac{Q \times S_0}{\text{OLR}_{\max}} = \frac{1{,}200 \times 300}{20} = 18{,}000 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Required Number of Discs:**

$$N = \frac{2 A_{s,\text{req}}}{\pi D^2} = \frac{2 \times 18{,}000}{\pi \times 4.0} = \frac{36{,}000}{4 \pi}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 2{,}865 \text{ discs}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Realism:** A single shaft cannot practically hold 2,865 discs (shaft length would exceed 200 m). Therefore, in practice, the plant would deploy **multiple parallel RBC trains** (e.g., 6 trains × 478 discs each) or pre-treat the wastewater to lower influent BOD via primary clarification with chemical precipitation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Final-minute revision card — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ RBC = **Fixed-film** (attached-growth) aerobic wastewater treatment system.
- ✅ Disc material: **HDPE / polystyrene**, 1.0–3.6 m diameter, 20–40 mm spacing.
- ✅ Rotation speed: **1.0–1.6 rpm** (slow — to avoid sloughing control loss).
- ✅ Submergence: **40 %** of disc in wastewater.
- ✅ Wetted area formula: $\mathbf{A_s = \dfrac{\pi N D^2}{2}}$ (factor of 2 for both faces).
- ✅ HLR = $Q / A_s$ (m³/m²·d); OLR = $Q \cdot S_0 / A_s$ (g/m²·d).
- ✅ Standard OLR: **5–20 g/m²·d** (carbonaceous), **1–2.5 g/m²·d** (nitrification).
- ✅ BOD removal: **80–95 %**.
- ✅ Three working phases: **Submerged uptake → Aerial oxygenation → Sloughing**.
- ✅ Sloughing threshold biofilm thickness: **~4 mm**.
- ✅ Key advantage over ASP: lower energy, no sludge bulking, better shock-load resilience.
- ✅ Nitrification possible in **staged design** (Stage 1 = BOD, Stage 2 = NH₃ → NO₃⁻).
- ✅ Always convert **mg/L → g/m³** before OLR calculation.
- ✅ Nitrifying bacteria: *Nitrosomonas* (NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻) and *Nitrobacter* (NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻).
- ✅ Downstream unit: **secondary clarifier** for sloughed biomass separation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluidized Bed Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluidized Bed Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluidized Bed Reactor (FBR)** is a type of chemical/biological reactor in which a bed of solid particles (typically a support matrix carrying immobilized biomass, catalyst, or sorbent media) is suspended in a fluid (gas or liquid) by passing the fluid upward through the bed at a velocity sufficient to overcome the gravitational force on the particles. This causes the solid particles to behave like a fluid, exhibiting properties such as fluid-like flow, pressure transmission, and mixing.

In the context of **Environmental Biotechnology**, FBRs are extensively employed for **aerobic and anaerobic wastewater treatment**, **biofilm processes**, **heavy metal removal**, and **biogas production**, where the fluidization provides excellent mass transfer, uniform temperature distribution, and high surface area for microbial attachment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fluidized Bed Reactors are classified under *Unit Operations in Bioreactor Design*. The student must understand the transition regimes, fluidization velocity calculations, and biotechnological applications — these are recurring **high-weightage** questions in ESE (End Semester Examination).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen blender with sand and water**. When the blender is off, the sand sits at the bottom (this is a **fixed bed**). When you turn the blender on slowly, water swirls and lifts the sand grains — the sand-water mixture now flows like a thick liquid. The sand particles are no longer resting on the bottom but are suspended, moving freely. This suspended, liquid-like state of solids in a fluid is exactly **fluidization**.

In a Fluidized Bed Reactor:
- The **sand** represents the support particles (e.g., activated carbon, sand, biocarrier) with attached microbes.
- The **water** represents the wastewater (the fluidizing medium).
- The **upward flow velocity** of water determines whether the bed is *fixed*, *fluidized*, or *transported* out.

## 1.3 Key Operational Parameters & Standard Metrics

The following physical and operational parameters govern FBR performance:

- **Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$)** — typically in the range of **0.001 to 0.1 m/s** for biological carriers.
- **Operating Velocity ($U_0$)** — usually maintained at **1.5 to 3 times** $U_{mf}$.
- **Superficial Velocity ($U$)** — the velocity of fluid if the bed were empty.
- **Bed Porosity ($\varepsilon$)** — ranges from **0.4 (fixed bed)** to **1.0 (free flow)**.
- **Particle Reynolds Number ($Re_p$)** — dimensionless number for flow regime classification.
- **Pressure Drop ($\Delta P$)** — measured in **Pascals (Pa)** or **kPa**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box:**
> - **Fixed Bed:** Solid particles stationary; fluid flows through interstices.
> - **Fluidized Bed:** Solid particles suspended; behave like a fluid.
> - **Spouted Bed:** Central jet of fluid supports particles in a fountain-like motion.
> - **Pneumatic Transport:** Velocity too high; particles are entrained out of the reactor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Fluidization — The Underlying Physics

When fluid flows upward through a bed of solid particles, the following sequence of events occurs as the fluid velocity is gradually increased:

1. **Fixed Bed Regime (0 ≤ U < U_{mf}):** The particles remain stationary. The pressure drop increases with velocity and follows the **Ergun equation**.
2. **Incipient Fluidization (U = U_{mf}):** The pressure drop equals the buoyant weight of the bed per unit area. The particles begin to suspend. The bed porosity reaches $\varepsilon_{mf}$.
3. **Fluidized Bed Regime (U_{mf} < U < U_t):** The bed expands. Pressure drop remains approximately constant at the bed weight per unit area. Particles exhibit vigorous mixing.
4. **Transport/Entrainment Regime (U > U_t):** Particles are carried out of the reactor with the fluid. This is the **terminal velocity** regime.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why:** Fluidization occurs because the upward drag force exerted by the fluid on the particles balances the net gravitational force (weight minus buoyancy). At this equilibrium, the particles are weightless in the fluid frame and behave like a fluid.

## 2.2 The Ergun Equation — The Heart of FBR Analysis

The Ergun equation gives the pressure drop through a packed (or fluidized) bed:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \mu U (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \, \varepsilon^3} + \frac{1.75 \rho_f U^2 (1-\varepsilon)}{d_p \, \varepsilon^3}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across the bed (Pa)
- $L$ = Bed height (m)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of fluid (Pa·s)
- $U$ = Superficial fluid velocity (m/s)
- $\varepsilon$ = Bed void fraction (porosity)
- $d_p$ = Particle diameter (m)
- $\rho_f$ = Fluid density (kg/m³)

The first term represents **viscous (laminar) losses**, and the second term represents **inertial (turbulent) losses**.

## 2.3 Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$)

At incipient fluidization, the pressure drop equals the buoyant weight of the bed:

$$\Delta P = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) (\rho_p - \rho_f) g L$$

Combining with the Ergun equation and solving for $U_{mf}$:

$$U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \, \mu}{d_p \, \rho_f}$$

Where the modified Reynolds number at minimum fluidization is given by:

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$$

And the **Archimedes Number (Ar)** is:

$$Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \, \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$$

## 2.4 Terminal Velocity ($U_t$)

The terminal velocity is the maximum allowable fluidization velocity before particles are entrained. For a single spherical particle settling under gravity:

$$U_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$$

Where $C_D$ is the drag coefficient (function of $Re_t$):

$$Re_t = \frac{d_p U_t \rho_f}{\mu}$$

| $Re_t$ Range | $C_D$ Formula | Regime |
|---|---|---|
| $Re_t < 0.1$ | $C_D = 24/Re_t$ | Stokes' Law |
| $0.1 < Re_t < 1000$ | $C_D = 18.5/Re_t^{0.6}$ | Intermediate |
| $1000 < Re_t < 2 \times 10^5$ | $C_D = 0.44$ | Newton's Law |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ergun Equation | $\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150 \mu U (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \varepsilon^3} + \frac{1.75 \rho_f U^2 (1-\varepsilon)}{d_p \varepsilon^3}$ | Pa/m | Pressure drop in packed/fluidized bed |
| 2 | Bed Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = (1-\varepsilon)(\rho_p - \rho_f) g L$ | Pa | Pressure drop at incipient fluidization |
| 3 | Archimedes Number | $Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$ | dimensionless | Buoyancy vs. viscous forces |
| 4 | Min. Fluidization Velocity | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 Ar} - 33.7$ | dimensionless | Threshold of fluidization |
| 5 | Terminal Velocity | $U_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$ | m/s | Maximum operating velocity |
| 6 | Bed Expansion | $\frac{L}{L_{mf}} = \frac{1-\varepsilon_{mf}}{1-\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | Operating bed height |
| 7 | Particle Reynolds Number | $Re_p = \frac{d_p U \rho_f}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime identification |
| 8 | Drag Coefficient (Stokes) | $C_D = 24/Re_p$ | dimensionless | Low $Re_p$ settling |
| 9 | Froude Number | $Fr = \frac{U^2}{g d_p}$ | dimensionless | Flow stability in bed |
| 10 | Residence Time | $\tau = V/Q$ | seconds | Hydraulic contact time |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fluidized Bed Reactors are the **workhorses** of modern environmental engineering:

- **Wastewater Treatment:** Anaerobic Fluidized Bed Reactors (AFBR) treat high-strength industrial effluents (e.g., food processing, pharmaceutical, distillery waste).
- **Heavy Metal Biosorption:** FBRs with immobilized algae or fungal biomass efficiently adsorb Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, and Cr⁶⁺ from contaminated water.
- **Biogas Production:** Fluidization enhances substrate-biomass contact, increasing methane yield.
- **Soil Bioremediation:** Air fluidized beds support aerobic degradation of hydrocarbons.
- **Drinking Water:** Biological activated carbon fluidized beds remove organic micropollutants and ammonia.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Fluidization Velocity from First Principles

### Statement
Derive the expression for minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) starting from the force balance on a single particle and the Ergun equation.

### Step 1: Force Balance at Incipient Fluidization
At $U = U_{mf}$, the net upward drag force equals the net downward gravitational force (weight minus buoyancy):

$$F_{drag} = F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy}$$

For a bed of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $L$:

$$F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} = (1-\varepsilon_{mf}) A L (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

### Step 2: Pressure Drop Equivalence
The drag force is transmitted as a pressure drop across the bed:

$$\Delta P \cdot A = (1-\varepsilon_{mf}) A L (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

Dividing by $A$ and $L$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = (1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

### Step 3: Equating with the Ergun Equation
Setting the Ergun pressure drop equal to the bed weight per unit volume:

$$(1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \frac{150 \mu U_{mf} (1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2}{d_p^2 \varepsilon_{mf}^3} + \frac{1.75 \rho_f U_{mf}^2 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{d_p \varepsilon_{mf}^3}$$

### Step 4: Simplification
Dividing both sides by $(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$:

$$(\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \frac{150 \mu U_{mf} (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{d_p^2 \varepsilon_{mf}^3} + \frac{1.75 \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{d_p \varepsilon_{mf}^3}$$

### Step 5: Conversion to Dimensionless Form
Multiplying both sides by $\frac{d_p^3 \rho_f}{\mu^2}$:

$$\frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2} = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} \cdot \frac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu} + \frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} \cdot \left(\frac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu}\right)^2$$

### Step 6: Recognizing the Dimensionless Groups
Defining $Ar$ and $Re_{mf}$:

$$Ar = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} Re_{mf} + \frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} Re_{mf}^2$$

### Step 7: Solving the Quadratic in $Re_{mf}$
This is a quadratic in $Re_{mf}$:

$$\frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} Re_{mf}^2 + \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3} Re_{mf} - Ar = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula and using $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$ as a typical value:

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$$

### Step 8: Final Expression for $U_{mf}$

$$U_{mf} = \frac{\mu \cdot Re_{mf}}{d_p \rho_f}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example Problem

**Problem:** A fluidized bed reactor uses sand particles ($d_p = 0.5 \text{ mm}$, $\rho_p = 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3$) to treat wastewater ($\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.001 \text{ Pa·s}$). Calculate the minimum fluidization velocity. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate the Archimedes Number:

$$Ar = \frac{(0.0005)^3 \times 1000 \times (2500 - 1000) \times 9.81}{(0.001)^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 1000 \times 1500 \times 9.81}{1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Ar = \frac{1.8394 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1839.4$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $Re_{mf}$:

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{(33.7)^2 + 0.0408 \times 1839.4} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 75.05} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1210.74} - 33.7 = 34.80 - 33.7 = 1.10$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $U_{mf}$:

$$U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{1.10 \times 0.001}{0.0005 \times 1000}$$

$$U_{mf} = \frac{0.0011}{0.5} = 0.0022 \text{ m/s} = 2.2 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Result:** The minimum fluidization velocity is **2.2 mm/s**. The operating velocity should be **3.3 to 6.6 mm/s** (1.5–3 times $U_{mf}$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: FBR vs. Other Bioreactors

| Feature | Fluidized Bed Reactor (FBR) | Packed Bed Reactor | Stirred Tank Reactor (STR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biomass Support | Small mobile particles | Large stationary particles | Suspended/attached |
| Surface Area | **Very High** | Moderate | Low |
| Mass Transfer | **Excellent** | Limited by diffusion | Good |
| Clogging Risk | **Very Low** | **High** | None |
| Energy for Mixing | Moderate | Low | High |
| Biomass Washout | Possible if $U > U_t$ | None | Possible |
| Scale-up Complexity | High | Low | Moderate |
| Biofilm Thickness Control | Easy | Difficult | N/A |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Best for | High-strength wastewater | Low flow, polishing | Fermentation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Fluidized Bed Reactor System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Influent Wastewater Reservoir] --> B[Feed Pump with Flow Controller]
    B --> C[Flow Distributor Plate at Reactor Base]
    C --> D[Conical Bottom Section for Uniform Flow]
    D --> E[Fluidized Bed Zone with Biocarrier Particles]
    E --> F[Biocarrier Surface with Immobilized Biofilm]
    F --> G[Expanded Freeboard Section]
    G --> H[Cyclone Separator for Particle Recovery]
    H --> I[Recirculation Line]
    I --> E
    H --> J[Effluent Outlet for Treated Water]
    E --> K[Biogas Collection Dome if Anaerobic]
    K --> L[Gas Scrubber and Metering Unit]
    E --> M[Pressure Tap Points for Delta-P Measurement]
    M --> N[Differential Pressure Transmitter]
    N --> O[SCADA Control and Monitoring System]
    O --> B
```

## 4.2 Fluidization Regimes Diagram (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 1: Fixed Bed] -->|U increases| B[Stage 2: Expanded Bed]
    B -->|U = Umf| C[Stage 3: Incipient Fluidization]
    C -->|Umf less than U less than Ut| D[Stage 4: Fluidized Bed]
    D -->|U = Ut| E[Stage 5: Terminal Velocity]
    E -->|U greater than Ut| F[Stage 6: Pneumatic Transport and Entrainment]
    
    A1[Particles stationary] --- A
    B1[Particles vibrate slightly] --- B
    C1[Particles just suspended] --- C
    D1[Particles mix vigorously] --- D
    E1[Particles begin entrainment] --- E
    F1[Particles leave reactor] --- F
```

## 4.3 Anaerobic Fluidized Bed Reactor (AFBR) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RAW[Raw Industrial Wastewater] --> EQ[Equalization Tank]
    EQ --> NEUT[Neutralization and pH Adjustment Unit]
    NEUT --> HT[Heat Exchanger for Temperature Control at 35 to 37 C]
    HT --> PUMP[High Recirculation Pump]
    PUMP --> DIST[Flow Distributor]
    DIST --> AFBR[Fluidized Bed Reactor with Sand or Biocarrier]
    AFBR --> GAS[Biogas Collection in Gas Holder]
    GAS --> SCRUB[Gas Cleaning and Desulfurization]
    AFBR --> SEP[Effluent Solid Liquid Separator]
    SEP --> POL[Polishing Pond or Tertiary Treatment]
    POL --> DIS[Discharge to Environment]
    AFBR --> REC[Recycle Line to Maintain Fluidization]
    REC --> PUMP
```

## 4.4 Labelled Component Diagram of FBR

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Reactor Cylindrical Body] --> P2[Top Freeboard Section]
    P2 --> P3[Gas Vent or Biogas Outlet]
    P1 --> P4[Sample Ports at Multiple Heights]
    P1 --> P5[Pressure Measurement Taps]
    P1 --> P6[Fluidized Bed Zone with Biocarrier]
    P6 --> P7[Biofilm Layer on Particle Surface]
    P6 --> P8[Interstitial Liquid Phase]
    P1 --> P9[Conical Distributor Section]
    P9 --> P10[Inlet Feed and Recirculation Line]
    P9 --> P11[Perforated Distributor Plate]
    P9 --> P12[Support Grid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **conical bottom** and **distributor plate** are critical for uniform fluidization. Poor distribution leads to **channeling** (preferential flow paths) and **dead zones** where biomass activity drops, costing the student significant marks if not mentioned in design problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **Minimum Fluidization Velocity** and list **two** factors that affect it.

**Model Answer:**
Minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) is defined as the superficial fluid velocity at which the pressure drop across a packed bed first becomes equal to the buoyant weight of the bed per unit cross-sectional area, causing the particles to become suspended.

**Two factors affecting $U_{mf}$:**
1. **Particle diameter ($d_p$):** Larger particles require higher $U_{mf}$ (since drag force is proportional to $d_p^2$ but weight is proportional to $d_p^3$).
2. **Density difference ($\rho_p - \rho_f$):** Greater density difference requires higher $U_{mf}$ because particles are heavier relative to the fluid.

(Any other valid factors: fluid viscosity, bed porosity, particle shape — accept up to 2.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between **particulate fluidization** and **aggregative fluidization** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Particulate Fluidization | Aggregative Fluidization |
|---|---|---|
| Also called | Smooth fluidization | Bubbling fluidization |
| Typical particles | Small, dense, uniform | Large, light, non-uniform |
| Fluid medium | Liquids | Gases |
| Bed behavior | Uniform expansion, no bubbles | Bubbles form, vigorous mixing |
| Typical example | Liquid–sand FBR (wastewater) | Air–catalyst FBR (FCC units) |

The dimensionless **Froude Number** at minimum fluidization ($Fr_{mf}$) is the criterion:
- $Fr_{mf} < 0.13$: Particulate
- $Fr_{mf} > 0.13$: Aggregative

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice: Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper Style]**

**Question A:**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labelled diagram**, explain the construction and working of an **Anaerobic Fluidized Bed Reactor (AFBR)** used for industrial wastewater treatment. Describe the role of biocarrier particles and the biofilm formation mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **Minimum Fluidization Velocity** using the **Ergun equation**. A wastewater FBR uses sand particles of 0.6 mm diameter (density 2650 kg/m³) treating water at 30°C. Calculate $U_{mf}$. Given: $\rho_{water} = 995 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu_{water} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — AFBR Construction and Working (7 Marks)**

**Constructional Features (3 marks):**
1. **Cylindrical Reactor Vessel:** Made of stainless steel or corrosion-resistant material; height-to-diameter ratio typically 4:1 to 6:1.
2. **Conical Bottom Section:** Facilitates uniform distribution of influent and prevents dead zones.
3. **Perforated Distributor Plate:** Located at the base; ensures uniform upward flow of wastewater.
4. **Biocarrier Particles:** Sand, granular activated carbon (GAC), or proprietary plastic media (typical size 0.2–1.0 mm, density 1.5–2.6 g/cm³). These form the support matrix for biofilm.
5. **Freeboard Section:** Top expansion zone where fluid velocity decreases, preventing particle entrainment.
6. **Biogas Collection Dome:** Sealed top with gas outlet for collecting CH₄ and CO₂.
7. **Recirculation System:** High-flow pump to maintain fluidization velocity.
8. **Pressure Tap Points:** For monitoring bed pressure drop.

**Working Principle and Biofilm Formation (4 marks):**
- Wastewater is pumped upward through the distributor plate.
- The upward velocity suspends the biocarrier particles, fluidizing the bed.
- Anaerobic microorganisms (methanogens, acidogens) naturally attach to the carrier surface, forming a **biofilm** of thickness 50–200 µm.
- The biofilm formation stages: (i) reversible adsorption → (ii) irreversible attachment via EPS (extracellular polymeric substances) → (iii) biofilm maturation → (iv) cell proliferation and substrate degradation.
- Organic pollutants diffuse into the biofilm, get metabolized to biogas, and the treated water exits through the top.
- Biogas (60–70% CH₄) is collected from the dome.

**Diagram Reference:** (See Section 4.3 above for Mermaid AFBR flow diagram.)

---

**Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical (7 Marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 above for full step-by-step working.)*

Final expressions:

$$Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$$

$$U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \, \mu}{d_p \, \rho_f}$$

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- $d_p = 0.6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $\rho_p = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_f = 995 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 2: Calculate Archimedes Number**

$$Ar = \frac{(6 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 995 \times (2650 - 995) \times 9.81}{(0.8 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{-10} \times 995 \times 1655 \times 9.81}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$Ar = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.615 \times 10^7}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$Ar = \frac{3.488 \times 10^{-3}}{6.4 \times 10^{-7}} = 5450$$

**Step 3: Calculate $Re_{mf}$**

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \times 5450} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 222.36} - 33.7 = \sqrt{1358.05} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = 36.85 - 33.7 = 3.15$$

**Step 4: Calculate $U_{mf}$**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{3.15 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \times 10^{-4} \times 995} = \frac{2.52 \times 10^{-3}}{0.597} = 4.22 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{U_{mf} = 4.22 \text{ mm/s}}$$

---

### Module Internal Choice: Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**Question B:**

**(a)** Explain the **various fluidization regimes** with the help of a plot of pressure drop vs. superficial velocity. Discuss the significance of each regime for FBR design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of Fluidized Bed Reactors in environmental biotechnology? Compare FBR with **packed bed reactor** in terms of mass transfer, clogging, and biomass retention. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Fluidization Regimes (7 Marks)**

**The Plot Description (3 marks):**

| Region | Velocity Range | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **AB (Fixed Bed)** | $0 < U < U_A$ | Pressure drop increases linearly with velocity; particles stationary. |
| **B (Packing Point)** | $U_A$ | Slight non-linearity; particles begin rearrangement. |
| **BC (Expanded Bed)** | $U_A < U < U_{mf}$ | Bed expands slightly; voids increase; pressure drop deviates. |
| **CD (Incipient Fluidization)** | $U = U_{mf}$ | Pressure drop equals bed weight per unit area; particles just suspended. |
| **DE (Fluidized Bed)** | $U_{mf} < U < U_t$ | Pressure drop remains essentially **constant** (plateau region); vigorous mixing. |
| **EF (Transport Regime)** | $U > U_t$ | Pressure drop rises again; particles entrained out of bed. |

**Plot ASCII Schematic:**
```
Pressure
Drop (ΔP)
   |          ____E (Terminal velocity)
   |         /    \____
   |        /         \___F (Transport)
   |       /
   |------D===========  (Constant ΔP in fluidized regime)
   |     /
   |    C
   |   /
   |  / B (Expanded)
   | /
   |/_____ A (Fixed bed)
   |________________________
   0    U_mf         U_t   U (Velocity)
```

**Significance for Design (4 marks):**
1. **Operating Range:** Reactor must operate in DE zone (typically $U = 1.5 U_{mf}$ to $0.8 U_t$).
2. **Pressure Drop Equals Bed Weight:** This unique feature means ΔP doesn't depend on velocity in fluidized regime — design simplification.
3. **Transition Velocity Identification:** Critical for control systems to avoid defluidization or entrainment.
4. **Channeling Avoidance:** Operator must ensure $U$ never falls below $U_{mf}$.

---

**Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Advantages of FBR (3 marks):**
1. **High biomass concentration** (up to 30–40 g/L VSS) — much higher than CSTR or trickling filters.
2. **Excellent mass transfer** — fluidization prevents external mass transfer limitations.
3. **No clogging** — fluidized particles self-clean the support surface.
4. **Uniform temperature and pH** — due to intense mixing.
5. **Compact reactor footprint** — high MLSS allows small reactor volume.
6. **Handles high organic and hydraulic loading rates.**

**Limitations of FBR (2 marks):**
1. **High pumping energy** required to maintain fluidization.
2. **Complex control** of bed expansion and biofilm thickness.
3. **Particle attrition** and loss of fine biocarrier.
4. **Scale-up difficulties** due to non-uniform fluidization in large diameters.

**Comparison with Packed Bed (2 marks):**

| Parameter | FBR | Packed Bed |
|---|---|---|
| Mass Transfer | **Excellent** (forced convection) | Poor (channeling, dead zones) |
| Clogging | **Minimal** (self-cleaning) | **Severe** (biofilm overgrowth) |
| Biomass Retention | Moderate (continuous sloughing) | Very High (immobile biofilm) |
| Operating Cost | Higher (pumping) | Lower |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting particle diameter from **mm to m** is the most common error in $U_{mf}$ numerical problems. Always use SI units throughout.
> 2. **Forgetting Density Difference:** Many students write $\rho_p$ instead of $(\rho_p - \rho_f)$ in the Archimedes number. This changes the answer by 60%.
> 3. **Skipping Freeboard Design:** Examiners specifically test whether students mention the **freeboard section** and **particle recovery** (cyclone) — marks deducted otherwise.
> 4. **Confusing Regimes:** Students often write "fluidization starts at terminal velocity" — this is **WRONG**. Fluidization starts at $U_{mf}$; entrainment starts at $U_t$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for FBR (Must Memorize):**

- ✅ **FBR Definition:** A reactor where solid particles are suspended by upward fluid flow, behaving fluid-like.
- ✅ **Key Velocities:** $U_{mf}$ (start of fluidization) < $U$ (operating) < $U_t$ (start of entrainment).
- ✅ **Ergun Equation:** Pressure drop = viscous loss + inertial loss.
- ✅ **Archimedes Number:** $Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$ — governs fluidization behavior.
- ✅ **$U_{mf}$ Formula:** $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 Ar} - 33.7$.
- ✅ **Constant ΔP Rule:** In the fluidized regime, ΔP equals the buoyant weight of the bed and is **independent** of velocity.
- ✅ **Typical Operating Range:** $U = 1.5$ to $3 \times U_{mf}$.
- ✅ **Bioprocess Types:** AFBR (anaerobic), BAFB (aerobic), GAC-FBR (adsorption + biology).
- ✅ **Critical Components:** Distributor plate, biocarrier, freeboard, recycle pump, biogas dome (anaerobic).
- ✅ **Biofilm Thickness:** 50–200 µm; stages — adsorption → attachment → EPS production → maturation.
- ✅ **Advantages over PBR:** No clogging, higher mass transfer, smaller footprint, handles higher loads.
- ✅ **Disadvantages vs PBR:** Higher energy cost, complex controls, particle attrition, scale-up difficulty.
- ✅ **KTU Favourite Topics:** (1) Derivation of $U_{mf}$, (2) FBR diagram with labels, (3) Comparison tables, (4) AFBR for industrial wastewater.
- ✅ **Remember:** Particulate fluidization (liquid–solid) vs. aggregative (gas–solid) — classified by **Froude number** at $U_{mf}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circulating Bed Biofilm Reactors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Circulating Bed Biofilm Reactors

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circulating Bed Biofilm Reactor (CBBR)** — also referred to as a *Circulating Fluidized Bed Bioreactor (CFBBR)* — is a **three-phase (gas–liquid–solid) attached-growth biological wastewater treatment system** in which small, low-density biocarrier particles (typically **0.2–0.5 mm** diameter) are kept in continuous fluidized suspension through high recycle flow and/or aeration. The carriers circulate between an **aerobic riser/column** and a **non-aerobic (anoxic) downcomer**, allowing simultaneous removal of carbonaceous BOD, nitrification, and denitrification within a single reactor vessel.

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Carrier diameter | **0.2 – 0.5 mm** |
| Carrier material | HDPE, PE, or porous sintered glass/ceramic |
| Carrier density | **0.9 – 1.05 g/cm³** |
| Dissolved Oxygen (DO) | **2 – 6 mg/L** in riser zone |
| HRT | **0.5 – 4 hours** |
| Recirculation ratio (R) | **5 – 40** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Aquarium Pebble Filter"**
> Imagine a fish tank with thousands of tiny hollow plastic beads constantly swirling in the water (kept aloft by an air pump pushing bubbles upward). Bacteria grow as a thin slimy layer on every bead. As dirty water flows through, the bacteria "eat" the waste. The beads are not stuck — they **circulate**. The reactor uses this same idea at industrial scale: a tall vertical column where air bubbles (or water flow) push thousands of micro-carriers upward; when they hit the top, they tumble back down through a separate down-comer and are re-injected. This endless **loop** exposes microorganisms to both oxygen-rich (rising) and oxygen-poor (falling) zones, mimicking nature's day–night cycle but at thousands of times the efficiency.

**Key intuition:**
- **Carrier** = mobile apartment for microbes
- **Circulation** = the "heartbeat" of the reactor
- **Riser + Downcomer** = a vertical treadmill of life

## 1.3 Why CBBRs Are Used (Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **High biomass concentration** without sludge recycling (10,000–20,000 mg VSS/L).
> - **Compact footprint** — about **1/5 the size** of conventional activated sludge.
> - Simultaneous **C, N, and P removal** in a single reactor.
> - **No clogging** because carriers are free-moving (unlike fixed-film trickling filters).
> - Resistant to shock loads and toxic influents.

## 1.4 Sub-Topics Covered in This Module Note

1. Reactor architecture and working principle
2. Biocarrier properties and biofilm kinetics
3. Mass transfer and oxygen demand equations
4. Design equations (HRT, F/M, recycle ratio)
5. Schematic flow architecture
6. KTU-style numerical and descriptive questions
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Influent entry** → wastewater enters the *downcomer* zone (low DO → anoxic/anaerobic).
2. **Carrier fluidization** → air injected at the base of the *riser* drags biofilm-coated carriers upward.
3. **Biofilm contact** → as carriers move upward, dissolved organics diffuse into the biofilm (mass-transfer-limited reaction).
4. **Separator at top** → at the reactor top, gas escapes; carriers fall into the downcomer due to gravity loss of buoyant gas.
5. **Circulation loop** → carriers re-enter the riser; cycle time per particle ≈ **30 – 120 seconds**.
6. **Effluent withdrawal** → clarified treated water leaves from the upper separator zone.
7. **Sloughing control** → excess biofilm detaches naturally, removed as effluent suspended solids.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of circulation:** Continuous motion prevents biofilm overgrowth, maintains uniform DO/ORP zones, and provides simultaneous nitrification–denitrification (SND) in a single vessel.

---

## 2.2 Biofilm Kinetic Framework

The **steady-state biofilm model** (Rittmann & McCarty, 1980) governs substrate flux into the biofilm:

$$J = -D_f \frac{dS}{dz}\bigg|_{z=0}$$

For a deep biofilm (zero-order intrinsic reaction limited by diffusion), the surface substrate flux is:

$$J = \eta \cdot k_{0,f} \cdot L_f$$

where the **effectiveness factor** $\eta$ corrects for diffusion limitation:

$$\eta = \frac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi} \quad ; \quad \phi = L_f \sqrt{\frac{k_{0,f}}{D_f \cdot S_s}}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete KTU-ready formula bank** for CBBR design problems. Master every row.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Equation | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hydraulic Retention Time** | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | hours (h) | $V$ = reactor volume; $Q$ = flow |
| 2 | **F/M Ratio (Food-to-Microorganism)** | $F/M = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X}$ | day⁻¹ | CBBR operates at **0.05 – 0.3 d⁻¹** |
| 3 | **Recycle Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{Q_R}{Q}$ | dimensionless | CBBR: **5 – 40** |
| 4 | **Carrier Surface Area Loading** | $A_{spec} = \dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{m_p}$ | m²/g | $d_p$ = particle diameter; $m_p$ = particle mass |
| 5 | **Volumetric Oxygen Transfer Coefficient** | $K_L a_{20} = K_L a_T \cdot 1.024^{(20-T)}$ | h⁻¹ | Standard correction to 20 °C |
| 6 | **Oxygen Requirement (Carbonaceous)** | $O_2 = 1.42 \cdot \Delta BOD$ | kg O₂/d | 1.42 = stoichiometric coefficient |
| 7 | **Oxygen Requirement (Nitrification)** | $O_2 = 4.57 \cdot \Delta NH_4^+$–N | kg O₂/d | Complete nitrification stoichiometry |
| 8 | **Biofilm Surface Substrate Flux** | $J = \dfrac{D_f \cdot (S - S_s)}{L_f}$ | g/m²·d | $D_f$ = diffusion coefficient in biofilm |
| 9 | **Thiele Modulus** | $\phi = L_f \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{0,f}}{D_f \cdot S_s}}$ | dimensionless | Determines diffusion limitation |
| 10 | **Effectiveness Factor** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tanh(\phi)}{\phi}$ | dimensionless | $\eta \to 1$ for thin biofilm |
| 11 | **Carrier Bed Porosity** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{V_{carriers}}{V_{reactor}}$ | dimensionless | Typical: **0.4 – 0.7** |
| 12 | **Upflow Fluidization Velocity** | $U_f = \dfrac{Q_{air} + Q_{water}}{A_{cross}}$ | m/s | Must exceed $U_{mf}$ (minimum fluidization) |
| 13 | **Total Reactor Sizing** | $V = Q \cdot \theta$ | m³ | Direct product for plug-flow assumption |
| 14 | **Biofilm Density** | $\rho_{bio} \approx 1.03$ | g/cm³ | Used in MLSS-equivalent calculations |
| 15 | **SRT (Solids Retention Time)** | $\theta_c = \dfrac{X \cdot V}{X_e \cdot Q_e}$ | days | CBBR decouples $\theta$ from $\theta_c$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU pitfall:** Students often confuse **HRT ($\theta$)** with **SRT ($\theta_c$)**. In CBBRs, SRT can be **20 – 100 days** while HRT is only **hours** — this is the very advantage of attached-growth systems!

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Municipal STP retrofits** — used in space-constrained Indian cities (e.g., Kochi, Bengaluru).
> - **Industrial effluent** — handles high-strength pharmaceutical, textile, and food-processing wastewater.
> - **Aquaculture recirculating systems (RAS)** — provides nitrification in fish-farming tanks.
> - **Landfill leachate treatment** — robust against ammonia spikes (10,000+ mg/L NH₃-N).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From First-Principles Mass Balance to Effluent Substrate Concentration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the steady-state CBBR design equation linking influent BOD ($S_0$) to effluent BOD ($S_e$) for a single-pass riser with recycle.

### Step 1 — Define the System and Variables

- $Q$ = influent flow (m³/d)
- $Q_R$ = recycle flow (m³/d)
- $S_0$ = influent substrate concentration (mg/L)
- $S_e$ = effluent substrate concentration (mg/L)
- $X$ = attached biomass concentration (g VSS/m³ of reactor)
- $k$ = maximum specific substrate utilization rate (d⁻¹)
- $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (mg/L)
- $Y$ = yield coefficient
- $k_d$ = endogenous decay coefficient (d⁻¹)
- $V$ = riser volume (m³)

### Step 2 — Write the Steady-State Mass Balance (Monod Kinetics)

At steady state, the rate of substrate utilization equals the rate of substrate removal across the reactor:

$$Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) = V \cdot X \cdot \frac{k \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e}$$

This expresses that **mass of substrate removed per day** = **volumetric reaction rate × reactor volume**.

### Step 3 — Rearrange to Obtain the Design Equation

$$\frac{S_0 - S_e}{X \cdot \theta} = \frac{k \cdot S_e}{K_s + S_e}$$

where $\theta = V / Q$ is the hydraulic retention time.

Cross-multiplying:

$$(S_0 - S_e)(K_s + S_e) = k \cdot X \cdot \theta \cdot S_e$$

Expand the left-hand side:

$$S_0 K_s + S_0 S_e - S_e K_s - S_e^2 = k \cdot X \cdot \theta \cdot S_e$$

### Step 4 — Quadratic in $S_e$

Rearrange into standard quadratic form $a S_e^2 + b S_e + c = 0$:

$$S_e^2 + S_e(K_s - S_0 + k X \theta) - S_0 K_s = 0$$

Solving via the quadratic formula:

$$S_e = \frac{-(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta) + \sqrt{(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta)^2 + 4 S_0 K_s}}{2}$$

This is the **fundamental CBBR design equation** linking substrate removal to operational parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "+" root is taken** because $S_e$ must be non-negative (substrate cannot be negative in concentration terms).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A CBBR treats **$Q = 1000$ m³/d** of municipal wastewater with $S_0 = 300$ mg/L BOD. Kinetic constants are $k = 5$ d⁻¹, $K_s = 60$ mg/L, $X = 8000$ mg VSS/L, $Y = 0.6$, $k_d = 0.05$ d⁻¹. Required effluent $S_e \le 20$ mg/L. Design the reactor.

**Step 1 — Compute the discriminant term**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= (K_s - S_0 + kX\theta)^2 + 4 S_0 K_s \\
       &= (60 - 300 + 5 \cdot 8000 \cdot \theta)^2 + 4 \cdot 300 \cdot 60
\end{aligned}$$

We must iterate on $\theta$. Try $\theta = 0.05$ d (= 1.2 h):

$$\begin{aligned}
kX\theta &= 5 \cdot 8000 \cdot 0.05 = 2000 \\
K_s - S_0 + kX\theta &= 60 - 300 + 2000 = 1760 \\
\Delta &= 1760^2 + 72000 = 3{,}097{,}600 + 72{,}000 = 3{,}169{,}600 \\
\sqrt{\Delta} &\approx 1780.4 \\
S_e &= \frac{-1760 + 1780.4}{2} = \frac{20.4}{2} = 10.2 \text{ mg/L} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

The target of $S_e \le 20$ mg/L is **met with $\theta = 0.05$ d**.

**Step 2 — Reactor Volume**

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 1000 \cdot 0.05 = 50 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — F/M Ratio**

$$F/M = \frac{Q \cdot S_0}{V \cdot X} = \frac{1000 \cdot 300}{50 \cdot 8000} = 0.75 \text{ d}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This F/M is **higher** than the typical CBBR range (0.05 – 0.3 d⁻¹). The student should conclude that the **effective biomass on carriers** is much larger than the suspended $X = 8000$ mg/L; the attached biomass contribution is the dominant one.

**Step 4 — Daily Oxygen Demand (carbonaceous)**

$$O_2^{carbon} = 1.42 \cdot \Delta BOD = 1.42 \cdot 1000 \cdot (0.300 - 0.0102) = 1.42 \cdot 289.8 \approx 411.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Conventional Activated Sludge (for context)**

| Parameter | Activated Sludge | CBBR (this design) |
|---|---|---|
| Volume | ~ 250 m³ | **50 m³** |
| F/M | 0.3 d⁻¹ | 0.75 (apparent) |
| SRT | 10 d | 30 – 60 d |
| Sludge yield | 0.4 kg/kg | 0.25 – 0.35 kg/kg |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CBBR vs. Other Biofilm Reactors

| Feature | CBBR | MBBR | Trickling Filter | RBC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier motion | **Full circulation** | Partial (stirred) | Stationary | Rotating |
| Biomass (g/L) | **10 – 20** | 3 – 6 | 1 – 3 | 2 – 5 |
| Footprint | **Smallest** | Small | Large | Medium |
| Recirculation required | **Yes (high R)** | No | Sometimes | No |
| Nitrification capability | **Excellent** | Good | Moderate | Good |
| Clogging risk | **None** | Low | High | Medium |
| Power consumption | **High** | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Typical application | High-strength leachate, RAS | Municipal STP | Rural STP | Industrial effluent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CBBR Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INF["INFLUENT ZONE"]
        IN["Raw Wastewater Q, S0"]
    end

    subgraph DOWNCOMER["DOWNCOMER (Anoxic / Low DO)"]
        DN1["Carrier Settling Zone"]
        DN2["Denitrification NO3 to N2"]
    end

    subgraph RISER["RISER (Aerobic / High DO)"]
        RS1["Air Diffuser 2 to 6 mg per L DO"]
        RS2["Biofilm Carriers Fluidized"]
        RS3["Nitrification NH4 to NO3"]
        RS4["Organic Removal BOD Uptake"]
    end

    subgraph SEP["TOP SEPARATOR"]
        SP1["Gas Escape"]
        SP2["Carrier Recycle to Downcomer"]
    end

    subgraph EFF["EFFLUENT ZONE"]
        OUT["Treated Effluent Se less than 20 mg per L"]
    end

    IN --> DN1
    DN1 --> DN2
    DN2 --> RS1
    RS1 --> RS2
    RS2 --> RS3
    RS3 --> RS4
    RS4 --> SP1
    SP1 --> SP2
    SP2 --> DN1
    SP1 --> OUT

    classDef zone fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    class INF,DOWNCOMER,RISER,SEP,EFF zone
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Mass Transfer & Reaction Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Bulk Liquid Substrate Sb"] -->|"External Diffusion kL"| B["Liquid Biofilm Interface Ss"]
    B -->|"Internal Diffusion Df"| C["Biofilm Depth z"]
    C -->|"Monod Reaction k, Ks"| D["Microbial Cell Utilization"]
    D --> E["End Products CO2 H2O Biomass"]

    classDef step fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E step
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Tree — CBBR Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CBBR["Circulating Bed Biofilm Reactors"]
    CBBR --> P1["Aerobic CBBR - Single Riser"]
    CBBR --> P2["Anoxic Oxic CBBR - Dual Loop"]
    CBBR --> P3["Three Phase CBBR - Air Water Biomass"]
    CBBR --> P4["Airlift CBBR - Internal Circulation"]
    P2 --> A1["Simultaneous Nitrification Denitrification SND"]
    P4 --> A2["Compact Footprint RAS Applications"]
    P3 --> A3["Industrial Leachate Treatment"]

    classDef root fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef var fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    class CBBR root
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 var
    class A1,A2,A3 app
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Total marks: 2 × 3 = 6**

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Circulating Bed Biofilm Reactor (CBBR). List any four key advantages over conventional activated sludge process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A CBBR is a three-phase (gas–liquid–solid) attached-growth bioreactor in which small biocarrier particles (0.2–0.5 mm) are kept in continuous fluidized circulation by aeration and/or high recycle flow. The carriers are colonized by biofilms that perform carbon oxidation, nitrification, and denitrification in a single vessel.

> **Advantages (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. High biomass concentration (10,000 – 20,000 mg VSS/L) without sludge recycling.
> 2. Compact footprint — about **1/5** of activated-sludge volume for the same load.
> 3. Simultaneous nitrification–denitrification due to riser/downcomer DO gradient.
> 4. Tolerant to hydraulic and toxic shock loads.
> 5. No clogging (carriers are free-moving).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of biocarrier media in a CBBR. List three important carrier properties that affect reactor performance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Role (1.5 marks):** Biocarriers provide a large protected surface area for microbial attachment, enabling biofilm formation. They act as mobile substrates that physically transport biomass between aerobic (riser) and anoxic (downcomer) zones, facilitating simultaneous carbon removal and nitrogen transformation.

> **Carrier Properties (3 × 0.5 = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Density** — should be slightly less than water (0.9 – 1.05 g/cm³) for easy fluidization.
> 2. **Specific surface area** — higher area means more biofilm per unit volume (typical 2000 – 5000 m²/m³).
> 3. **Diameter and shape** — small (0.2 – 0.5 mm) and rough/porous for better colonization.
> 4. **Chemical inertness** — non-toxic, non-biodegradable HDPE or PE.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal choice: Answer either Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Adapted]**

#### **(a)** Describe the working principle of a Circulating Bed Biofilm Reactor with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of the riser and downcomer zones. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Definition & Concept (2 marks):**
A CBBR is a three-phase attached-growth system in which biofilm-coated micro-carriers circulate continuously between an aerobic *riser* (driven by air bubbles) and an anoxic *downcomer* (gravity-driven). The reactor operates as a vertical loop, providing simultaneous organic carbon removal and biological nitrogen removal.

**2. Riser Zone (2 marks):**
- Aeration at the base (DO = 2 – 6 mg/L) fluidizes the carriers.
- Carriers move upward, exposing biofilm to oxygen.
- **Nitrification** occurs: $NH_4^+ \to NO_2^- \to NO_3^-$.
- **Carbonaceous BOD removal** via heterotrophic biofilm.
- Air bubbles attach to carriers, increasing buoyancy.

**3. Downcomer Zone (2 marks):**
- Bubbles disengage at the top, carriers lose buoyancy and sink.
- DO drops (< 0.5 mg/L), creating anoxic conditions.
- **Denitrification** occurs: $NO_3^- \to N_2 \uparrow$.
- Internal carbon from influent feeds denitrifiers.

**4. Circulation Loop (1 mark):**
A single particle completes a full cycle in **30 – 120 seconds**, providing a high-frequency alternation between oxic and anoxic microenvironments — ideal for **SND** (simultaneous nitrification–denitrification).

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for the labelled architecture.

---

#### **(b)** A CBBR is to treat a pharmaceutical effluent of flow **500 m³/d** with influent BOD = **1200 mg/L** and NH₄⁺–N = **180 mg/L**. Required effluent standards: BOD ≤ **30 mg/L** and NH₄⁺–N ≤ **10 mg/L**. Kinetic parameters: $k = 4$ d⁻¹, $K_s = 50$ mg/L, $X = 12{,}000$ mg/L. Calculate (i) the required HRT, (ii) the reactor volume, and (iii) the daily oxygen demand for both carbonaceous and nitrogenous loads. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) HRT Calculation (2.5 marks):**

Use the design equation solved for $\theta$:

$$S_e = \frac{-(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta) + \sqrt{(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta)^2 + 4 S_0 K_s}}{2}$$

Iterate with $\theta = 0.04$ d:

$$\begin{aligned}
kX\theta &= 4 \cdot 12000 \cdot 0.04 = 1920 \\
K_s - S_0 + kX\theta &= 50 - 1200 + 1920 = 770 \\
\Delta &= 770^2 + 4 \cdot 1200 \cdot 50 = 592900 + 240000 = 832900 \\
\sqrt{\Delta} &\approx 912.6 \\
S_e &= \frac{-770 + 912.6}{2} = \frac{142.6}{2} = 71.3 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}$$

$S_e = 71.3$ mg/L > 30 mg/L. **Increase HRT**. Try $\theta = 0.07$ d:

$$\begin{aligned}
kX\theta &= 4 \cdot 12000 \cdot 0.07 = 3360 \\
K_s - S_0 + kX\theta &= 50 - 1200 + 3360 = 2210 \\
\Delta &= 2210^2 + 240000 = 4884100 + 240000 = 5124100 \\
\sqrt{\Delta} &\approx 2263.6 \\
S_e &= \frac{-2210 + 2263.6}{2} = \frac{53.6}{2} = 26.8 \text{ mg/L} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

So **HRT $\theta \approx 0.07$ d ≈ 1.7 hours**.

**(ii) Reactor Volume (1.5 marks):**

$$V = Q \cdot \theta = 500 \cdot 0.07 = 35 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Daily Oxygen Demand (3 marks):**

**Carbonaceous:**
$$O_2^{carbon} = 1.42 \cdot Q \cdot (S_0 - S_e) = 1.42 \cdot 500 \cdot (1.200 - 0.0268) = 1.42 \cdot 586 = 832.1 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

**Nitrogenous (full nitrification):**
$$O_2^{nitro} = 4.57 \cdot Q \cdot (\Delta NH_4^+) = 4.57 \cdot 500 \cdot (0.180 - 0.010) = 4.57 \cdot 85 = 388.5 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

**Total daily O₂ demand:**
$$O_2^{total} = 832.1 + 388.5 = 1220.6 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{d}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a)** Compare CBBR with MBBR and Trickling Filter with respect to (i) biomass concentration, (ii) footprint, (iii) clogging tendency, and (iv) energy consumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

| Criterion | CBBR | MBBR | Trickling Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Biomass (g VSS/L)** | **10 – 20** (highest) | 3 – 6 | 1 – 3 |
| **Footprint** | **Smallest (1/5 of ASP)** | Compact | Largest (2 – 3× CBBR) |
| **Clogging Tendency** | **None** (moving carriers) | Very low | High (slime bridging) |
| **Energy Consumption** | **Highest** (high recycle) | Moderate | Lowest (gravity) |
| **Recirculation Needed** | **Yes — high** (R = 5–40) | No | Optional |
| **Nitrification** | **Excellent (SND)** | Good | Moderate |
| **Sludge Handling** | **Low yield** | Low yield | Periodic sloughing |

**Key Insights (1 mark):**
- CBBR is the **most compact** but **most energy-intensive** of the three.
- Trickling filter is **cheapest to operate** but occupies the largest land area — impractical in dense urban Indian cities.
- MBBR is the **balanced compromise** and is widely used in KTU-relevant Indian STPs.

---

#### **(b)** Derive the steady-state CBBR design equation for effluent substrate concentration $S_e$ from a Monod-kinetic mass balance. Clearly state assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady-state operation.
2. Completely mixed reactor (CSTR-equivalent for the riser).
3. Constant biomass concentration on carriers ($X$).
4. Monod kinetics for substrate utilization: $r_{su} = \dfrac{k \cdot S_e \cdot X}{K_s + S_e}$.
5. No substrate in recycle stream.
6. No biomass growth/decay within one hydraulic cycle.

**Mass Balance (3 marks):**
At steady state, the rate of substrate removed = rate of substrate utilized:

$$Q \cdot S_0 - Q \cdot S_e = V \cdot \frac{k \cdot S_e \cdot X}{K_s + S_e}$$

Substitute $\theta = V / Q$:

$$\frac{S_0 - S_e}{\theta} = \frac{k \cdot S_e \cdot X}{K_s + S_e}$$

**Algebraic Rearrangement (2 marks):**
Cross-multiply and bring all terms to one side:

$$(S_0 - S_e)(K_s + S_e) = k \cdot X \cdot \theta \cdot S_e$$

Expand and collect $S_e^2$ terms:

$$S_e^2 + (K_s - S_0 + kX\theta) \cdot S_e - S_0 K_s = 0$$

**Final Design Equation:**
$$\boxed{\,S_e = \frac{-(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta) + \sqrt{(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta)^2 + 4 S_0 K_s}}{2}\,}$$

The **positive root** is taken because $S_e$ is non-negative.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in CBBR Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing HRT ($\theta$) with SRT ($\theta_c$).** CBBR decouples these. A 1-mark penalty is common for mixing them.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 1.42 in carbonaceous O₂ demand.** It represents the theoretical oxygen demand per unit BOD oxidized.
> 3. **Using the wrong stoichiometric coefficient 4.57 for nitrification.** Many students write 4.33 (which is for NO₂⁻ formation only).
> 4. **Iterating on $\theta$ instead of solving directly.** Always show **two iterations** with the first one rejecting and the second one satisfying the constraint.
> 5. **Missing the "positive root" justification** when solving the quadratic — KTU evaluators expect this explicitly.
> 6. **Not labelling the Mermaid / diagram properly.** Lose 1 mark for every unlabelled arrow.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CBBR (Module 3, PEBBT864)**

- ✅ CBBR is a **three-phase** (gas–liquid–solid) **fluidized-bed** biofilm reactor with **continuous carrier circulation**.
- ✅ Carriers: **0.2 – 0.5 mm**, density **0.9 – 1.05 g/cm³**, surface area **2000 – 5000 m²/m³**.
- ✅ **Riser** = aerobic (DO 2 – 6 mg/L) → **nitrification + BOD removal**.
- ✅ **Downcomer** = anoxic (DO < 0.5 mg/L) → **denitrification**.
- ✅ Circulation time per particle: **30 – 120 s**; recycle ratio **R = 5 – 40**.
- ✅ HRT is **hours**, but SRT is **20 – 100 days** (decoupling is the chief advantage).
- ✅ Biomass concentration: **10,000 – 20,000 mg VSS/L** — 5–10× activated sludge.
- ✅ Footprint reduction: **~ 1/5** of conventional activated-sludge plant.
- ✅ Monod design equation: $S_e = \dfrac{-(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta) + \sqrt{(K_s - S_0 + kX\theta)^2 + 4 S_0 K_s}}{2}$.
- ✅ Carbonaceous O₂ demand: **$1.42 \cdot \Delta$BOD**; Nitrogenous: **$4.57 \cdot \Delta$NH₄⁺–N**.
- ✅ Biofilm flux: $J = D_f (S - S_s)/L_f$; Effectiveness factor $\eta = \tanh(\phi)/\phi$; Thiele modulus $\phi = L_f \sqrt{k_{0,f}/(D_f S_s)}$.
- ✅ **No clogging**, **no sludge recycling**, but **high energy** cost for aeration/recycle.
- ✅ Applications: high-strength industrial effluent, landfill leachate, aquaculture RAS, municipal retrofits.
- ✅ Key CBBR variants: **Aerobic single-riser**, **Anoxic–Oxic dual loop**, **Airlift internal circulation**, **Three-phase fluidized**.
- ✅ For KTU Part B: always show **two iterations of HRT** in numericals and justify the **positive root** of the quadratic.

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — Circulating Bed Biofilm Reactors*
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education framework.*
