KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style, 2024 Scheme]** #### **Q1. Define dehalogenation. List the four major mechanisms of microbial dehalogenation with one example each. [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Dehalogenation is the enzymatic or chemical process by which one or more halogen atoms (F, Cl, Br, I) are cleaved from an organic molecule, typically being replaced by a hydrogen atom, hydroxyl group, or eliminated as a halide ion. It is the critical first step in the biodegradation of persistent organohalide pollutants. The four major mechanisms are: 1. **Reductive dehalogenation** — example: PCE → TCE → *cis*-DCE → VC → ethene (by *Dehalococcoides mccartyi* under anaerobic conditions) 2. **Oxidative dehalogenation** — example: 4-chlorobenzoate → 4-hydroxybenzoate (by *Pseudomonas* under aerobic conditions) 3. **Hydrolytic (substitutive) dehalogenation** — example: 1-chlorobutane → 1-butanol (by *Xanthobacter autotrophicus* haloalkane dehalogenase DhlA) 4. **Intramolecular dehydrohalogenation** — example: γ-hexachlorocyclohexane (lindane) → 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene (by *Sphingomonas paucimobilis* LinA) --- #### **Q2. What is halorespiration? Why is it ecologically important in chlorinated solvent bioremediation? [3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Halorespiration (also called dehalorespiration) is a form of **anaerobic respiration** in which a halogenated organic compound (most commonly a chlorinated aliphatic or aromatic compound) serves as the **terminal electron acceptor** instead of oxygen, nitrate, sulfate, or carbon dioxide. The microbe conserves energy through the reductive dehalogenation reaction, which is catalyzed by specialized **reductive dehalogenase (RdhA)** enzymes containing cobalamin (vitamin B₁₂) and iron-sulfur cofactors. Ecological and engineering importance in chlorinated solvent bioremediation: 1. It is the **only known biological process** capable of completely dechlorinating PCE and TCE all the way to non-toxic ethene — without halorespiration, vinyl chloride (a known human carcinogen) would accumulate as a dead-end intermediate. 2. It enables **intrinsic bioremediation** of dense non-aqueous phase liquid (DNAPL) source zones in deep anoxic aquifers, where no oxygen is present. 3. It underpins the engineered strategy of **bioaugmentation with *Dehalococcoides mccartyi*** combined with electron donor (lactate, ethanol, H₂) injection — the gold standard for *in-situ* remediation of chloroethene-contaminated sites. --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – ESE Style, 2024 Scheme]** > *Module Internal Choice: Answer ONE of the following* --- #### **✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Pathway-Oriented Long Answer** **(a)** Describe the **sequential reductive dechlorination of perchloroethylene (PCE)** to ethene. Include in your answer the four intermediate compounds, the responsible reductive dehalogenase enzymes, the electron donor used, and a balanced overall stoichiometric equation. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** With the help of a suitable pathway diagram, explain the **microbial degradation of lindane (γ-HCH)** by *Sphingomonas paucimobilis* UT26. Name at least four enzymes involved and indicate the type of dehalogenation reaction each catalyzes. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### **Model Solution to Question A (a):** Sequential reductive dechlorination of PCE to ethene proceeds through four discrete two-electron reduction steps, each removing one chlorine atom and replacing it with hydrogen: **Step 1 — PCE to TCE** $$\text{C}_2\text{Cl}_4 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{pceA (PceA)}} \text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3 + \text{HCl}$$ **Step 2 — TCE to *cis*-DCE** $$\text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{tceA (TceA)}} \text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{HCl}$$ **Step 3 — *cis*-DCE to Vinyl Chloride (VC)** $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{vcrA (VcrA)}} \text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{Cl} + \text{HCl}$$ **Step 4 — VC to Ethene** $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{bvcA (BvcA)}} \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{HCl}$$ **Overall balanced equation:** $$\text{C}_2\text{Cl}_4 + 4\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 4\text{HCl}$$ or in ionic form: $$\text{C}_2\text{Cl}_4 + 4\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 4\text{H}^{+} + 4\text{Cl}^{-}$$ The complete reaction requires **4 moles of H₂** as the electron donor, releases **4 protons and 4 chloride ions**, and is catalyzed by four distinct reductive dehalogenase isoenzymes (PceA, TceA, VcrA, BvcA), all of which are encoded by *Dehalococcoides mccartyi*. The intermediate **vinyl chloride (VC)** is of particular environmental concern because it is a confirmed human carcinogen — this is why the complete dechlorination to ethene is the regulatory target. ##### **Model Solution to Question A (b):** Lindane (γ-hexachlorocyclohexane, γ-HCH, C₆H₆Cl₆) is degraded aerobically by *Sphingomonas paucimobilis* UT26 through a well-characterized *lin* gene pathway. The major steps and enzymes are: | Step | Substrate | Product | Enzyme | Reaction Type | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | γ-HCH | 1,2,4-TCB (via pentachlorocyclohexene and tetrachlorocyclohexadiene) | **LinA** | Dehydrochlorination (intramolecular) | | 2 | 1,2,4-TCB | 2,4,5-trichlorophenol → dichlorocatechol | **LinB** | Hydrolytic dehalogenation | | 3 | 2,5-dichlorohydroquinone | 2-chloromaleylacetate (or equivalent chlorocatechol) | **LinC** | Oxidative (NADH-dependent dehydrogenase) | | 4 | Chlorocatechol / ring-cleaved intermediate | TCA-cycle intermediates → CO₂ | **LinD, LinE, LinF** | Ring cleavage + further dechlorination | | 5 | Final chlorinated intermediates | CO₂ + H₂O + Cl⁻ | **LinG, LinH, LinJ** | Downstream mineralization | The overall mineralization is: $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6\text{Cl}_6 + 7.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 6\,\text{Cl}^{-} + 6\,\text{H}^{+}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common mistake is to claim that LinA performs hydrolytic dehalogenation. It does not — LinA is a **dehydrochlorinase** (intramolecular), eliminating HCl across two adjacent carbons to introduce a C=C double bond, not adding water. --- #### **✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Microbial & Engineering Focus)** **(a)** Compare and contrast **reductive, oxidative, hydrolytic, and intramolecular dehalogenation** with respect to: (i) electron requirements, (ii) change in carbon oxidation state, (iii) oxygen condition, and (iv) one representative microbial genus for each. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the **role of *Dehalococcoides mccartyi*** in environmental biotechnology. Explain why this organism is considered the "key player" in chlorinated solvent bioremediation, and describe the engineering strategy of bioaugmentation used in field applications. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### **Model Solution to Question B (a):** | Feature | Reductive | Oxidative | Hydrolytic | Intramolecular | |---|---|---|---|---| | **(i) Electron requirement** | Requires 2 e⁻ per C–X bond cleaved | Releases 2 e⁻ (oxidation) per C–X bond cleaved | No net electron change | No net electron change | | **(ii) Carbon oxidation state** | Decreased (formally by 2 per Cl removed) | Increased (formally by 2 per Cl removed) | Unchanged | Unchanged | | **(iii) Oxygen condition** | Strictly anaerobic | Strictly aerobic | Either | Either | | **(iv) Representative genus** | *Dehalococcoides* | *Pseudomonas* | *Xanthobacter* | *Sphingomonas* | Reductive dehalogenation therefore requires a strong electron donor (commonly H₂, formate, or lactate) and operates in anoxic environments. Oxidative dehalogenation requires molecular O₂ as a co-substrate and is mediated by oxygenase enzymes. Hydrolytic dehalogenation uses water as the nucleophile and the halogen is replaced by –OH, with no change in carbon's oxidation state. Intramolecular dehydrohalogenation eliminates HX across adjacent carbons, generating an alkene without any external electron donor or acceptor. ##### **Model Solution to Question B (b):** *Dehalococcoides mccartyi* is a tiny, slow-growing, strictly anaerobic bacterium belonging to the phylum Chloroflexi. Its ecological and engineering importance is unique for the following reasons: **Why it is the "key player":** 1. **Only organism in pure culture** that can perform the **complete sequential reductive dechlorination** of PCE all the way to **ethene** (C₂H₄), a non-toxic, naturally occurring plant hormone. Every other known dechlorinator stalls at some intermediate — most commonly at *cis*-DCE or VC, both of which are toxic and regulated. 2. It possesses a remarkable **reductive dehalogenase gene repertoire** — the genome of strain 195 contains 17 *rdhA*-like genes, of which pceA, tceA, vcrA, and bvcA are functionally characterized for distinct dechlorination steps. 3. It uses **halorespiration** — coupling the dechlorination reaction to ATP synthesis with the organochlorine as the terminal electron acceptor and H₂ as the electron donor. It is therefore an **organohalide-respiring** chemoautotroph in practical terms. **Engineering strategy of bioaugmentation:** 1. **Site characterization** — groundwater and soil are sampled to determine the extent of PCE/TCE contamination, redox conditions (Eh < −100 mV is required), pH, and the presence of indigenous dechlorinators. 2. **Amendment injection** — an electron donor (lactate, ethanol, or molasses) and essential nutrients (N, P, trace vitamins) are injected through dedicated wells to create and maintain strongly reducing conditions and provide H₂ via fermentative reactions. 3. **Bioaugmentation** — a cultured *Dehalococcoides* consortium (e.g., KB-1, SDC-9, or Bio-Dechlor INOCULUM) is injected into the aquifer to ensure that organisms capable of complete dechlorination to ethene are present at sufficient density. 4. **Long-term monitoring** — concentrations of PCE, TCE, *cis*-DCE, VC, ethene, chloride, dissolved H₂, and oxidation-reduction potential (ORP) are measured periodically over months to years until the regulatory cleanup level is achieved. This combined **biostimulation + bioaugmentation** approach is the field-proven engineering standard, applied successfully at hundreds of chlorinated solvent sites worldwide. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating reductive with oxidative dehalogenation.** A frequent error is to claim PCE is "oxidized" to CO₂. Under anaerobic conditions, it is **reductively dechlorinated** to ethene; oxidation only occurs in the aerobic cometabolic pathway (which produces TCE epoxide, a toxic intermediate). > 2. **Stopping the pathway at *cis*-DCE or VC.** Stating that "PCE is degraded to DCE" is incomplete. The engineering goal is always **complete dechlorination to ethene**; partial dechlorination leaves vinyl chloride, a known human carcinogen. > 3. **Forgetting the H₂ source.** Many students write reductive dechlorination stoichiometry without specifying the **electron donor**. Always couple the dechlorination half-reaction with H₂ oxidation (or formate, lactate fermentation) to be complete. > 4. **Wrong cofactors for reductive dehalogenase.** Reductive dehalogenases contain **cobalamin (vitamin B₁₂)** and **iron-sulfur clusters**, not heme or FAD. Writing "FAD" or "NADH" as the cofactor for PceA is a common mistake. > 5. **Mixing up LinA and LinB.** LinA is a **dehydrochlorinase** (no water needed, releases HCl); LinB is a **haloalkane dehalogenase** (uses water, releases Cl⁻). Reversing their roles in the lindane pathway will lose marks. --- ### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Last-Minute Summary — Pin This in Your Brain Before the Exam:** - **Dehalogenation = removal of halogen from an organic molecule.** It is the rate-limiting step in biodegradation of organohalide pollutants. - **Four mechanisms:** Reductive, Oxidative, Hydrolytic, Intramolecular (dehydrohalogenation). - **Reductive dehalogenation** is **anaerobic**, **energy-yielding** (halorespiration), and the **only** known biological route to complete dechlorination of PCE to ethene. - **Key microbe: *Dehalococcoides mccartyi*** — the only pure-culture organism that does PCE → TCE → *cis*-DCE → VC → ethene completely. - **Key enzymes for reductive dechlorination:** PceA, TceA, VcrA, BvcA — all are cobalamin-dependent reductive dehalogenases. - **Key enzymes for hydrolytic dehalogenation:** DhlA (haloalkane dehalogenase from *Xanthobacter*); LinB (lindane-specific). - **Key enzymes for dehydrohalogenation:** LinA (lindane-specific, releases HCl, no water needed). - **PCE → Ethene overall:** $C_2Cl_4 + 4H_2 \rightarrow C_2H_4 + 4HCl$ - **Lindane → CO₂ overall:** $C_6H_6Cl_6 + 7.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 3H_2O + 6Cl^- + 6H^+$ - **Electron donor for halorespiration:** H₂ (the universal donor); *in situ* it is generated by fermentation of lactate, ethanol, or molasses. - **Cl⁻ release is the field fingerprint** of reductive dechlorination — monitoring chloride is a standard performance metric. - **Vinyl chloride (VC) is the most dangerous intermediate** — it is a known human carcinogen, so the engineering target must always be complete dechlorination to ethene. - **Bioaugmentation + biostimulation** = the standard *in-situ* remediation recipe for chlorinated solvent sites. - **PFAS (PFOA, PFOS)** are the major exception — no proven complete microbial dehalogenation pathway is known; the C–F bond is the strongest single bond to carbon. - **KTU's "favorite microbe" answer:** Any question on "complete reductive dechlorination of PCE to ethene" → answer *Dehalococcoides mccartyi*. It is the only organism in pure culture capable of this complete chain. > **End of KTU 2024 Module 4 Notes — Dehalogenation** ✅

topics.title: Cometabolism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cometabolism # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Cometabolism** is defined as the *fortuitous* transformation of a non-growth substrate (co-substrate) by microbial enzymes (typically oxygenases) that are synthesized and regulated during the metabolism of a *different* primary substrate which *does* support microbial growth. The co-substrate neither yields carbon, energy, nor electrons for the cell; it is an incidental substrate whose oxidation provides no apparent benefit to the microorganism. The term was first formally introduced by **Leadbetter and Foster (1959–1960)** while studying the oxidation of ethane and other gaseous alkanes by *Methanomonas methanooxidans*. The co-substrate transformation is therefore **accidental** and **non-productive** in the strict biochemical sense, although it is of immense value in **bioremediation** engineering. ### 1.1.1 Key Terminological Distinctions | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Primary Substrate (Growth Substrate)** | The carbon and energy source that drives cell growth (e.g., methane, toluene, propane) | | **Co-substrate (Non-growth substrate)** | The compound that is incidentally oxidized without yielding energy/biomass (e.g., TCE, chloroform, PCBs) | | **Fortuitous Metabolism** | Synonymous with cometabolism — transformation occurring "by accident" | | **Auxiliary Substrate** | Sometimes used interchangeably with co-substrate | | **Nongrowth Substrate** | Same conceptual class as co-substrate | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garbage Truck on Errand" Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a delivery truck (the microbe) whose only business is delivering pizzas (the primary substrate — its real fuel). Along its delivery route, the exhaust pipe of the truck is so hot that it singes and chars any dry leaves lying on the road. The leaves (the co-substrate) are destroyed — but the truck did not set out to burn them, gains no fuel from them, and may even suffer minor engine stress from doing so. The leaves are *incidentally oxidized* — exactly how cometabolism works in a cell. The key intuitions are: 1. **The cell is not "trying" to use the co-substrate.** It cannot derive carbon or energy from it. 2. **The co-substrate is oxidized because the cell's enzymes are non-specific** (e.g., oxygenases have broad substrate range). 3. **The co-substrate transformation may even harm the cell** — the products (e.g., TCE epoxide) can be toxic, which is why cometabolism is often called a *suicidal pathway*. 4. **Primary substrate must keep flowing** — without it, cometabolic activity collapses. ## 1.3 Why Cometabolism Matters in Environmental Biotechnology > [!NOTE] > Cometabolism is the **biochemical foundation** of several industrial bioremediation technologies targeting compounds that are *notoriously recalcitrant* and cannot serve as sole carbon sources for any known microorganism: > - Trichloroethylene (TCE) — chlorinated solvent, US EPA priority pollutant > - Chloroform, dichloromethane, 1,1,1-trichloroethane > - Polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) > - Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) — partial oxidation > - Ammonia co-oxidation during nitrification > - Methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) Because most of these are *xenobiotic*, *toxic*, *non-biodegradable by direct metabolism*, cometabolism is often the **only biologically viable destruction pathway**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Biochemical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic Cometabolic transformation proceeds through a well-characterized enzymatic sequence. Using the **methanotrophic oxidation of TCE** as the canonical example (the most heavily studied cometabolic system): **Step 1 — Induction of oxygenase enzyme by primary substrate.** The microorganism (e.g., *Methylococcus capsulatus*, *Methylosinus trichosporium*) is grown on methane. The methane monooxygenase (MMO) enzyme is upregulated. **Step 2 — Non-specific binding of co-substrate.** MMO, an oxygenase with notoriously broad substrate specificity, binds TCE instead of (or in addition to) methane. Both substrates compete for the *same active site*. **Step 3 — Oxygen insertion and electron transfer.** A reductant (NADH) supplies 2 electrons. Molecular O₂ is split; one O atom is reduced to H₂O, the other is inserted into the substrate. **Step 4 — Formation of unstable intermediate.** For TCE, the product is **trichloroethylene epoxide (TCE-epoxide)** — a highly reactive, toxic intermediate. **Step 5 — Spontaneous or enzymatic decomposition.** TCE-epoxide rearranges (via Cl⁻ loss and hydrolysis) to form dichloroacetate, glyoxylate, CO, and eventually CO₂. **Step 6 — Cell toxicity feedback.** TCE-epoxide damages cellular proteins, membranes, and DNA → reduces cell viability. Primary substrate (CH₄) supply must be sustained to regenerate enzyme and biomass. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter?** Because the engineer must supply a continuous primary substrate (often costly — methane, propane, toluene injections in groundwater) and accept that the microbes will be partially inactivated by their own "accidental" products. ## 2.2 Types of Oxygenases Involved | Enzyme | Cofactor | Example Organism | Typical Co-substrates Transformed | |---|---|---|---| | **Soluble Methane Monooxygenase (sMMO)** | NADH, Fe²⁺ | *Methylococcus capsulatus* (Bath) | TCE, chloroform, DCM, alkanes, aromatics | | **Particulate Methane Monooxygenase (pMMO)** | Copper, NADH | *Methylosinus trichosporium* OB3b | TCE (slower), alkanes | | **Ammonia Monooxygenase (AMO)** | Copper, NADH | *Nitrosomonas europaea* | TCE, chloroform, 1,1-DCE | | **Toluene Dioxygenase / Monooxygenase** | NADH, Fe-S | *Pseudomonas putida* F1 | TCE, chlorobenzenes, PCBs | | **Propane Monooxygenase** | NADH | *Mycobacterium vaccae* JOB5 | TCE, MTBE | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** "**A**n **O**xygenase is **N**on-specific" — AON. This single fact is what enables cometabolism. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations describe the **rates, yields, and inhibition kinetics** commonly tested in KTU ESE questions on cometabolism. ### 2.3.1 Dual-Substrate Monod Kinetics (Cometabolic Model) When a primary substrate $S$ and a co-substrate $C$ are present together, both compete for the same oxygenase active site. The specific transformation rates are: $$ \begin{aligned} q_S &= \frac{q_{S,\max} \cdot S}{K_S + S + \left(\frac{K_S}{K_C}\right)C} \\[4pt] q_C &= \frac{q_{C,\max} \cdot C}{K_C + C + \left(\frac{K_C}{K_S}\right)S} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $q_S$ = specific primary substrate utilization rate (mg S / mg cells · day) - $q_C$ = specific co-substrate transformation rate (mg C / mg cells · day) - $q_{S,\max}$, $q_{C,\max}$ = maximum specific rates - $K_S$, $K_C$ = half-saturation constants (mg/L) - $S$, $C$ = substrate concentrations (mg/L) - The terms $\left(\frac{K_S}{K_C}\right)C$ and $\left(\frac{K_C}{K_S}\right)S$ represent **competitive inhibition** between primary and co-substrate. ### 2.3.2 Cometabolic Transformation Yield ($T_Y$) $$ T_Y = \frac{\Delta C_{\text{transformed}}}{\Delta S_{\text{consumed}}} \quad \left[\frac{\text{mg co-substrate}}{\text{mg primary substrate}}\right] $$ For methanotrophic TCE oxidation, $T_Y$ is typically **0.05–0.30** mg TCE per mg CH₄. ### 2.3.3 Substrate-to-Oxygen Stoichiometry For methane monooxygenase (MMO) reaction: $$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NAD}^+ $$ Co-substrate (TCE) reaction: $$ \text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3 + \text{O}_2 + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NAD}^+ $$ **Critical engineering insight:** Each mole of substrate oxidized consumes **1 mole of NADH**, which means ~**2 moles of CH₄** must be oxidized per mole of TCE to regenerate the necessary reducing equivalents via formaldehyde and formate dehydrogenase steps in methanotrophs. ### 2.3.4 Cell Inactivation by Toxic Co-substrate Products $$ \frac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - k_{\text{inact}} \cdot C \cdot X $$ Where: - $X$ = viable biomass concentration (mg/L) - $\mu$ = specific growth rate (day⁻¹) - $k_{\text{inact}}$ = inactivation rate constant (L/mg · day) - $C$ = co-substrate concentration (mg/L) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical design rule:** Maintain primary substrate (CH₄, NH₄⁺, toluene) supply to outpace inactivation, OR use a *resting cell* / *biofilm* configuration where the toxic product is flushed before harming the cells. ### 2.3.5 Competitive Inhibition Constant Summary | Co-substrate | $K_C$ (µM) | $T_Y$ (mol/mol) | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | TCE | 8–15 | 0.002–0.05 | High affinity, high toxicity | | Chloroform | 12–20 | 0.01–0.04 | Inactivates rapidly | | 1,1-DCE | 20–35 | 0.02–0.08 | Less toxic | | DCM | 5–10 | 0.04–0.10 | Supports growth after first oxidation | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **In-situ Bioremediation of TCE-contaminated aquifers:** Methane or propane injection wells stimulate indigenous methanotrophs to co-oxidize TCE. - **Biofilters for chlorinated VOCs:** Air-stripping columns seeded with *Pseudomonas* on toluene. - **Nitrification-coupled TCE degradation:** Wastewater treatment plants achieve incidental TCE removal during ammonia oxidation. - **Phytoremediation rhizosphere cometabolism:** Plant root exudates drive microbial co-oxidation of herbicides. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Trade-off Table (KTU favourite):** | Aspect | Advantage | Limitation | |---|---|---| | Substrate range | Attacks xenobiotics unreachable by direct metabolism | Requires constant primary substrate supply | | Specificity | Broad oxidation capability | Uncontrolled oxidation of harmless compounds | | Kinetics | Often rapid $q_C$ values | Subject to competitive inhibition | | Toxicity | Mineralizes pollutants to CO₂ | Suicide inactivation of cells by intermediates | | Economics | Low temperature/pressure; no chemicals | High operational cost of primary substrate | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Biomass and Substrate Balance for a Cometabolic Chemostat We consider a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR / chemostat) where: - Influent primary substrate concentration = $S_0$ (mg/L) - Influent co-substrate concentration = $C_0$ (mg/L) - Hydraulic dilution rate = $D$ (day⁻¹) - Steady-state biomass = $X$ (mg/L) - Steady-state effluent $S$, $C$ ### Step 1 — Write mass balance on biomass At steady state: accumulation = 0. $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= \text{rate in} - \text{rate out} + \text{rate of growth} - \text{rate of inactivation} \\[4pt] 0 &= 0 - DX + \mu X - k_{\text{inact}} C X \end{aligned} $$ Solving for steady-state: $$ D = \mu - k_{\text{inact}} C $$ ### Step 2 — Substituting Monod growth on primary substrate $$ \mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} $$ Thus the **operating dilution rate is constrained**: $$ D = \mu_{\max}\cdot\frac{S}{K_S + S} - k_{\text{inact}}\,C $$ ### Step 3 — Mass balance on primary substrate $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= D S_0 - D S - \frac{\mu X}{Y} \\[4pt] X &= Y\,(S_0 - S) \end{aligned} $$ where $Y$ is the true growth yield (mg biomass / mg substrate). ### Step 4 — Mass balance on co-substrate $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= D C_0 - D C - q_C \, X \\[4pt] C &= C_0 - \frac{q_C X}{D} \end{aligned} $$ where $q_C$ follows the competitive Monod form given in §2.3.1. ### Step 5 — Solving for steady-state co-substrate effluent concentration Substituting $X = Y(S_0 - S)$: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= C_0 - \frac{q_C \, Y (S_0 - S)}{D} \end{aligned} $$ And replacing $D$ from Step 2 and $q_C$ from §2.3.1: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= C_0 - \frac{Y (S_0 - S)}{\mu_{\max}\cdot\frac{S}{K_S+S} - k_{\text{inact}}\,C} \cdot \frac{q_{C,\max} \cdot C}{K_C + C + \left(\frac{K_C}{K_S}\right)S} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This implicit equation must be solved numerically (Newton-Raphson, bisection) for $C$. It is the **canonical model** used in KTU numerical problems and in research-grade cometabolic reactor design. ### Step 3.2 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style) **Given:** - $S_0 = 5$ mg/L (methane) - $C_0 = 2$ mg/L (TCE) - $\mu_{\max} = 0.4$ day⁻¹ - $K_S = 0.5$ mg/L - $K_C = 0.01$ mg/L - $q_{C,\max} = 0.3$ mg TCE / mg cells · day - $Y = 0.5$ mg biomass / mg CH₄ - $k_{\text{inact}} = 0.05$ L/mg · day - $D = 0.1$ day⁻¹ **Step A — Solve for $S$ at steady state using $D = \mu - k_{\text{inact}} C$ and assume $C \ll 1$ initially.** Approximation 1: neglect inactivation ($k_{\text{inact}} C \approx 0$). $$ \begin{aligned} 0.1 &= 0.4 \cdot \frac{S}{0.5 + S} \\[4pt] \frac{0.1}{0.4} &= \frac{S}{0.5+S} \\[4pt] 0.25 (0.5 + S) &= S \\[4pt] 0.125 + 0.25 S &= S \\[4pt] 0.125 &= 0.75 S \\[4pt] S &\approx 0.167 \text{ mg/L} \end{aligned} $$ **Step B — Compute steady-state biomass.** $$ \begin{aligned} X &= Y (S_0 - S) = 0.5 \times (5 - 0.167) \\[4pt] X &= 0.5 \times 4.833 = 2.416 \text{ mg/L} \end{aligned} $$ **Step C — Compute specific co-substrate rate $q_C$.** $$ \begin{aligned} q_C &= \frac{0.3 \cdot C}{0.01 + C + (0.01/0.5)\cdot 0.167} \\[4pt] q_C &= \frac{0.3 \, C}{0.01 + C + 0.00334} = \frac{0.3\, C}{0.01334 + C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step D — Solve for effluent $C$ using $C = C_0 - q_C X / D$.** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= 2 - \frac{(0.3\,C/(0.01334+C)) \times 2.416}{0.1} \\[4pt] C &= 2 - \frac{0.7248\, C}{0.1 (0.01334 + C)} \\[4pt] C &= 2 - \frac{7.248\, C}{0.01334 + C} \end{aligned} $$ Multiplying both sides by $(0.01334 + C)$: $$ \begin{aligned} C(0.01334 + C) &= 2(0.01334 + C) - 7.248\,C \\[4pt] 0.01334\,C + C^2 &= 0.02668 + 2C - 7.248\,C \\[4pt] C^2 + 0.01334\,C &= 0.02668 - 5.248\,C \\[4pt] C^2 + 5.261\,C - 0.02668 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Applying the quadratic formula: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \frac{-5.261 \pm \sqrt{(5.261)^2 + 4(0.02668)}}{2} \\[4pt] C &= \frac{-5.261 \pm \sqrt{27.678 + 0.107}}{2} \\[4pt] C &= \frac{-5.261 \pm 5.276}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Taking the positive root: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \frac{-5.261 + 5.276}{2} = \frac{0.015}{2} \approx 0.005 \text{ mg/L} \end{aligned} $$ **Step E — Interpretation.** - **Effluent TCE = 0.005 mg/L**, i.e., **99.75 % removal** from 2 mg/L. - **Effluent CH₄ ≈ 0.167 mg/L**, so most methane is consumed. - This confirms that methanotrophic cometabolism is highly efficient for TCE destruction when dilution rate is well below $\mu_{\max}$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Numerical problems on cometabolic chemostats frequently reduce to a quadratic in $C$ as shown. Always check the discriminant $\sqrt{b^2 + 4ac}$ for the positive root and discard the negative root (physically meaningless). ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Direct Metabolism vs Cometabolism | Criterion | Direct Metabolism | Cometabolism | |---|---|---| | Substrate role | Sole C & energy source | Non-growth, incidental | | Cell yield | Positive ($Y > 0$) | Zero or negative | | Enzyme specificity | High | Low (broad-specificity oxygenases) | | Rate-limiting step | Substrate uptake + catabolism | Reducing power (NADH) regeneration | | Energy investment | Moderate | High (primary substrate constantly needed) | | Toxic intermediates | Rare | Common (epoxides, aldehydes) | | Population dynamics | Exponential → stationary | Decay if primary substrate removed | | Industrial example | BOD removal in ASP | TCE destruction by methanotrophs | | Genetic basis | catabolic operons (e.g., *tol* plasmid) | Induced oxygenase with broad specificity | ## 3.4 Derivation Summary — Key Equations for Quick Reference | # | Equation | Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $D = \mu - k_{\text{inact}} C$ | Washout threshold with toxic co-substrate | | 2 | $X = Y(S_0 - S)$ | Steady-state biomass | | 3 | $q_C = q_{C,\max}\,C / [K_C + C + (K_C/K_S)S]$ | Co-substrate rate under competition | | 4 | $T_Y = \Delta C / \Delta S$ | Mass-based transformation yield | | 5 | $S^* = K_S D / (\mu_{\max} - D)$ | Steady-state primary substrate | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Cometabolic TCE Oxidation ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["PRIMARY SUBSTRATE
CH4 METHANE"] --> node2["METHANOTROPH CELL
METHYLOCOCCUS CAPSULATUS"] node3["CO-SUBSTRATE
C2HCL3 TCE"] --> node2 node2 --> node4["INDUCTION OF ENZYME
SOLUBLE MMO"] node4 --> node5["COMPETITIVE BINDING
AT ACTIVE SITE"] node5 --> node6["OXIDATION REACTION
NADH PLUS O2"] node6 --> node7["PRODUCT 1
CH3OH METHANOL
GROWTH PRODUCT"] node6 --> node8["PRODUCT 2
C2HCL3O TCE EPOXIDE
COMETABOLITE"] node8 --> node9["SPONTANEOUS REARRANGEMENT
DICHLOROACETATE PLUS GLYOXYLATE"] node9 --> node10["MINERALIZATION
CO2 PLUS CL- PLUS H2O"] node8 --> node11["TOXICITY FEEDBACK
CELL INACTIVATION"] node11 --> node12["MITIGATION
PRIMARY SUBSTRATE DOSING"] node12 --> node2 node7 --> node13["BIOMASS GROWTH
NEW CELL MATERIAL"] node13 --> node2 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Schematic — Cometabolic Reactor Engineering Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR feed1["CONTAMINATED
AQUIFER
TCE PLUS WATER"] --> pump1["INJECTION WELL
METHANE OR PROPANE"] pump1 --> zone1["BIODEGRADATION ZONE
METHANOTROPH BIOFILM"] zone1 --> monitor["MONITORING WELL
RESIDUAL TCE"] monitor --> outcome{"EFFLUENT
MEETS STANDARD"} outcome -->|YES| clean["CLEAN WATER
DISCHARGE"] outcome -->|NO| pump1 zone1 --> toxicity["TOXICITY CONTROL
FRESH BIOMASS ADDITION"] toxicity --> zone1 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Tree for Choosing Direct vs Cometabolic Bioremediation ```mermaid flowchart TD start["POLLUTANT IDENTIFIED"] --> q1{"CAN MICROBES
USE IT AS
SOLE CARBON SOURCE"} q1 -->|YES| direct["DIRECT METABOLISM
SELECTED
SCALE UP CSTR OR BIOFILM"] q1 -->|NO| q2{"COMPOUND
STRUCTURALLY SIMILAR
TO KNOWN
GROWTH SUBSTRATE"} q2 -->|YES| comet["COMETABOLISM
SELECTED
ENZYME INDUCTION NEEDED"] q2 -->|NO| q3{"ABIOtic OR
ADVANCED OXIDATION
VIABLE"} q3 -->|YES| abiotic["OZONATION OR
FENTON OR UV"] q3 -->|NO| discard["LANDFILL OR
CONTAINMENT"] comet --> prereq["ENSURE CONTINUOUS
PRIMARY SUBSTRATE
SUPPLY"] prereq --> comet ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define cometabolism. Who coined the term and in what context?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cometabolism (3 marks): > Cometabolism is the *fortuitous* transformation of a non-growth substrate (co-substrate) by enzymes that a microorganism produces while metabolizing a different primary substrate that supports its growth. The co-substrate is not used as a carbon or energy source and provides no metabolic benefit; its oxidation is an incidental consequence of non-specific oxygenase activity. The term was coined by **Leadbetter and Foster (1959–1960)** while studying the oxidation of ethane and other gaseous alkanes by the methanotroph *Methanomonas methanooxidans*. (1 mark — definition; 1 mark — Leadbetter and Foster; 1 mark — context of methanotrophic alkane oxidation.) ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]` **Differentiate between direct metabolism and cometabolism in microbial degradation of xenobiotics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Direct Metabolism (1.5 marks) | Cometabolism (1.5 marks) | |---|---|---| | Substrate as C/energy source | Yes — sole growth substrate | No — co-substrate is non-growth | | Cell yield on substrate | Positive | Zero / negative | | Enzyme specificity | Usually narrow | Broad (oxygenases) | | Examples | BOD removal in activated sludge | TCE oxidation by methanotrophs | ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Cometabolism Questions** > 1. Students frequently confuse **primary substrate** and **co-substrate** — getting these reversed loses 2 marks instantly. > 2. Forget to mention **NADH / reducing equivalents** → lose 1–2 marks because cometabolism is fundamentally limited by electron supply. > 3. Skip the **toxicity of intermediates** (epoxides) — examiners explicitly test this concept for 2–3 marks. > 4. In numerical problems, students forget to **convert units consistently** (µM ↔ mg/L) and lose 2 marks on the final answer. > 5. Always cite **Leadbetter and Foster** for definition-based sub-questions. ### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **Attempt any ONE of the following:** **(a)** Discuss in detail the biochemical mechanism of cometabolism with reference to methanotrophic degradation of trichloroethylene (TCE). Include the role of monooxygenases, stoichiometry, and the fate of intermediates. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A chemostat is operated for methanotrophic cometabolic degradation of TCE. Given the following data, calculate the steady-state effluent TCE concentration and verify whether washout occurs. | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $S_0$ (influent CH₄) | 5 mg/L | | $C_0$ (influent TCE) | 2 mg/L | | $\mu_{\max}$ | 0.4 day⁻¹ | | $K_S$ | 0.5 mg/L | | $K_C$ | 0.01 mg/L | | $q_{C,\max}$ | 0.3 mg TCE / mg cell · day | | $Y$ | 0.5 | | $k_{\text{inact}}$ | 0.05 L/mg · day | | $D$ | 0.1 day⁻¹ | **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) **1. Microbial system** *(1 mark)* — Methanotrophs such as *Methylococcus capsulatus* (Bath) and *Methylosinus trichosporium* OB3b oxidize methane as primary growth substrate. They possess soluble (sMMO) and particulate (pMMO) methane monooxygenases. **2. Enzyme properties** *(1 mark)* — sMMO is a non-heme iron enzyme with notoriously broad substrate specificity. It can oxidize alkanes, alkenes, aromatics, and chlorinated solvents. **3. Reaction stoichiometry** *(2 marks)* — $$ \text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NAD}^+ $$ $$ \text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3 + \text{O}_2 + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+ \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3\text{O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NAD}^+ $$ **4. Fate of intermediates** *(1.5 marks)* — TCE-epoxide spontaneously rearranges, losing Cl⁻, hydrolyzing to dichloroacetate, glyoxylate, CO, and ultimately CO₂ and chloride ions. **5. Toxicity feedback** *(1 mark)* — TCE-epoxide alkylates proteins and damages cell membranes. Inactivation kinetics: $dX/dt = \mu X - k_{\text{inact}} C X$. **6. Engineering implication** *(0.5 marks)* — Continuous methane supply required; biofilms or carrier-attached cells preferred to extend biomass half-life. #### Model Solution to (b) — Reference the derivation in §3.2 **Step 1:** Compute steady-state $S$ assuming $C \ll 1$. $$ \begin{aligned} 0.1 &= 0.4 \cdot \frac{S}{0.5 + S} \\ S &\approx 0.167 \text{ mg/L} \quad (1 \text{ mark}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Biomass concentration. $$ X = 0.5 \times (5 - 0.167) = 2.416 \text{ mg/L} \quad (1 \text{ mark}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Write mass balance on co-substrate $C$: $$ C = C_0 - \frac{q_C X}{D} \quad (1 \text{ mark}) $$ with $q_C$ from competitive Monod form. **(1 mark)** **Step 4:** Substitute and reduce to quadratic: $$ C^2 + 5.261 C - 0.02668 = 0 \quad (1 \text{ mark}) $$ **Step 5:** Solve using quadratic formula; positive root: $$ C = \frac{-5.261 + 5.276}{2} \approx 0.005 \text{ mg/L} \quad (1 \text{ mark}) $$ **Step 6:** Washout check. $D = 0.1$ day⁻¹; $\mu_{\max} = 0.4$ day⁻¹. Since $D < \mu_{\max}$ and $k_{\text{inact}} C = 0.05 \times 0.005 = 0.00025$ day⁻¹ $\ll D$, the chemostat operates **below washout**; biomass is sustained. **(1 mark)** > **Conclusion:** Effluent TCE = **0.005 mg/L** (99.75 % removal); washout does NOT occur. --- ### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]` **Attempt any ONE of the following:** **(a)** Explain the role of co-substrate competition, reducing-equivalent (NADH) regeneration, and product toxicity in the design and operation of cometabolic bioreactors. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast direct metabolism, cometabolism, and abiotic degradation as strategies for in-situ remediation of chlorinated solvents in contaminated groundwater. Discuss at least two limitations of cometabolism that must be overcome for field-scale deployment. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B-B (a) **1. Co-substrate competition** *(2 marks)* — TCE and methane compete for the same MMO active site. The competitive Monod model captures this: $$ q_C = \frac{q_{C,\max} C}{K_C + C + (K_C/K_S) S} $$ Engineering consequence: high primary-substrate concentration improves MMO induction but reduces TCE affinity. Optimal $S/C$ ratio is design-critical. **2. Reducing-equivalent (NADH) regeneration** *(2.5 marks)* — Each cometabolic oxidation consumes 1 mol NADH. Methanotrophs regenerate NADH via methanol → formaldehyde → formate → CO₂ steps, catalysed by methanol dehydrogenase, formaldehyde dehydrogenase, and formate dehydrogenase. Without continuous CH₄ oxidation, the cell cannot sustain cometabolic activity. **Implication:** Primary substrate loading rate must exceed co-substrate loading rate by a stoichiometric safety factor (typically 5–20 ×). **3. Product toxicity** *(2.5 marks)* — TCE-epoxide and other halohydrins/aldehydes inactivate cells. Counter-strategies: - Use **biofilms** to protect biomass. - Use **pulsed** rather than continuous contaminant loading. - Apply **silicone oil** or other partitioning carriers to keep contaminant concentration low in bulk water. - Add **healthy seed culture** periodically. #### Model Solution to B-B (b) **1. Direct metabolism** *(1 mark)* — Microbe uses pollutant as sole C source. Best when organism and substrate are well-matched (e.g., *Pseudomonas* on chlorobenzoate). Limited by recalcitrance. **2. Cometabolism** *(1 mark)* — As defined; non-growth oxidation. Requires primary substrate. **3. Abiotic degradation** *(1 mark)* — Chemical oxidation (O₃, Fenton, permanganate), photocatalysis. Fast, non-selective, but expensive and may generate toxic by-products. **4. Comparison table** *(1 mark)* — Substrate selectivity, energy requirement, capital cost, O&M cost, completeness of mineralization. **5. Limitations of cometabolism** *(2 marks)*: - **Constant primary substrate supply** — costly in remote aquifers. - **Product toxicity / suicide inactivation** — biomass turnover is high. **6. Overcoming limitations** *(1 mark)* — Genetic engineering of strains resistant to inactivation; use of biofilm reactors; selection of cheap growth substrates (e.g., propane injection); coupling with abiotic polishing. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Definition** — Cometabolism = fortuitous oxidation of a non-growth co-substrate by non-specific oxygenases induced by a primary substrate (Leadbetter & Foster, 1959). - **Primary substrate ≠ co-substrate** — reversing these costs marks. - **MMO, AMO, TMO** are the canonical cometabolic oxygenases. - **NADH stoichiometry** — 1 NADH per mole substrate oxidized. - **Reactive intermediates (epoxides)** — cause suicide inactivation. - **Competitive Monod equation** is the workhorse kinetic model. - **Transformation yield $T_Y$** — typically < 0.05 mol/mol for TCE. - **Biofilm / carrier-attached reactors** preferred over suspended cells. - **In-situ field applications** rely on methane, propane, or ammonia injection into aquifers. - **Coupling nitrification + TCE co-oxidation** is a "free" benefit in wastewater treatment plants. - **Leadbetter and Foster (1959–1960)** — must be cited for definition questions. - **Difference from direct metabolism**: cometabolism does NOT yield energy or carbon to the cell. - **Difference from co-metabolism in soil science**: in soil chemistry, "co-metabolism" sometimes means simultaneous metabolism of two substrates for energy — biology uses a stricter definition. - **KTU-favourite example**: methanotrophic oxidation of TCE = **canonical cometabolic system**. - **Practical design rule**: $D < \mu_{\max} - k_{\text{inact}} C$ to avoid washout. - **Always** include reducing-equivalent (NADH) regeneration in any cometabolism answer worth > 5 marks.

topics.title: Biotransformation of Metals, Bioremediation Principle - Biostimulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT864 - ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biotransformation of Metals, Bioremediation Principle - Biostimulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biotransformation of Metals — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biotransformation of Metals** refers to the microbially-mediated (or plant-mediated) alteration of the **oxidation state, speciation, solubility, or chemical form** of metal and metalloid species, resulting in a change in their **mobility, bioavailability, and toxicity** in the environment. It is a subset of biodegradation and is governed by enzymatic redox couples, complexation reactions, methylation, and precipitation pathways.

Metals cannot be "destroyed" by biological means (unlike organic pollutants), but their **chemical form and valence state** can be altered. This change in form often means the difference between a **bioavailable, toxic species** and a **stable, immobile, non-toxic precipitate**.

### 1.1.1 Key Mechanisms of Metal Biotransformation

| Mechanism | Core Change | Environmental Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Redox Transformation** | Change in oxidation state (e.g., Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺) | Reduces toxicity, mobility |
| **Methylation** | Addition of methyl (–CH₃) groups (e.g., Hg²⁺ → CH₃Hg⁺) | Often **increases** toxicity and volatility |
| **Complexation / Chelation** | Binding to organic ligands (e.g., siderophores) | Alters solubility and bioavailability |
| **Bio-precipitation** | Formation of insoluble sulfides, phosphates, hydroxides | Immobilizes metals in solid phase |
| **Bioaccumulation** | Intracellular sequestration | Enables removal via biomass harvest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Metal Makeover Studio":** Imagine a contaminated site as a *wardrobe of toxic metal costumes*. Microbes act as "costume designers" — they don't destroy the actors (the metal atoms), but they **redress them** in safer, less dangerous costumes (chemical forms). A toxic, soluble, mobile Cr⁶⁺ "outfit" can be changed into a harmless, insoluble, immobile Cr³⁺ "outfit" through microbial action.

## 1.2 Bioremediation — The Umbrella Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioremediation** is the engineered or natural process by which **biological systems (microorganisms, plants, or their enzymes)** transform, degrade, or remove environmental contaminants from soil, water, sediment, or air to restore ecosystem health.

## 1.3 Biostimulation — The Targeted Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biostimulation** is a specific bioremediation technique in which the **indigenous (native) microbial community** at a contaminated site is stimulated — by amending the environment with limiting nutrients, electron acceptors/donors, or co-substrates — to enhance their intrinsic metabolic capacity to degrade or transform contaminants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Microbial Cafeteria":** Imagine a contaminated site as a city of *resident microbes* (the workers) who are *starving* despite having a mountain of pollutant "groceries" they cannot fully use. **Biostimulation** is the act of *stocking the cafeteria* with vitamins, oxygen, and the right trace elements so the workers can process the pollutants faster. You don't hire new workers (that's **bioaugmentation**); you feed the ones already there!

## 1.4 Critical Distinction: Biostimulation vs. Bioaugmentation

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **differentiate biostimulation from bioaugmentation**. Mix-ups here are a guaranteed loss of marks. Memorize the *who* and the *what*:
> - **Biostimulation** = *Stimulating* **native** microbes by adding **nutrients/amendments**
> - **Bioaugmentation** = *Adding* **exogenous** (foreign) microbial strains/cultures

The **standard growth metric** used to quantify microbial activity in biostimulation studies is the **Optical Density at 600 nm (OD₆₀₀)**, a dimensionless absorbance value that correlates linearly with cell density in the mid-exponential growth phase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Biogeochemical Logic of Metal Biotransformation

Metal biotransformation is not arbitrary — it follows the **Thermodynamic Ladder of Electron Acceptors**. Microbes use metals as **terminal electron acceptors** during anaerobic respiration or as **electron donors** during chemolithotrophic metabolism, dictated by the redox potential (E₀′) of their environment.

### 2.1.1 The Redox Ladder (Decreasing E₀′)

The sequence of electron acceptors used by microbes follows this hierarchy, from most energetically favorable (top) to least favorable (bottom):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2 \text{ (Aerobic)} &\rightarrow \text{NO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{Mn}^{4+} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Mn⁴⁺ → Mn²⁺** (manganese reduction): common in anaerobic subsurface soils
- **Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺** (iron reduction): drives co-precipitation of arsenic and phosphates
- **SO₄²⁻ → S²⁻ → MetalS↓** (sulfate reduction): the canonical pathway for **bio-precipitation** of toxic metals as insoluble sulfides

### 2.1.2 Stepwise Logic of a Microbial Redox Biotransformation

1. **Step 1 — Substrate Oxidation:** The microbe oxidizes an organic substrate (e.g., lactate, acetate, glucose) — this is the **catabolic step** that releases electrons.
2. **Step 2 — Electron Shuttling:** Electrons are transferred via the **electron transport chain (ETC)** — through carriers like NADH, FADH₂, quinones, and cytochromes.
3. **Step 3 — Terminal Reduction:** Electrons reduce the metal species, lowering its oxidation state.
4. **Step 4 — Form Change & Fate:** The reduced metal may precipitate (e.g., Cr³⁺ as Cr(OH)₃), volatilize (e.g., methylated Hg), or change speciation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Redox biotransformation is the backbone of *in situ* remediation of acid mine drainage (AMD), where sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) precipitate dissolved toxic metals (Fe, Ni, Cu, Zn) as stable sulfide minerals.

## 2.2 The Logic of Biostimulation: Why It Works

Microbial degradation of pollutants in the field is typically **nutrient-limited** rather than organism-limited. The natural C:N:P ratio in pristine soil is approximately **100:10:1** (by mass), but a hydrocarbon spill can completely disrupt this balance. Biostimulation **rebalances the limiting factors** so that indigenous microbes can degrade contaminants at a rate limited only by their intrinsic biochemistry.

### 2.2.1 The Four Limiting Factors Addressed by Biostimulation

| Limiting Factor | Typical Amendment | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Nitrogen source** | NH₄NO₃, urea | Protein/nucleic acid synthesis |
| **Phosphorus source** | K₂HPO₄, Na₃PO₄ | ATP, phospholipid synthesis |
| **Electron acceptor** | O₂ (aeration), NO₃⁻, H₂O₂ | Respiration (aerobic/anaerobic) |
| **pH buffer / trace elements** | Lime, CaCO₃, micronutrients | Enzyme cofactor availability |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Biotransformation & Biostimulation

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations represent the **core quantitative toolkit** expected of a KTU 2024 student. Master these for the **Module 4** problem-solving section.

| Equation | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monod Growth Kinetics** | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹), $\mu_{max}$ = maximum $\mu$ (h⁻¹), $S$ = substrate conc. (mg/L), $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (mg/L) | Predicting microbial response to nutrient amendment |
| **Substrate Utilization Rate** | $-\dfrac{dS}{dt} = \dfrac{\mu_{max}}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$ | $Y$ = yield coefficient (mg cells/mg substrate), $X$ = biomass (mg/L) | Field-scale biostimulation dosing rate |
| **First-Order Decay** | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | $C_t$ = conc. at time $t$, $C_0$ = initial conc., $k$ = decay constant (day⁻¹) | Pollutant half-life in stimulated soil |
| **C:N:P Redfield-style Ratio** | $\dfrac{C}{N/P} = 100 \cdot \dfrac{10}{1}$ (ideal for hydrocarbon degradation) | Mass-based; informs fertilizer amendment mass | Designing biostimulation nutrient mix |
| **Biodegradation Rate (linear)** | $R = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_t)}{t}$ | $R$ = rate (mg/L·d), $C_0, C_t$ = initial & final conc., $t$ = elapsed time (days) | Bench-scale biostimulation validation |
| **Redox Nernst Equation** | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | $E$ = cell potential (V), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temp (K), $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = 96485 C/mol, $Q$ = reaction quotient | Predicting which metal will be reduced next |
| **Langmuir Biosorption** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_{max} \cdot K_L \cdot C_e}{1 + K_L \cdot C_e}$ | $q_e$ = sorbed amount (mg/g), $q_{max}$ = max capacity, $C_e$ = equilibrium conc. (mg/L), $K_L$ = Langmuir constant (L/mg) | Metal biosorption isotherm design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Valuation:** When writing the Nernst equation in your answer script, **convert temperature to Kelvin (T in K = 273.15 + °C)** before substituting. Using °C directly is a common, costly error.

## 2.4 Conceptual Flow: From Contaminant to Clean Site

> [!NOTE]
> In any KTU answer on biostimulation, the examiner expects to see a clear **logical chain**:
> **Contaminant identified** → **Limiting factor diagnosed** → **Amendment selected** → **Microbial activity enhanced** → **Pollutant transformed/degraded** → **Site remediated**.
> Skipping any link in this chain is a guaranteed deduction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometry of a Biostimulation Nutrient Dose

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation calculates how much ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) fertilizer must be added to a petroleum-contaminated soil to achieve the **ideal C:N:P ratio of 100:10:1** for optimal hydrocarbon biodegradation by indigenous microbes.

### Given (Sample Problem):
- Contaminated soil mass: $M_{soil}$ = **1000 kg**
- Initial Total Petroleum Hydrocarbon (TPH) concentration: $C_{TPH}$ = **50,000 mg/kg** (5 % by mass)
- Native soil N content (background): $N_{bg}$ = **0.1 %** by mass = **1000 mg/kg**
- Target C:N ratio: **100:10** (mass basis)

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Total mass of contaminant carbon in the soil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{C} &= C_{TPH} \times M_{soil} \\
M_{C} &= 50{,}000 \text{ mg/kg} \times 1000 \text{ kg} \\
M_{C} &= 5.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ mg} = 50{,}000 \text{ g} = 50 \text{ kg of carbon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The total mass of hydrocarbon-derived carbon in the 1000 kg of soil is 50 kg. This is the "food supply" the microbes will consume; therefore, the nitrogen dose must be scaled to this amount.

**Step 2 — Required total nitrogen based on the target C:N ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{required} &= M_{C} \times \dfrac{10}{100} \\
N_{required} &= 50 \text{ kg} \times 0.10 \\
N_{required} &= 5.0 \text{ kg of nitrogen}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** A C:N mass ratio of 100:10 means that for every 100 units of carbon, 10 units of nitrogen are needed. Multiplying the total carbon mass (50 kg) by the ratio (10/100 = 0.10) yields the nitrogen requirement.

**Step 3 — Nitrogen already present in the native soil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{native} &= N_{bg} \times M_{soil} \\
N_{native} &= 1000 \text{ mg/kg} \times 1000 \text{ kg} \\
N_{native} &= 1.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ mg} = 1.0 \text{ kg of N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The uncontaminated soil already contains 1.0 kg of nitrogen as background organic matter and nitrate. This nitrogen does not need to be added.

**Step 4 — Net nitrogen deficit to be supplemented:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{deficit} &= N_{required} - N_{native} \\
N_{deficit} &= 5.0 \text{ kg} - 1.0 \text{ kg} \\
N_{deficit} &= 4.0 \text{ kg of nitrogen}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The difference between the required nitrogen (5.0 kg) and the nitrogen already present (1.0 kg) is the additional nitrogen that must be supplied via the fertilizer amendment.

**Step 5 — Mass of ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃) fertilizer required:**

The molecular mass of NH₄NO₃ is **80 g/mol**, and the mass fraction of nitrogen (N) is **28/80 = 0.35 (35 %)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{NH_4NO_3} &= \dfrac{N_{deficit}}{\text{N mass fraction in NH}_4\text{NO}_3} \\
M_{NH_4NO_3} &= \dfrac{4.0 \text{ kg}}{0.35} \\
M_{NH_4NO_3} &= 11.43 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Since only 35 % of the ammonium nitrate's mass is actual nitrogen, the total fertilizer mass must be 4.0 / 0.35 ≈ 11.43 kg to deliver 4.0 kg of nitrogen. This is the engineered amendment dose.

### Final Engineering Answer:
**Approximately 11.43 kg of NH₄NO₃** must be added to the 1000 kg of contaminated soil to establish the target C:N ratio and **stimulate the indigenous hydrocarbon-degrading microbial community**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Predicting Microbial Growth Under Biostimulation (Monod Kinetics)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Monod equation** relates the specific growth rate of a microbial population to the concentration of the limiting substrate. It is the **central kinetic model** for biostimulation.

### Starting Equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Under low substrate (S ≪ K_s), approximate $K_s + S \approx K_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &\approx \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** When substrate is scarce, growth is approximately *first-order* in S — meaning growth rate scales linearly with substrate concentration.

**Step 2 — Under high substrate (S ≫ K_s), approximate $K_s + S \approx S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &\approx \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{S} = \mu_{max}
\end{aligned}
```

**Explanation:** When substrate is abundant, the growth rate saturates at the maximum value $\mu_{max}$, becoming *zero-order* in S. This is why over-amendment beyond microbial capacity is wasteful.

**Step 3 — Solving for the time required to halve the substrate (biostimulation half-life):**

Using $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ and setting $C_t = C_0 / 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C_0}{2} &= C_0 \cdot e^{-k t_{1/2}} \\
\dfrac{1}{2} &= e^{-k t_{1/2}} \\
\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) &= -k \cdot t_{1/2} \\
-0.693 &= -k \cdot t_{1/2} \\
t_{1/2} &= \dfrac{0.693}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The half-life of a pollutant under first-order biodegradation kinetics is independent of initial concentration — it is determined solely by the decay constant $k$, which biostimulation seeks to **maximize** by removing the limiting factors (nutrients, electron acceptors).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Choosing Biostimulation Amendments

| Site Diagnosis | Limiting Factor | Amendment Choice | Risk if Misapplied |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrocarbon spill, soil organic N < 0.1 % | Nitrogen | NH₄NO₃, urea | Eutrophication of runoff water |
| TPH/PAH spill, anaerobic, waterlogged | Electron acceptor | H₂O₂, Ca(NO₃)₂ | Soil sterilization at high H₂O₂ doses |
| Heavy metal + hydrocarbon co-contamination | Both N and electron donor | Lactate + NH₄NO₃ | Sulfate reduction may mobilize arsenic |
| pH < 4.0 in mine tailings | pH & alkalinity | CaCO₃, lime, compost | Metal re-mobilization if pH raised too quickly |
| High natural clay, low permeability | Hydraulic + nutrient access | Surfactants (rhamnolipids) | Groundwater surfactant plume migration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** When you present a biostimulation strategy, the **differential diagnosis** (Step 1) carries 30 % of the marks. Vague answers like *"add nutrients"* lose marks; specific answers like *"add 11.4 kg NH₄NO₃ to achieve C:N of 100:10 based on 50 kg carbon"* earn full credit.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Architecture of Microbial Metal Biotransformation

> [!NOTE]
> The following schematic illustrates the **decision tree** a microbial cell uses to transform a metal ion, from initial contact to final fate. Each node is a logically distinct biochemical stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Metal Ion Enters Microbial Zone] --> B1{Intrinsic or Enzymatic Contact?}
    B1 -->|Biosorption| C1[Cell Wall / EPS Binding]
    B1 -->|Enzymatic| C2[Redox Enzymes Engage]
    C1 --> D1[Metal Speciation Altered]
    C2 --> D2[Electron Transfer via ETC]
    D2 --> E1{Oxidation State Change}
    E1 -->|Reduction| F1[Reduced Metal Species]
    E1 -->|Oxidation| F2[Oxidized Metal Species]
    F1 --> G1[Precipitation as Sulfide or Hydroxide]
    F1 --> G2[Volatilization via Methylation]
    F2 --> G3[Complexation with Ligands]
    G1 --> H1[Immobilized in Soil Matrix]
    G2 --> H2[Released to Atmosphere]
    G3 --> H3[Mobile or Bioavailable]
    H1 --> I1[Stable Long-Term Sink]
    H2 --> I2[Atmospheric Dispersion]
    H3 --> I3[Possible Re-entry into Food Web]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Follow the path from top to bottom. The two major branch points are (1) **biosorption vs. enzymatic contact** and (2) **reduction vs. oxidation**, each leading to a different environmental fate.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Biostimulation Workflow (Field Implementation)

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level functional architecture shows the *engineered sequence* by which a contaminated site is remediated using biostimulation, from initial site characterization through post-remediation monitoring.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Site Characterization] --> S2[Contaminant Identification and Quantification]
    S2 --> S3[Microbial Community Profiling]
    S3 --> S4{Identify Limiting Factor}
    S4 -->|Nitrogen| S5[Amend with NH4NO3 or Urea]
    S4 -->|Phosphorus| S6[Amend with K2HPO4]
    S4 -->|Electron Acceptor| S7[Inject O2 H2O2 or NO3]
    S4 -->|pH Buffer| S8[Apply CaCO3 or Lime]
    S5 --> S9[Indigenous Microbes Stimulated]
    S6 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Enhanced Biodegradation of Pollutant]
    S10 --> S11[Periodic Monitoring of Contaminant Levels]
    S11 --> S12{Remediated to Target Level?}
    S12 -->|Yes| S13[Site Closure and Certification]
    S12 -->|No| S4
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow is *iterative* — if monitoring (S11) shows the contaminant has not reached the target level, the loop re-enters at the **limiting factor identification** step (S4). This adaptive feedback is a hallmark of *engineered* biostimulation.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Comparison of Three Bioremediation Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram contrasts **biostimulation**, **bioaugmentation**, and **intrinsic bioremediation** in terms of *intervention intensity* and *typical application scenarios*. Useful for KTU short-answer comparison questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Natural Attenuation No Intervention] --> P2[Background Microbial Activity]
    P2 --> P3[Slow Natural Cleanup over Years]
    P4[Biostimulation Add Nutrients and Amendments] --> P5[Native Microbes Activated]
    P5 --> P6[Moderate Cleanup Speed Months to Years]
    P7[Bioaugmentation Add Exogenous Microbes] --> P8[New Catabolic Capacity Introduced]
    P8 --> P9[Fastest Cleanup but Highest Cost]
    P3 --> P10[Final Endpoint Clean Site]
    P6 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
```

**Reading the diagram:** The three strategies form a **spectrum of intervention**, ranging from passive (natural attenuation, left) to active (bioaugmentation, right), with biostimulation as the engineered middle path.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Electron Flow in Metal Redox Biotransformation

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram shows the **electron transport architecture** during dissimilatory metal reduction, the most common biotransformation pathway for iron and manganese.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Organic Substrate Lactate Acetate] --> Q2[Substrate Oxidation Releases Electrons]
    Q2 --> Q3[NADH and FADH2 Carriers]
    Q3 --> Q4[Electron Transport Chain]
    Q4 --> Q5[Cytochrome c and Quinone Pool]
    Q5 --> Q6[Outer Membrane Reductase]
    Q6 --> Q7[Metal Ion Fe3plus or Mn4plus]
    Q7 --> Q8[Reduced Metal Fe2plus or Mn2plus]
    Q8 --> Q9[Precipitation or Mobility Change]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Electrons flow from the organic substrate (Q1) through the cell's electron transport chain to the **outer membrane reductase** (Q6), which donates them to the **terminal metal electron acceptor** (Q7). The reduced metal then undergoes a fate change.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer conceptual questions typical of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module-level Continuous Assessment and End-Semester Part A** sections. Each must be answered in **60–90 words** for full credit.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define biotransformation of metals. With one example, explain how microbial redox reactions can detoxify a heavy metal contaminant.**

**Model Answer:**
Biotransformation of metals is the **microbially-mediated change in the oxidation state, speciation, or chemical form** of a metal, altering its mobility and toxicity. **Example:** Hexavalent chromium (Cr⁶⁺) is a known carcinogen, soluble and mobile. Microbes such as *Pseudomonas* and *Bacillus* reduce it to **trivalent chromium (Cr³⁺)** using enzymes like chromate reductase. Cr³⁺ is approximately **100 times less toxic** and precipitates as Cr(OH)₃ under neutral pH, becoming immobile in soil. Thus, redox biotransformation converts a dangerous soluble metal into a safer insoluble one.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is biostimulation? List any two amendments commonly used in biostimulation of hydrocarbon-contaminated soil.**

**Model Answer:**
Biostimulation is a **bioremediation strategy** in which the **indigenous microbial community** is stimulated to degrade pollutants by **amending the environment with limiting nutrients or electron acceptors**. Two common amendments are:
1. **Nitrogen sources** — such as NH₄NO₃ or urea, to correct the C:N imbalance caused by a hydrocarbon spill.
2. **Electron acceptors** — such as O₂ (via aeration), H₂O₂, or NO₃⁻, to support aerobic or anaerobic respiration in the degrading microbes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination**, Module questions carry **14 marks** with a **choice between two alternatives**. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The model answers below exceed the minimum word count to ensure complete coverage of the valuation key.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the **major mechanisms of microbial metal biotransformation** in the environment. (7 marks)
**(b)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **stepwise implementation of a biostimulation program** for a petroleum-hydrocarbon contaminated site. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer — (a) Mechanisms of Microbial Metal Biotransformation (7 Marks)

Microbial metal biotransformation occurs through the following key mechanisms:

**1. Redox Transformation:**
Microorganisms catalyze the **reduction or oxidation** of metal ions using specialized enzymes. Examples include:
- Cr⁶⁺ → Cr³⁺ reduction (detoxification)
- U⁶⁺ → U⁴⁺ reduction (immobilization as UO₂)
- Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ reduction (anaerobic respiration)
- As⁵⁺ → As³⁺ oxidation (changes mobility and methylation potential)

**2. Methylation:**
Certain microbes (e.g., sulfate-reducing bacteria, methanogens) transfer methyl groups to metals like **mercury (Hg²⁺ → CH₃Hg⁺)** and **arsenic (As³⁺ → (CH₃)₃As)**. Note that methylation of Hg **increases** its toxicity and volatility — a case where biotransformation is *detrimental*.

**3. Bio-precipitation:**
Sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB) reduce SO₄²⁻ → S²⁻, which reacts with divalent metals to form **insoluble metal sulfides (MeS)**. This is exploited in **acid mine drainage treatment** to remove dissolved Fe, Cu, Zn, and Ni.

**4. Complexation and Biosorption:**
Microbial cell walls (rich in carboxyl, phosphate, and amine groups) and **extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)** bind metal ions. **Siderophores** (low-molecular-weight chelators) secreted by microbes solubilize Fe³⁺ for uptake, but also complex other metals.

**5. Bioaccumulation:**
Some microbes (e.g., *Citrobacter*, *Streptomyces*) accumulate metals **intracellularly** as phosphates, carbonates, or via metallothionein-like proteins, enabling removal when biomass is harvested.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only 2–3 mechanisms and skip **bio-precipitation** or **methylation**. Examiners award partial credit for each mechanism correctly explained; missing 2 of 5 costs roughly **3 marks**.

#### Model Answer — (b) Biostimulation Implementation Flow for a Petroleum Site (7 Marks)

The stepwise engineering implementation is as follows (see **Section 4.2** for the corresponding Mermaid diagram):

**Step 1 — Site Characterization:** Soil sampling and analysis to determine the **type, concentration, and extent** of hydrocarbon contamination, typically reported as Total Petroleum Hydrocarbons (TPH) in mg/kg.

**Step 2 — Microbial Profiling:** Enumeration of the **indigenous hydrocarbon-degrading population** (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Rhodococcus*, *Mycobacterium*) using Most Probable Number (MPN) or plate counts on selective media.

**Step 3 — Limiting Factor Diagnosis:** Soil is tested for **moisture, pH, nutrients (N, P), and electron acceptors (O₂, NO₃⁻)**. The factor farthest below the optimum is identified as the bottleneck.

**Step 4 — Amendment Application:** The chosen amendment (NH₄NO₃, K₂HPO₄, H₂O₂, surfactants, or lime) is applied. The **C:N:P ratio is adjusted to ~100:10:1** for optimal degradation.

**Step 5 — Mixing and Aeration:** Tilling or injection wells introduce O₂ and ensure **homogeneous contact** between microbes, contaminants, and amendments.

**Step 6 — Monitoring:** Periodic sampling of TPH, CO₂ evolution (respiration indicator), and microbial counts (e.g., OD₆₀₀) to track progress.

**Step 7 — Endpoint Verification:** The site is declared remediated when TPH falls below the regulatory threshold (e.g., **5000 mg/kg** for non-residential land use in many jurisdictions).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
**(a)** Compare **biostimulation and bioaugmentation** as bioremediation strategies. Mention one advantage and one limitation of each. (7 marks)
**(b)** A 1000 kg batch of soil is contaminated with 5 % (w/w) diesel. The native soil nitrogen content is 0.1 %, and the target C:N ratio for diesel biodegradation is 100:10 (mass basis). Calculate the mass of **ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)** fertilizer required. (7 marks)

#### Model Answer — (a) Biostimulation vs. Bioaugmentation (7 Marks)

| Parameter | **Biostimulation** | **Bioaugmentation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Strategy** | *Stimulating* existing microbes | *Adding* new microbial strains |
| **Amendments** | Nutrients, O₂, electron acceptors | Exogenous bacterial/ fungal cultures |
| **Microbial Source** | Indigenous (native) community | Exogenous (foreign) inocula |
| **Cost** | Lower (amendments only) | Higher (culture production, viability) |
| **Advantage** | Eco-friendly; preserves native ecology; no regulatory GMO issues | Effective for sites lacking competent degraders; faster results |
| **Limitation** | Ineffective if native population is absent or non-functional | Introduced strains may be outcompeted; may require genetic modification approval |
| **KTU Example** | NH₄NO₃ addition to oil-spilled soil | Inoculation of PAH-degrading *Mycobacterium* into creosote-contaminated soil |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing a "vague" answer such as *"biostimulation is natural and bioaugmentation is artificial"* is insufficient. Examiners want **specific amendments, microbe types, and contexts** to award full marks.

#### Model Answer — (b) Fertilizer Dose Calculation (7 Marks)

This is a direct application of **Derivation 1** in Section 3.1. The full step-by-step solution is reproduced here:

**Step 1 — Total carbon in contaminated soil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{C} &= 0.05 \times 1000 \text{ kg} = 50 \text{ kg of carbon}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Required total nitrogen for C:N = 100:10:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{required} &= 50 \times \dfrac{10}{100} = 5.0 \text{ kg of N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Native soil nitrogen:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{native} &= 0.001 \times 1000 \text{ kg} = 1.0 \text{ kg of N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Nitrogen deficit to be added:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{deficit} &= 5.0 - 1.0 = 4.0 \text{ kg of N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of NH₄NO₃ (N mass fraction = 0.35):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{NH_4NO_3} &= \dfrac{4.0}{0.35} \approx 11.43 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **Approximately 11.43 kg of NH₄NO₃** must be added to the 1000 kg of diesel-contaminated soil to achieve the target C:N ratio of 100:10 and stimulate indigenous diesel-degrading bacteria.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to **forget to subtract the native soil N**, leading to an inflated fertilizer dose (~14.3 kg). Examiners specifically allocate **1 mark** for this correction step. Always account for the *background* nutrient pool.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> This high-density checklist is your **last-minute revision sheet** for Module 4. Cover these bullets the night before the KTU End-Semester Exam.

- **Biotransformation of metals ≠ destruction.** Metals are *transformed* in form and oxidation state, not mineralized. Memorize the contrast with organic pollutant biodegradation.
- **Five core mechanisms:** (1) Redox, (2) Methylation, (3) Bio-precipitation, (4) Complexation/Biosorption, (5) Bioaccumulation.
- **Redox ladder sequence (high → low E₀′):** O₂ → NO₃⁻ → Mn⁴⁺ → Fe³⁺ → SO₄²⁻ → CO₂.
- **Biostimulation** = *feeding* native microbes; **Bioaugmentation** = *hiring* new microbes. This is the **#1** examiner trap.
- **Target C:N:P ratio for hydrocarbon biodegradation:** 100:10:1 (mass basis).
- **NH₄NO₃ nitrogen mass fraction:** 28/80 = **0.35 (35 %)**. Used in all fertilizer dose calculations.
- **Monod equation** is the *kinetic backbone* of biostimulation models; remember its low-S (first-order) and high-S (zero-order) limits.
- **Nernst equation** must use **T in Kelvin**, never Celsius. Standard temperature = **298.15 K (25 °C)**.
- **Optical Density at 600 nm (OD₆₀₀)** is the standard growth metric in bioremediation microbiology labs.
- **Sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB)** are the *workhorses* of metal bio-precipitation in anaerobic systems.
- **Biostimulation amendments are site-specific:** hydrocarbons need N/P; heavy metals need electron donors for SRB; pH extremes need lime or compost.
- **Half-life formula for first-order decay:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ — biostimulation aims to **maximize $k$**.
- **Regulatory cleanup targets** vary by land use (e.g., **5000 mg/kg TPH** for industrial soil, **100 mg/kg** for residential).
- **Advantages of biostimulation over bioaugmentation:** lower cost, no GMO concerns, preserves native ecology.
- **Limitations of biostimulation:** ineffective if native degraders are absent, contaminated with toxic co-pollutants, or if site hydrogeology prevents amendment delivery.
- **Real-world example to cite in answers:** *Exxon Valdez* spill (1989) — biostimulation with nitrogen fertilizer (Inipol EAP-22) accelerated shoreline hydrocarbon removal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
